Here is an example of a valid [YAML](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/YAML)
 file:

    - independent: I am quite happy
      dependent: happy
    - independent: I am despondent
      dependent: unhappy
    - independent: I am over the moon
      dependent: happy
    - independent: My life is meaningless
      dependent: unhappy

### Adding training data with code

You can also use code within your interview to add training data to a training set when you know both the “independent” and the “dependent” variable. You can do so with the [`.add_to_training_set()`](https://docassemble.org/docs/ml.html#SimpleTextMachineLearner.add_to_training_set)
 method of the `SimpleTextMachineLearner` object.

    ml.add_to_training_set("I am feeling blue", "unhappy")

The first parameter is the independent variable, and the second parameter is the dependent variable.

The [`.add_to_training_set()`](https://docassemble.org/docs/ml.html#SimpleTextMachineLearner.add_to_training_set)
 method is explained in more detail in the next section, which explains all of the methods available.

The `SimpleTextMachineLearner` object[¶](https://docassemble.org/docs/ml.html#DAModel)

=======================================================================================

The `SimpleTextMachineLearner` is an object for interacting with **docassemble**’s machine learning features. It uses the k-nearest neighbor (kNN) algorithm from the [`pattern.vector`](https://github.com/clips/pattern/wiki/pattern-vector)
 package.

    ml = SimpleTextMachineLearner('abc')

The first parameter sets the attribute `group_id`, which is the name of the data set to be used for training and/or prediction. Assuming that the above code exists in the interview `alphabet.yml` in the `docassemble.demo` package, the `group_id` will be set to `docassemble.demo:data/sources/ml-alphabet.json:abc`. See the [`machine learning storage`](https://docassemble.org/docs/initial.html#machine%20learning%20storage)
 specifier for information about modifying this value.

It is also possible to set the `group_id` to an explicit value. You can do:

    ml = SimpleTextMachineLearner(group_id='abcdefg')

or:

    ml = SimpleTextMachineLearner()
    ml.group_id = 'abcdefg'

Another parameter of the [`SimpleTextMachineLearner`](https://docassemble.org/docs/ml.html#SimpleTextMachineLearner)
 is `initial_file`, which is a reference to a file (assumed to be in the [sources folder](https://docassemble.org/docs/playground.html#sources)
 unless otherwise specified) that contains initial training data. If you want to use the conventional initial file, you can do:

    ml = SimpleTextMachineLearner('abc', use_initial_file=True)

This will set the `initial_file` attribute to, for example, `docassemble.demo:data/sources/ml-alphabet.json`.

You can also set `initial_file` to a specific value by passing an optional second parameter:

    ml = SimpleTextMachineLearner('abc', 'initial-training-set.json')

Or, you can give it as an optional keyword parameter:

    ml = SimpleTextMachineLearner('abc', initial_file='initial-training-set.json')

Or, you can set it with code:

    ml = SimpleTextMachineLearner('abc')
    ml.initial_file = 'initial-training-set.json'

A `SimpleTextMachineLearner` needs a `group_id` in order to operate, but the `initial_file` is optional.

The data set is stored in the SQL database and persists beyond the life of the interview. Any interview can use a data set by referencing its `group_id`.

The `initial_file` will only be used to populate the data set if the data set is completely empty. If you want to update a data set that already has entries, you can use other methods, such as [`.add_to_training_set()`](https://docassemble.org/docs/ml.html#SimpleTextMachineLearner.add_to_training_set)
.

A workflow for developing and distributing an interview that uses machine learning is as follows:

*   Create an interview file in the [Playground](https://docassemble.org/docs/playground.html)
     called `triage.yml`.
*   Initialize the model with `ml = SimpleTextMachineLearner('legal_issue', use_initial_file=True)`.
*   Develop a training set. You can do this using the [`.save_for_classification()`](https://docassemble.org/docs/ml.html#SimpleTextMachineLearner.save_for_classification)
     method in your interview.
*   Train your data set. You can do this by going to [Train](https://docassemble.org/docs/ml.html#train)
     from the main menu. Or, you could use [`.one_unclassified_entry()`](https://docassemble.org/docs/ml.html#SimpleTextMachineLearner.one_classified_entry)
     in combination with [`.classify()`](https://docassemble.org/docs/ml.html#MachineLearningEntry.classify)
     and [`.save()`](https://docassemble.org/docs/ml.html#MachineLearningEntry.classify)
     to train from within an interview. Or, you could use [`.add_to_training_set()`](https://docassemble.org/docs/ml.html#SimpleTextMachineLearner.add_to_training_set)
     in your interview to store complete observations.
*   Package your interview in the [Playground](https://docassemble.org/docs/playground.html)
    . Under “Sources,” include the file `ml-triage.json`.
*   Now, if someone installs your package on their server, then your training data will initialize the model.

.add\_to\_training\_set()[¶](https://docassemble.org/docs/ml.html#SimpleTextMachineLearner.add_to_training_set)

----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

If you know the independent and dependent variables for an observation, the [`.add_to_training_set()`](https://docassemble.org/docs/ml.html#SimpleTextMachineLearner.add_to_training_set)
 method will create an entry in the data for you. It takes two parameters: the independent variable and the dependent variable.

It also takes an optional keyword parameter `key`, which if set will store the entry using the given `key`. You do not need to use `key`s with your data, but you may find it helpful to add `key`s to help keep your data organized.

    ml.add_to_training_set("I hate my life", "unhappy")
    ml.add_to_training_set("i luv my cat!!!!! xoxo", "happy", key='juvenile')

.classified\_entries()[¶](https://docassemble.org/docs/ml.html#SimpleTextMachineLearner.classified_entries)

------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

The [`.classified_entries()`](https://docassemble.org/docs/ml.html#SimpleTextMachineLearner.classified_entries)
 method returns a [`DAList`](https://docassemble.org/docs/objects.html#DAList)
 of objects of type [`MachineLearningEntry`](https://docassemble.org/docs/ml.html#MachineLearningEntry)
, which represent entries in the database. See also [`.unclassified_entries()`](https://docassemble.org/docs/ml.html#SimpleTextMachineLearner.unclassified_entries)
.

    question: Prediction
    subquestion: |
      This prediction was based on the following data:
    
      % for record in ml.classified_entries():
      * "${ record.independent }" means "${ record.dependent }."
      % endfor

.confusion\_matrix()[¶](https://docassemble.org/docs/ml.html#SimpleTextMachineLearner.confusion_matrix)

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

The [`.confusion_matrix()`](https://docassemble.org/docs/ml.html#SimpleTextMachineLearner.confusion_matrix)
 method returns a [confusion matrix](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Confusion_matrix)
 that can be used to evaluate the performance of the machine learner on the data set.

Each entry in the data set is randomly assigned to either a training set or a testing set. The model is trained on the training set and then tested with the testing set. The “Actual” values are along the top. The “Predicted” values are along the left side.

If the dependent values are `'Unhappy'` and `'Happy'`, the [Python dictionary](https://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#dict)
 returned from `ml.confusion_matrix()` will be something like this:

    {'Unhappy': {'Unhappy': 8, 'Happy': 3}, 'Happy': {'Unhappy': 2, 'Happy': 6}}

The outer keys refer to “actual” values and the inner keys refer to “predicted” values.

The output of `ml.confusion_matrix(output_format='html')` will look like:

|     |     | Actual |     |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
|     |     | Unhappy | Happy |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Predicted | Unhappy | 8   | 2   |
| Happy | 3   | 6   |

Other valid `output_format` values are `json` and `yaml` for [JSON](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/JSON)
 and [YAML](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/YAML)
 output, respectively.

You can limit the universe of values used for testing and training by setting the optional keyword parameter `key`. Then, only entries created with that `key` will be used as part of the training and testing.

Note that since the data set is split into a training set and a testing set in a random fashion, there is no guarantee that the size of the training set and the testing set will be the same. The split is random each time [`.confusion_matrix()`](https://docassemble.org/docs/ml.html#SimpleTextMachineLearner.confusion_matrix)
 is called, so the exact results will not be reproducible. It is a good idea to look at several [confusion matrices](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Confusion_matrix)
 when assessing the functionality of a machine learning model.

If your data set has more than a few dependent variables, you might prefer to test the true/false question of whether the model predicts a particular value, or not a particular value. If you set the optional keyword parameter `split` to `True`, [`.confusion_matrix()`](https://docassemble.org/docs/ml.html#SimpleTextMachineLearner.confusion_matrix)
 will return a [Python dictionary](https://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#dict)
 of responses, where each key is a dependent variable in the data set and each value is a [Python dictionary](https://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#dict)
 representing the [confusion matrix](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Confusion_matrix)
. If `output_format` is [HTML](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/HTML)
, all of the [confusion matrices](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Confusion_matrix)
 will be listed, with headers indicating the dependent variable being tested.

.delete\_by\_id()[¶](https://docassemble.org/docs/ml.html#SimpleTextMachineLearner.delete_by_id)

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

The [`.delete_by_id()`](https://docassemble.org/docs/ml.html#SimpleTextMachineLearner.delete_by_id)
 method will delete a single entry from the data set, indicated by the ID.

    for entry in ml.unclassified_entries():
      if entry.independent == 'ignore':
        ml.delete_by_id(entry.id)

.delete\_by\_key()[¶](https://docassemble.org/docs/ml.html#SimpleTextMachineLearner.delete_by_key)

---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

The [`.delete_by_key()`](https://docassemble.org/docs/ml.html#SimpleTextMachineLearner.delete_by_key)
 method will delete entries in the data set that use the given key.

    ml.delete_by_key('user')

.delete\_training\_set()[¶](https://docassemble.org/docs/ml.html#SimpleTextMachineLearner.delete_training_set)

---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

The [`.delete_training_set()`](https://docassemble.org/docs/ml.html#SimpleTextMachineLearner.delete_training_set)
 method will delete all of the entries in the data set.

    ml.delete_training_set()

.dependent\_in\_use()[¶](https://docassemble.org/docs/ml.html#SimpleTextMachineLearner.dependent_in_use)

---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

The [`.dependent_in_use()`](https://docassemble.org/docs/ml.html#SimpleTextMachineLearner.dependent_in_use)
 method returns a sorted list of unique values of the dependent variable for all classified entries.

    question: |
      What is the meaning of ${ phrase }?
    fields:
      - no label: meaning
        datatype: radio
        code: |
          ml.dependent_in_use()

.export\_training\_set()[¶](https://docassemble.org/docs/ml.html#SimpleTextMachineLearner.export_training_set)

---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

If you have accumulated a data set within **docassemble** and you want to extract it, you can export it. The [`.export_training_set()`](https://docassemble.org/docs/ml.html#SimpleTextMachineLearner.export_training_set)
 method returns [YAML](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/YAML)
 or [JSON](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/JSON)
 text containing the values of the data set.

The [`.export_training_set()`](https://docassemble.org/docs/ml.html#SimpleTextMachineLearner.export_training_set)
 method takes an optional keyword parameter `output_format`, which defaults to `'json'`. The available output formats are `'json'` and `'yaml'`. The [`.export_training_set()`](https://docassemble.org/docs/ml.html#SimpleTextMachineLearner.export_training_set)
 method returns a string containing the data in the `output_format` format.

All entries in the data set will be returned, but you can limit the results to entries tagged with a particular key by setting the optional keyword parameter `key`.

The following code creates a [`DAFile`](https://docassemble.org/docs/objects.html#DAFile)
 and writes the data set to that file in [YAML](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/YAML)
 format. The `yaml_export` variable contains the data as text, and the `export` variable refers to the file to which this text is written.

    yaml_export = ml.export_training_set(output_format='yaml')
    export = DAFile()
    export.write(yaml_export)
    export.commit()

The following interview exports the data in the `'demo'` data set to a file called `my_training_data.json` in the [sources folder](https://docassemble.org/docs/playground.html#sources)
 of the user’s [Playground](https://docassemble.org/docs/playground.html)
. (The user needs to be a developer or administrator for this to work.)

    ---
    modules:
      - docassemble.webapp.playground
    ---
    initial: True
    code: |
      ml = SimpleTextMachineLearner('demo')
      the_data = ml.export_training_set(output_format='json')
      sources = PlaygroundSection('sources')
      sources.write_file('my_training_data.json', the_data)
    ---
    mandatory: True
    question: |
      The training data have been saved.
    buttons:
      - Save again: refresh

The following interview exports the data in the `'demo'` dataset directly to the browser in [JSON](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/JSON)
 format.

    initial: True
    code: |
      ml = SimpleTextMachineLearner('demo')
      the_data = ml.export_training_set(output_format='json')
      response(response=the_data, content_type='application/json')

[GitHub](https://github.com/jhpyle/docassemble/blob/master/docassemble_base/docassemble/base/data/questions/examples/ml-export.yml "Open on GitHub")

[![Screenshot of ml-export example](https://docassemble.org/img/examples/ml-export.png)](https://demo.docassemble.org/interview?i=docassemble.base:data/questions/examples/ml-export.yml&reset=1 "Click to try this interview")

The following interview exports the data in the `'demo'` data set directly to the browser in [YAML](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/YAML)
 format.

    initial: True
    code: |
      ml = SimpleTextMachineLearner(group_id='demo')
      the_data = ml.export_training_set(output_format='yaml')
      response(response=the_data, content_type='text/plain')

[GitHub](https://github.com/jhpyle/docassemble/blob/master/docassemble_base/docassemble/base/data/questions/examples/ml-export-yaml.yml "Open on GitHub")

[![Screenshot of ml-export-yaml example](https://docassemble.org/img/examples/ml-export-yaml.png)](https://demo.docassemble.org/interview?i=docassemble.base:data/questions/examples/ml-export-yaml.yml&reset=1 "Click to try this interview")

.is\_empty()[¶](https://docassemble.org/docs/ml.html#SimpleTextMachineLearner.is_empty)

----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

The [`.is_empty()`](https://docassemble.org/docs/ml.html#SimpleTextMachineLearner.is_empty)
 method indicates whether there are any entries in the training set. It returns `True` if there are no entries and otherwise returns `False`.

    code: |
      if ml.is_empty():
        no_data_screen
      else:
        prediction_screen

.new\_entry()[¶](https://docassemble.org/docs/ml.html#SimpleTextMachineLearner.new_entry)

------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

The [`.new_entry()`](https://docassemble.org/docs/ml.html#SimpleTextMachineLearner.new_entry)
 method returns a single [`MachineLearningEntry`](https://docassemble.org/docs/ml.html#MachineLearningEntry)
 object.

The following interview uses [`.new_entry()`](https://docassemble.org/docs/ml.html#SimpleTextMachineLearner.new_entry)
 to create a [`MachineLearningEntry`](https://docassemble.org/docs/ml.html#MachineLearningEntry)
 object. It calls [`.save()`](https://docassemble.org/docs/ml.html#MachineLearningEntry.classify)
 on the new entry. This triggers the asking of a question to get the `.independent` variable. The entry goes into the data set as an unclassified entry. A prediction is made and presented to the user. If the user presses “Try again,” the slate is cleaned and the process repeats.

    code: |
      entry = ml.new_entry()

[GitHub](https://github.com/jhpyle/docassemble/blob/master/docassemble_base/docassemble/base/data/questions/examples/save-and-predict.yml "Open on GitHub")

[![Screenshot of save-and-predict example](https://docassemble.org/img/examples/save-and-predict.png)](https://demo.docassemble.org/interview?i=docassemble.base:data/questions/examples/save-and-predict.yml&reset=1 "Click to try this interview")

.one\_unclassified\_entry()[¶](https://docassemble.org/docs/ml.html#SimpleTextMachineLearner.one_unclassified_entry)

---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

The [`.one_unclassified_entry()`](https://docassemble.org/docs/ml.html#SimpleTextMachineLearner.one_classified_entry)
 method is useful for interactively asking the user to classify unclassified entries. It returns a single [`MachineLearningEntry`](https://docassemble.org/docs/ml.html#MachineLearningEntry)
 object.

To classify the entry, use the [`.classify()`](https://docassemble.org/docs/ml.html#MachineLearningEntry.classify)
 method.

    code: |
      entry = ml.one_unclassified_entry()
    ---
    mandatory: true
    code: |
      while True:
        if entry is None:
          del entry
          no_entries
        else:
          entry.classify()
          del entry
    ---
    question: |
      How do you classify this?
    subquestion: |
      ${ quote_paragraphs(entry.independent) }
    fields:
      - no label: entry.dependent
        datatype: radio
        choices:
          - Happy
          - Unhappy
          - Angry
          - Sad

[GitHub](https://github.com/jhpyle/docassemble/blob/master/docassemble_base/docassemble/base/data/questions/examples/classify.yml "Open on GitHub")

[![Screenshot of classify example](https://docassemble.org/img/examples/classify.png)](https://demo.docassemble.org/interview?i=docassemble.base:data/questions/examples/classify.yml&reset=1 "Click to try this interview")

.predict()[¶](https://docassemble.org/docs/ml.html#DAModel.predict)

--------------------------------------------------------------------

The `.predict()` method of the `SimpleTextMachineLearner` class returns the predictions of the machine learner for the given independent variable.

    predictions = ml.predict("I want to have my cake and eat it too.")
    if len(predictions):
      sentiment = predictions[0]

If you call `.predict()` with the keyword parameter `probabilities=True`, the return value will be a list of tuples, where the first element of the tuple is the dependent variable and the second value is the probability, expressed as a number between zero and one.

    mandatory: True
    question: |
      The prediction
    subquestion: |
      The text provided was:
      > ${ phrase }
      
      % if len(predictions):
      The prediction was:
    
      % for prediction in predictions:
      * ${ '%s (%f)' % prediction }
      % endfor
      % else:
      There is not enough training data
      to make a prediction yet.
      % endif
    buttons:
      - Restart: restart

[GitHub](https://github.com/jhpyle/docassemble/blob/master/docassemble_base/docassemble/base/data/questions/examples/ml-predict-probabilities.yml "Open on GitHub")

[![Screenshot of ml-predict-probabilities example](https://docassemble.org/img/examples/ml-predict-probabilities.png)](https://demo.docassemble.org/interview?i=docassemble.base:data/questions/examples/ml-predict-probabilities.yml&reset=1 "Click to try this interview")

.reset()[¶](https://docassemble.org/docs/ml.html#SimpleTextMachineLearner.reset)

---------------------------------------------------------------------------------

For efficiency, **docassemble** keeps a cache of machine learning objects in memory.

The [`.reset()`](https://docassemble.org/docs/ml.html#SimpleTextMachineLearner.reset)
 method clears the cache and causes the machine learner to re-read training data from the database.

.retrieve\_by\_id()[¶](https://docassemble.org/docs/ml.html#SimpleTextMachineLearner.retrieve_by_id)

-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

The [`.retrieve_by_id()`](https://docassemble.org/docs/ml.html#SimpleTextMachineLearner.retrieve_by_id)
 method returns a [`MachineLearningEntry`](https://docassemble.org/docs/ml.html#MachineLearningEntry)
 for a given ID.

    entry = ml.retrieve_by_id(the_id)

.save\_for\_classification()[¶](https://docassemble.org/docs/ml.html#SimpleTextMachineLearner.save_for_classification)

-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

The [`.save_for_classification()`](https://docassemble.org/docs/ml.html#SimpleTextMachineLearner.save_for_classification)
 method creates an unclassified entry in the data set for the given independent variable and returns the ID of the database entry.

    ml.save_for_classification("I'm so tired.  I haven't slept a wink.")

If an unclassified entry already exists in the data set for the given independent variable, a new entry will not be created, and the ID of the first existing entry will be returned.

.set\_dependent\_by\_id()[¶](https://docassemble.org/docs/ml.html#SimpleTextMachineLearner.set_dependent_by_id)

----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

The [`.set_dependent_by_id()`](https://docassemble.org/docs/ml.html#SimpleTextMachineLearner.set_dependent_by_id)
 method classifies an existing entry based on its ID. The first parameter is the ID of the entry. The second parameter is the dependent variable.

    ml.set_dependent_by_id(the_id, "unhappy")

.unclassified\_entries()[¶](https://docassemble.org/docs/ml.html#SimpleTextMachineLearner.unclassified_entries)

----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

The [`.unclassified_entries()`](https://docassemble.org/docs/ml.html#SimpleTextMachineLearner.unclassified_entries)
 method is like the [`.classified_entries()`](https://docassemble.org/docs/ml.html#SimpleTextMachineLearner.classified_entries)
, except that it returns only entries that have not yet been classified.

    question: |
      Unclassified entries
    subquestion: |
      % for entry in ml.unclassified_entries():
      * "${ entry.independent }" has not yet been classified.
      % endfor

The `SVMMachineLearner` object[¶](https://docassemble.org/docs/ml.html#SVMMachineLearner)

==========================================================================================

The `SVMMachineLearner` works just like `SimpleTextMachineLearner` except that is uses the Support Vector Machines algorithm from the [`pattern.vector`](https://github.com/clips/pattern/wiki/pattern-vector)
 package.

The `RandomForestMachineLearner` object[¶](https://docassemble.org/docs/ml.html#RandomForestMachineLearner)

============================================================================================================

The `RandomForestMachineLearner` object works just like [`SimpleTextMachineLearner`](https://docassemble.org/docs/ml.html#SimpleTextMachineLearner)
, except that its independent variable is a [Python dictionary](https://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#dict)
 rather than a piece of text. Instead of processing natural language, it uses the [random forest](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Random_forest)
 algorithm from the [sklearn](https://scikit-learn.org/stable/)
 package to build a predictive model.

Here is a simple example that shows how a model can be constructed from raw data expressed as [Python dictionaries](https://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#dict)
.

    mandatory: True
    code: |
      rf = RandomForestMachineLearner('fruit', use_initial_file=True)
      if rf.is_empty():
        rf.add_to_training_set({'a': 1, 'b': 2.0, 'c': 3.0, 'd': 'A'}, 'apple')
        rf.add_to_training_set({'a': 0.9, 'b': 2, 'c': 3.0, 'd': 'A'}, 'apple')
        rf.add_to_training_set({'a': 0.8, 'b': 2.0, 'c': 3.0, 'd': 'A'}, 'apple')
        rf.add_to_training_set({'a': 1.0, 'b': 2.1, 'c': 3, 'd': 'B'}, 'apple')
        rf.add_to_training_set({'a': 1.2, 'b': 2.0, 'c': 3.1, 'd': 'A'}, 'apple')
        rf.add_to_training_set({'a': 1.0, 'b': 7.0, 'c': 3.0, 'd': 'B'}, 'orange')
        rf.add_to_training_set({'a': 0.9, 'b': 6.9, 'c': 3.0, 'd': 'A'}, 'orange')
        rf.add_to_training_set({'a': 0.8, 'b': 6.7, 'c': 2.9, 'd': 'B'}, 'orange')
        rf.add_to_training_set({'a': 1.0, 'b': 7.1, 'c': 2.95, 'd': 'B'}, 'orange')
        rf.add_to_training_set({'a': 1.05, 'b': 6.8, 'c': 3.1, 'd': 'B'}, 'orange')
    ---
    code: |
      sample_data = {'a': 1, 'b': 3.8, 'c': 3.1, 'd': 'B'}
      predictions = rf.predict(sample_data, probabilities=True)

[GitHub](https://github.com/jhpyle/docassemble/blob/master/docassemble_demo/docassemble/demo/data/questions/examples/random-forest.yml "Open on GitHub")

[![Screenshot of random-forest example](https://docassemble.org/img/examples/random-forest.png)](https://demo.docassemble.org/interview?i=docassemble.demo:data/questions/examples/random-forest.yml&reset=1 "Click to try this interview")

Some things to note about this interview:

*   It is important to use the [`.is_empty()`](https://docassemble.org/docs/ml.html#SimpleTextMachineLearner.is_empty)
     method to see if the data set is empty before using [`.add_to_training_set()`](https://docassemble.org/docs/ml.html#SimpleTextMachineLearner.add_to_training_set)
     to populate the data set. Otherwise, this interview would add duplicative data points to the data set every time it ran.
*   `predictions[0]` is the most likely prediction. If it exists, `predictions[1]` is the second most likely prediction, and so on.
*   Because [`.predict()`](https://docassemble.org/docs/ml.html#SimpleTextMachineLearner.predict)
     was called with `probabilities=True`, each item in `predictions` is a [tuple](https://docs.python.org/3/tutorial/datastructures.html#tuples-and-sequences)
    , the first element of which is the predicted value (`'apple'` or `'orange'`), and the second element of which is a probability, a number between 0 and 1. Thus, the predicted dependent variables is `predictions[0][0]` and its probability is `predictions[0][1]`.
*   The [`.format()` method](https://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#str.format)
    , which is used to format the probability, is a standard [Python](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Python_%28programming_language%29)
     method. The code `{0:.1f}%` means “format the first number as a [floating point](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Floating-point_arithmetic)
     number using one decimal place, and put a percent sign after it.”

You can also train the model and make predictions using data gathered during interviews.

Here is an example of an interview that uses a [random forest](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Random_forest)
 model to try to identify fruit based on a set of characteristics, many of which are vague and subjective. The independent variable is a [`DADict`](https://docassemble.org/docs/objects.html#DADict)
 called `characteristics`. The dependent variables is the name of a fruit (e.g., `'apple'`).

    objects:
      - characteristics: DADict.using(gathered=True)
    ---
    mandatory: True
    question: |
      Think of a fruit.
    subquestion: |
      When you have thought of a fruit,
      press Continue.
    field: thought_of_fruit
    ---
    mandatory: True
    question: |
      Is it round?
    yesno: characteristics['round']
    ---
    mandatory: True
    question: |
      What color is it when ripe?
    field: characteristics['color']
    choices:
      - Red
      - Yellow
      - Purple
      - Blue
      - Green
      - Orange
    ---
    mandatory: True
    question: |
      Where are its seeds?
    field: characteristics['seed location']
    choices:
      - Inside
      - Outside
      - Throughout
    ---
    mandatory: True
    question: |
      How many inches wide is a
      typical specimen?
    fields:
      - no label: characteristics['width']
        datatype: range
        min: 0.1
        max: 12.0
        step: 0.1
        default: 6.0
    ---
    mandatory: True
    question: |
      How sweet is it, on a scale from
      1 to 5?
    fields:
      - no label: characteristics['sweetness']
        datatype: range
        min: 1
        max: 5
        default: 3
    ---
    code: |
      rf = RandomForestMachineLearner('fruit', use_initial_file=True)
    ---
    code: |
      predictions = rf.predict(characteristics, probabilities=True)
    ---
    mandatory: True
    code: |
      if len(predictions) > 0 and prediction_correct:
        rf.add_to_training_set(characteristics, predictions[0][0])
      else:
        fruit_name = fruit_name.lower()
        fruit_name = noun_singular(fruit_name)
        rf.add_to_training_set(characteristics, fruit_name)
    ---
    mandatory: True
    question: |
      Thank you!
    buttons:
      Try another one: restart
    ---
    question: |
      With
      ${ '{0:.1f}%'.format(predictions[0][1]*100) }
      certainty, I think it is
      ${ indefinite_article(predictions[0][0]) }.
      Am I right?
    yesno: prediction_correct
    ---
    question: |
      What fruit were you thinking of?
    fields:
      - Fruit: fruit_name

[GitHub](https://github.com/jhpyle/docassemble/blob/master/docassemble_demo/docassemble/demo/data/questions/examples/random-forest-interview.yml "Open on GitHub")

[![Screenshot of random-forest-interview example](https://docassemble.org/img/examples/random-forest-interview.png)](https://demo.docassemble.org/interview?i=docassemble.demo:data/questions/examples/random-forest-interview.yml&reset=1 "Click to try this interview")

Some things to note about this interview:

*   The previous interview used a plain [Python dictionary](https://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#dict)
     as the independent variable; this interview uses a [`DADict`](https://docassemble.org/docs/objects.html#DADict)
    . Both will work. Note that the [`.predict()`](https://docassemble.org/docs/ml.html#SimpleTextMachineLearner.predict)
     and [`.add_to_training_set()`](https://docassemble.org/docs/ml.html#SimpleTextMachineLearner.add_to_training_set)
     methods do not trigger a [gathering](https://docassemble.org/docs/groups.html#gathering)
     process on the [`DADict`](https://docassemble.org/docs/objects.html#DADict)
    .
*   The interview uses a lot of [`mandatory`](https://docassemble.org/docs/logic.html#mandatory)
     questions to make sure that each item in `characteristics` gets defined before it is used in the machine learning methods. Note that there are other ways to accomplish this without [`mandatory`](https://docassemble.org/docs/logic.html#mandatory)
     blocks. One way would be to refer to the expression `[characteristics[c] for c in ('round', 'color', 'seed location', 'width', 'sweetness')]`.
*   This is an example of a self-learning interview. It asks the user if the prediction is correct, and if the prediction is correct, the user’s answers are added to the data set to make it even more robust. If the prediction is not correct, the user is asked the name of the actual fruit, and this is added to the data set.
*   In order to avoid adding duplicative dependent variables to the data set, when the interview asks the user for the name of the fruit, the interview transforms the name into lowercase using the [built-in method `.lower()`](https://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#str.lower)
    , and then uses the function [`noun_singular()`](https://docassemble.org/docs/functions.html#noun_singular)
     to ensure that the name is in singular form. Otherwise, the data set would be littered with dependent variables like “Apples,” “apples,” “apple,” and “Apple,” as though they referred to different things.

The `MachineLearningEntry` object[¶](https://docassemble.org/docs/ml.html#MachineLearningEntry)

================================================================================================

The attributes of a `MachineLearningEntry` object are:

*   `ml`: The `SimpleTextMachineLearner` object associated with the entry.
*   `id`: The integer ID of the entry in the database.
*   `independent`: The independent variable.
*   `dependent`: The dependent variable.
*   `create_time`: The [`datetime`](https://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html#datetime-objects)
     when the entry was created.
*   `key`: A key associated with the entry, or `None` if no key was assigned.

If the entry is unclassified, the `dependent` attribute will be undefined.

The values of an entry can be changed, but it does not become part of the data set until it is saved, either by calling [`.classify()`](https://docassemble.org/docs/ml.html#MachineLearningEntry.classify)
 or [`.save()`](https://docassemble.org/docs/ml.html#MachineLearningEntry.classify)
.

.classify()[¶](https://docassemble.org/docs/ml.html#MachineLearningEntry.classify)

-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------

The [`.classify()`](https://docassemble.org/docs/ml.html#MachineLearningEntry.classify)
 method “classifies” the observation by saving it to the data set with the dependent variable set to the value of the `dependent` attribute.

    code: |
      entry = ml.one_unclassified_entry()
    ---
    mandatory: true
    code: |
      while True:
        if entry is None:
          del entry
          no_entries
        else:
          entry.classify()
          del entry
    ---
    question: |
      How do you classify this?
    subquestion: |
      ${ quote_paragraphs(entry.independent) }
    fields:
      - no label: entry.dependent
        datatype: radio
        choices:
          - Happy
          - Unhappy
          - Angry
          - Sad

[GitHub](https://github.com/jhpyle/docassemble/blob/master/docassemble_base/docassemble/base/data/questions/examples/classify.yml "Open on GitHub")

[![Screenshot of classify example](https://docassemble.org/img/examples/classify.png)](https://demo.docassemble.org/interview?i=docassemble.base:data/questions/examples/classify.yml&reset=1 "Click to try this interview")

If called with a parameter, the `dependent` attribute will be set before the entry is saved.

In other words, this:

    entry.classify("Unhappy")

is equivalent to:

    entry.dependent = "Unhappy"
    entry.classify()

.predict()[¶](https://docassemble.org/docs/ml.html#MachineLearningEntry.predict)

---------------------------------------------------------------------------------

The `.predict()` method of the `MachineLearningEntry` class returns the predictions of the machine learner given the entry’s independent variable.

    predictions = entry.predict()
    if len(predictions):
      best_guess = predictions[0]

The result of calling `.predict()` is a [list](https://docs.python.org/3/tutorial/datastructures.html)
 of dependent variables from the training set arranged in order from most likely to least likely. If there is no training data, an empty [list](https://docs.python.org/3/tutorial/datastructures.html)
 will be returned.

If you call `.predict()` with the keyword parameter `probabilities=True`, the return value will be a list of [tuple](https://docs.python.org/3/tutorial/datastructures.html#tuples-and-sequences)
s, where the first element of the [tuple](https://docs.python.org/3/tutorial/datastructures.html#tuples-and-sequences)
 is the dependent variable and the second value is the probability, expressed as a number between zero and one.

    question: |
      The prediction
    subquestion: |
      <%
        predictions = entry.predict(probabilities=True)
      %>
      The text provided was:
      > ${ entry.independent }
      
      % if len(predictions):
      The prediction was:
    
      % for prediction in predictions:
      * ${ '%s (%f)' % prediction }
      % endfor
      % else:
      There is not enough training data
      to make a prediction yet.
      % endif

.save()[¶](https://docassemble.org/docs/ml.html#MachineLearningEntry.save)

---------------------------------------------------------------------------

The `.save()` method saves the entry to the database. If the independent variable has not yet been set (i.e., the `independent` attribute is not defined), **docassemble** will ask for it.

Creating your own machine learning functions
============================================

If you are a [Python](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Python_%28programming_language%29)
 developer, you may want to make other machine learning algorithms available in interviews.

The `SimpleTextMachineLearner` class is a subclass of the generic `MachineLearner` class. The `MachineLearner` class handles the overhead of storing independent and dependent variables in a database, while the `SimpleTextMachineLearner` class handles the specific implementation. The `SimpleTextMachineLearner` class uses the `KNN()` function from the [`pattern.vector`](https://github.com/clips/pattern/wiki/pattern-vector)
 package.

One way you can make machine learning algorithms available in interviews is by creating your own subclasses of `MachineLearner` that define the following methods:

*   `_initialize()`
*   `_train()`
*   `predict()`

See the code of [`docassemble.webapp.machinelearning`](https://github.com/jhpyle/docassemble/blob/master/docassemble_webapp/docassemble/webapp/machinelearning.py)
 for more information.

Slow-running code
=================

If your machine learning algorithms use very large training sets, or take a long time to process, you may wish to call `.predict()` from a [background action](https://docassemble.org/docs/background.html#background)
. (The loading of the training set takes place during the first call to `.predict()`, not during the creation of the object.)

* * *

---

# Customizing the user interface

[Edit](https://github.com/jhpyle/docassemble/blob/gh-pages/_docs/ui.md "Edit this page on GitHub")

Customizing the user interface
==============================

Contents
--------

*   [1 Bootstrap](https://docassemble.org/docs/ui.html#bootstrap)
    
*   [2 CSS customization](https://docassemble.org/docs/ui.html#css)
    
*   [3 Screen parts](https://docassemble.org/docs/ui.html#screen%20parts)
    
*   [4 Navigation bar](https://docassemble.org/docs/ui.html#navigation%20bar)
    
*   [5 Other UI elements](https://docassemble.org/docs/ui.html#favicon)
    

This section provides an overview of ways that you can customize the user interface that end users see.

Bootstrap[¶](https://docassemble.org/docs/ui.html#bootstrap)

=============================================================

You can give **docassemble** a new “skin” by creating your own [Bootstrap](https://getbootstrap.com/)
 theme. This can be configured globally for the server with the [`bootstrap theme` Configuration directive](https://docassemble.org/docs/config.html#bootstrap%20theme)
 or for a particular interview with the [`bootstrap theme`](https://docassemble.org/docs/initial.html#bootstrap%20theme)
 feature.

Bootstrap has a “night mode” that is activated automatically based on the browser or OS settings. You can prevent the browser from switching into “night mode” using the [`auto color scheme`](https://docassemble.org/docs/config.html#auto%20color%20scheme)
 Configuration directive.

CSS customization[¶](https://docassemble.org/docs/ui.html#css)

===============================================================

If you are happy with the Bootstrap theme but you want to make tweaks to CSS in particular places, you can customize the CSS by providing your own [`css` files](https://docassemble.org/docs/initial.html#css)
. You can store these files in the Static folder of the Playground, or the `data/static` folder of your docassemble add-on package. It is also possible to enable CSS files globally on the server using the [`global css`](https://docassemble.org/docs/config.html#global%20css)
 Configuration directive.

You can use the Inspect tool of your browser to look at the HTML elements you want to modify, and they will usually have CSS classes prefixed with `da`, or an `id`. You can write CSS rules that target these elements. You can add CSS classes to field elements using the [`css class`](https://docassemble.org/docs/fields.html#css%20class)
 field modifier. The [`action buttons`](https://docassemble.org/docs/questions.html#action%20buttons)
 interface allows for the specification of a CSS class. You can set the [`css class` modifier](https://docassemble.org/docs/questions.html#css%20class)
 on a `question` in order to attach a CSS class to the `<body>` element for that question.

The CSS class of HTML tables can be customized at the `question` level with the [`table css class` modifier](https://docassemble.org/docs/questions.html#table%20css%20class)
 or at the interview level with the `table css class` [screen part](https://docassemble.org/docs/questions.html#screen%20parts)
.

You can globally change the Bootstrap color that is used for the various types of buttons by setting the [`buttons colors`](https://docassemble.org/docs/config.html#buttons%20colors)
 Configuration directive. You can also modify the [`button style`](https://docassemble.org/docs/config.html#button%20style)
 and [`button size`](https://docassemble.org/docs/config.html#button%20size)
.

If you use the `footer` [screen part](https://docassemble.org/docs/questions.html#screen%20parts)
, you can change its CSS class using the [`footer css class`](https://docassemble.org/docs/config.html#footer%20css%20class)
 Configuration directive.

Screen parts[¶](https://docassemble.org/docs/ui.html#screen%20parts)

=====================================================================

Many UI elements can be customized using [screen parts](https://docassemble.org/docs/questions.html#screen%20parts)
. Screen parts refer to particular parts of the screen, such as the interview title in the navigation bar, the name of the tab in the web browser, or the optional page footer. You can use screen parts to insert text or HTML into various areas of the screen. The [screen parts](https://docassemble.org/docs/questions.html#screen%20parts)
 system can also be used to customize the text and color of standard buttons, like back buttons and help buttons.

Some of these screen parts exist on non-interview pages. These can be customized using Configuration directives for the [screen parts of administrative pages](https://docassemble.org/docs/config.html#customization)
.

Navigation bar[¶](https://docassemble.org/docs/ui.html#navigation%20bar)

=========================================================================

The main navigation bar at the top of the screen can be hidden entirely with [`hide navbar`](https://docassemble.org/docs/initial.html#hide%20navbar)
.

By default, **docassemble** uses a Bootstrap “dark” appearance for the navigation bar, but this can be customized using [`inverse navbar`](https://docassemble.org/docs/initial.html#inverse%20navbar)
.

The back button can be removed with [`navigation back button`](https://docassemble.org/docs/initial.html#navigation%20back%20button)
. You can put a back button next to the Continue button using [`question back button`](https://docassemble.org/docs/initial.html#question%20back%20button)
.

After the back button, the navigation bar shows the `title` [screen part](https://docassemble.org/docs/questions.html#screen%20parts)
. On small screens, the `short title` [screen part](https://docassemble.org/docs/questions.html#screen%20parts)
 appears instead. It is important to test interviews on a variety of devices to make sure that the title looks appropriate. The `title` and `short title` can be replaced with raw HTML using the `logo` and `short logo` [screen parts](https://docassemble.org/docs/questions.html#screen%20parts)
. Using large logos is strongly discouraged because users may be using your site on small-screen devices, and they will be annoyed that your advertising is taking up a large percent of the screen. If your logo increases the height of the navigation bar, you will need to customize the CSS to [correct for this](https://docassemble.org/docs/config.html#bootstrap%20theme)
.

If you see “</>” in the navigation bar when using an interview, that means you are in debug mode. This is turned on by default when you are using the Playground, but it is not appropriate in production. This is controlled with the [`debug`](https://docassemble.org/docs/config.html#debug)
 Configuration directive.

The interface in the upper-right corner, which reduces to a “hamburger icon” on small screens, lets the user log in, or shows a menu, or shows the exit button. This can be customized using:

*   The [`show login` metadata specifier](https://docassemble.org/docs/ui.html#show%20login)
     or the [`show login`](https://docassemble.org/docs/config.html#show%20login)
     Configuration directive.
*   The [`menu_items` special variable](https://docassemble.org/docs/special.html#menu_items)
    , which lets you add your own items to the menu.
*   The [`hide standard menu`](https://docassemble.org/docs/initial.html#hide%20standard%20menu)
     option in [`features`](https://docassemble.org/docs/initial.html#features)
    .
*   The [`exit label`](https://docassemble.org/docs/initial.html#exit%20label)
    , [`exit link`](https://docassemble.org/docs/initial.html#exit%20link)
    , and [`exit url`](https://docassemble.org/docs/initial.html#exit%20url)
     screen parts, which allow you to customize the exit button.
*   The [`administrative interviews`](https://docassemble.org/docs/config.html#administrative%20interviews)
     Configuration directive, which lets you add items to the menu for launching particular interviews or visiting particular URLs.
*   The [`show profile link`](https://docassemble.org/docs/config.html#show%20profile%20link)
     Configuration directive.
*   The [`show interviews link`](https://docassemble.org/docs/config.html#show%20interviews%20link)
     Configuration directive.
*   The [`show dispatch link`](https://docassemble.org/docs/config.html#show%20dispatch%20link)
     Configuration directive.
*   The [`hide corner interface`](https://docassemble.org/docs/initial.html#hide%20corner%20interface)
     feature, which completely removes the corner interface.

You can insert your own HTML in the upper-right corner of the screen using the [`navigation bar html`](https://docassemble.org/docs/initial.html#navigation%20bar%20html)
 screen part. This is not affected by [`hide corner interface`](https://docassemble.org/docs/initial.html#hide%20corner%20interface)
.

Other UI elements[¶](https://docassemble.org/docs/ui.html#favicon)

===================================================================

You can customize the [favicon](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Favicon)
 using the [`favicon`](https://docassemble.org/docs/config.html#favicon)
 Configuration directive. If you want a different favicon in different interviews, you can use [JavaScript](https://docassemble.org/docs/initial.html#javascript)
 to alter the [favicon](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Favicon)
.

You can add a [progress bar](https://docassemble.org/docs/initial.html#progress%20bar)
 to the top of the screen. You can also add a [secondary navigation bar](https://docassemble.org/docs/initial.html#navigation%20bar)
 that shows the sections of the interview. This navigation bar can be vertical on the left side of the screen or horizontal across the top of the screen. It is also possible to insert a version of the navigation bar into the body of a question.

* * *

---

# Interview developers' playground

[Edit](https://github.com/jhpyle/docassemble/blob/gh-pages/_docs/playground.md "Edit this page on GitHub")

Interview developers' playground
================================

Contents
--------

*   [1 Components of the Playground page](https://docassemble.org/docs/playground.html#components)
    *   [1.1 The YAML text editor](https://docassemble.org/docs/playground.html#interview_files)
        
    *   [1.2 The Templates folder](https://docassemble.org/docs/playground.html#templates)
        
    *   [1.3 The Static folder](https://docassemble.org/docs/playground.html#static)
        
    *   [1.4 The Sources folder](https://docassemble.org/docs/playground.html#sources)
        
    *   [1.5 The Modules folder](https://docassemble.org/docs/playground.html#modules)
        
    *   [1.6 The Packages folder](https://docassemble.org/docs/playground.html#packages)
        
    *   [1.7 The Variables sidebar](https://docassemble.org/docs/playground.html#variables)
        
    *   [1.8 Meaning of icons](https://docassemble.org/docs/playground.html#icons)
        
    *   [1.9 The buttons](https://docassemble.org/docs/playground.html#buttons)
        
    *   [1.10 The examples area](https://docassemble.org/docs/playground.html#examples)
        
    *   [1.11 Switching Projects](https://docassemble.org/docs/playground.html#projects)
        
    *   [1.12 Viewing other users’ Playgrounds](https://docassemble.org/docs/playground.html#other%20users)
        
*   [2 The Wizard](https://docassemble.org/docs/playground.html#wizard)
    
*   [3 Google Drive integration](https://docassemble.org/docs/playground.html#google%20drive)
    
*   [4 OneDrive integration](https://docassemble.org/docs/playground.html#onedrive)
    
*   [5 Microsoft Word sidebar](https://docassemble.org/docs/playground.html#word%20addin)
    *   [5.1 Installing the sidebar in Word Online](https://docassemble.org/docs/playground.html#word%20addin%20online%20install)
        
    *   [5.2 Installing the sidebar in the desktop version of Word](https://docassemble.org/docs/playground.html#word%20addin%20desktop%20install)
        
    *   [5.3 Using XML manifest files other than your own](https://docassemble.org/docs/playground.html#word%20addin%20other%20xml)
        
    *   [5.4 Using the Playground sidebar](https://docassemble.org/docs/playground.html#word%20addin%20using)
        
*   [6 Recovering from infinite loops](https://docassemble.org/docs/playground.html#infiniteloops)
    

The “Playground” within the **docassemble** web application is a testing ground for interviews. It allows you to write [YAML](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/YAML)
 in one or more “files” and then run an interview with one click.

Components of the Playground page[¶](https://docassemble.org/docs/playground.html#components)

==============================================================================================

![Playground](https://docassemble.org/img/playground.png)

The [YAML](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/YAML)
 text editor[¶](https://docassemble.org/docs/playground.html#interview_files)

-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

The main area of the Playground consists of an in-browser text editor with which you can edit [YAML](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/YAML)
 [interview](https://docassemble.org/docs/interviews.html)
 files.

![Playground](https://docassemble.org/img/playground-main.png)

When using the editor, you can press the F11 key to enter full-screen mode. Press the Esc key to exit full-screen mode.

To create a new [YAML](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/YAML)
 file, click the  [Add](javascript:return(0)) button.

To upload a [YAML](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/YAML)
 file, click the  [Upload](javascript:return(0)) button. The filename of the file you upload will be used as the name of the file in the Playground. If a file exists with the same name, it will be overwritten.

You can use multiple files to organize parts of a single interview. For example, if you have a file called “interview.yml” and a file called “questions.yml,” you can incorporate one into the other by reference.

For example, suppose your “interview.yml” file contains:

    ---
    include:
      - questions.yml
    ---
    mandatory: True
    code:
      say_hello
    ---

and suppose your “questions.yml” file contains:

    ---
    sets: say_hello
    question: |
      Hello, world!
    ---

If you run the “interview.yml” file, you will go to an interview that says “Hello, world!” The “interview.yml” interview knew how to ask `say_hello` because it incorporated the “questions.yml” file by reference.

If you write more than one interview, you might want to put all of your questions into a separate [YAML](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/YAML)
 file (e.g. “questions.yml”) so that you can easily re-use the questions in different contexts. Your main interview files will consist only of “mandatory” code blocks. Improvements you make to the questions will be available automatically to all interviews.

### Inserting names into the editor

You can also insert names into the text editor by clicking the button for the name in the list in the [Variables](https://docassemble.org/docs/playground.html#variables)
 area of the Playground.

When you are using the text editor, you can start typing a name and then press Ctrl-space to autocomplete the name. This can help if you have long variable names in your interview and you can’t remember their literal spelling, or you can’t remember the name of a function.

The Templates folder[¶](https://docassemble.org/docs/playground.html#templates)

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Within your Playground there are five “folders” where you can upload, download, and/or create various resources related to the development of your interviews. These include:

*   [Templates](https://docassemble.org/docs/playground.html#templates)
     for storing [Markdown](https://daringfireball.net/projects/markdown/)
     and PDF templates that are used in documents and interview questions.
*   [Static files](https://docassemble.org/docs/playground.html#static)
     for images, Javascript, CSS, and other auxillary files that your interview uses.
*   [Sources](https://docassemble.org/docs/playground.html#sources)
     for [YAML](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/YAML)
     files containing translations of text into other languages, as well as for training data for the [machine learning](https://docassemble.org/docs/ml.html)
     function of **docassemble**.
*   [Modules](https://docassemble.org/docs/playground.html#modules)
     for [Python](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Python_%28programming_language%29)
     code that extends the features of your interview. You can also embed [Python](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Python_%28programming_language%29)
     code directly into your interviews, but your interviews will be more readable if you keep its embedded [Python](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Python_%28programming_language%29)
     code limited to if/then/else statements.
*   [Packages](https://docassemble.org/docs/playground.html#packages)
     for bundling your interviews into packages for installation on a production server or other means of distribution.

The first of these is the Templates folder. If you create [documents](https://docassemble.org/docs/documents.html)
, you might want to use separate document templates. In a typical **docassemble** package, these templates are files in the `data/templates` subdirectory. In the Playground, they are stored in the “Templates” folder. For more information about the different types of template files that can be provided as options to the [`attachments`](https://docassemble.org/docs/documents.html#attachments)
 specifier, see [documents](https://docassemble.org/docs/documents.html)
.

For example, you can write [Markdown](https://daringfireball.net/projects/markdown/)
 text in a separate text file called `small_claims_complaint.md` in the Templates folder and then incorporate that text by reference by including the line `content file: small_claims_complaint.md` within an [`attachments`](https://docassemble.org/docs/documents.html#attachments)
 specifier.

![](https://docassemble.org/img/playground-templates-page-clipped.png)

When you edit a [Markdown](https://daringfireball.net/projects/markdown/)
 file in the Templates folder, the “Variables” sidebar is shown, so that you can have access to the list of variables in any of your interview files.

In the Templates folder, you can upload files. Markdown and YAML files can be edited. From the Templates screen, you can convert uploaded files from [Microsoft Word](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Microsoft_Word)
 format (`.docx`, `.doc`, or `.rtf`) or [OpenDocument](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/OpenDocument)
 format (`odt`) to Markdown (`.md`) format, so that you can include the text in your documents.

The Static folder[¶](https://docassemble.org/docs/playground.html#static)

--------------------------------------------------------------------------

If your interviews include images or sound, you can bundle image and audio files with your interview’s **docassemble** [package](https://docassemble.org/docs/packages.html)
 by including them within the `data/static` subdirectory. In the Playground, these files are located within the “Static” folder.

![](https://docassemble.org/img/playground-static-page-clipped.png)

In this area, you can upload files or write files of your own. For example, you might want to write your own Javascript files here, or upload images that you want to include in interview questions.

The Sources folder[¶](https://docassemble.org/docs/playground.html#sources)

----------------------------------------------------------------------------

![](https://docassemble.org/img/playground-sources-page-clipped.png)

This is where you can store data files for use by your interviews, including:

*   Translation files for the [`word()`](https://docassemble.org/docs/functions.html#word)
     function, which you can include using the [`words`](https://docassemble.org/docs/config.html#words)
     directive in the [configuration](https://docassemble.org/docs/config.html)
    .
*   Training data for the [machine learning](https://docassemble.org/docs/ml.html)
     function.

The Modules folder[¶](https://docassemble.org/docs/playground.html#modules)

----------------------------------------------------------------------------

If your interviews contain any complicated Python code, or you want to create your own classes, you should create a Python module and import its names into your interview. In a **docassemble** [package](https://docassemble.org/docs/packages.html)
, these are located in the main directory (the directory that contains the `data` subdirectory). In the Playground, these files are located within the “Modules” folder. In this area, you can write your own Python classes and functions.

![](https://docassemble.org/img/playground-modules-page-clipped.png)

The best way to incorporate your module into your interview is to use Python’s notation for “relative imports.” For example, if your module file is called `fruit.py`, you would import the module’s resources by writing a [`modules`](https://docassemble.org/docs/initial.html#modules)
 block:

    ---
    modules:
      - .fruit
    ---

This notation will work both in the Playground and when the interview is bundled as a Python module.

The Packages folder[¶](https://docassemble.org/docs/playground.html#packages)

------------------------------------------------------------------------------

The Packages area allows you to bundle the [interview files](https://docassemble.org/docs/playground.html#interview_files)
, [templates](https://docassemble.org/docs/playground.html#templates)
, [static files](https://docassemble.org/docs/playground.html#static)
, and/or [modules](https://docassemble.org/docs/playground.html#modules)
 from your Playground into a [Python package](https://docassemble.org/docs/packages.html)
 that can be downloaded as a ZIP file, installed on the server, or published to [PyPI](https://pypi.python.org/pypi)
.

You can keep track of one or more packages in the Packages folder. Each package has its own tab. To create a new package, click the  [Add](javascript:return(0)) button.

To view a different package, select it from the [](javascript:return(0))
pull-down menu.

If you have an existing package that was created using **docassemble**, you can upload its contents into the Playground by clicking the  [Upload](javascript:return(0)) button and uploading a ZIP file. The ZIP file should contain a single directory called, for example, `docassemble-packagename`, and this directory needs to contain the `setup.py` file and a `docassemble` directory. If the directory structure is different, **docassemble** may not be able to read your ZIP file. Note: this will work as expected if all of your modules are in the folder `docassemble-packagename/docassemble/packagename`, and templates are in the folder `docassemble-packagename/docassemble/packagename/data/templates`, etc. If you created subfolders within these folders, **docassemble** will not know where to put your files.

If there is a package on [GitHub](https://github.com/)
 or [PyPI](https://pypi.python.org/pypi)
 and you would like to bring the contents of that package into your Playground, click the  [Pull](javascript:return(0)) button.

This imports package files in much the same way as the Upload feature does, and has the same limitations.

![](https://docassemble.org/img/playground-packages-page-clipped.png)

To “Pull” a private GitHub repository, [set up GitHub integration](https://docassemble.org/docs/installation.html#github)
 and use the SSH name of the GitHub repository. For example, if your private repository is called `docassemble-familylaw`, and your GitHub username is called `jsmith`, use `git@github.com:jsmith/docassemble-familylaw.git` as the GitHub URL. Alternatively, you can use a GitHub URL that embeds an OAuth code.

Packages are defined with the following elements:

*   **Package Name**: By necessity, all **docassemble** packages are subpackages of the `docassemble` [namespace package](https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0420/)
    . If the Package Name is “bankruptcy,” the actual [Python package](https://docassemble.org/docs/packages.html)
     will be known as `docassemble.bankruptcy`.
*   **Version**: The version number you indicate here will be the version number of your [Python package](https://docassemble.org/docs/packages.html)
    .
*   **License**: This is the name of the software license that will be associated with your [Python package](https://docassemble.org/docs/packages.html)
    . The default license is “MIT” which is the license under which **docassemble** is distributed. This is probably what you want to use. However, if you want to take greater control over your intellectual property, you can indicate a different license. If you choose the MIT License, the content of the license will be included in the package, but if you choose another, you will need to edit the `LICENSE` file manually. The License must be written in the form of a valid [SPDX expression](https://packaging.python.org/en/latest/guides/writing-pyproject-toml/#license)
    . If you want to use a custom license, it must begin with `LicenseRef-`.
*   **Description**: This is a short description of your package (no more than 255 characters).
*   **URL**: This is a URL indicating where information about your software can be found.
*   **Dependencies**: From the list of Python [packages](https://docassemble.org/docs/packages.html)
     installed on the system, indicate the ones that are required for your package to operate. When your package is installed on another system, these packages will be installed first. When **docassemble** creates your package, it will include within the package a `setup.py` file that defines basic information about the package, including which other Python packages are dependencies. You can see what these dependencies are by inspecting the `setup.py` file and looking for the `install_requires` list. Note that the assumption is that the dependency packages are available on PyPI, the Python Package Index. If you have a dependency package that you installed on your system using a ZIP file or a GitHub URL, the `setup.py` file will nevertheless assume that the package is available on PyPI. If you need your `setup.py` to pull a dependency from GitHub, you will need to edit it manually, and if your package lives on GitHub, you will need to use command line `git` to commit changes.
*   **Interview files**: From the [interview files](https://docassemble.org/docs/playground.html#interview_files)
     defined in your Playground, indicate which ones should be included in your package.
*   **Template files**: From the [template files](https://docassemble.org/docs/playground.html#templates)
     defined in your Playground, indicate which ones should be included in your package.
*   **Static files**: From the [static files](https://docassemble.org/docs/playground.html#static)
     defined in your Playground, indicate which ones should be included in your package.
*   **Source files**: From the [source files](https://docassemble.org/docs/playground.html#sources)
     defined in your Playground, indicate which ones should be included in your package.
*   **Modules**: From the [modules](https://docassemble.org/docs/playground.html#modules)
     defined in your Playground, indicate which ones should be included in your package.
*   **README file**: This is a text box into which you can type the contents of the `README.md` file that will be included in your package. If you leave this blank, the `README.md` file will list the package name, package description, and author.

The page also gives a report about whether the package exists on [PyPI](https://pypi.python.org/pypi)
 or whether it exists as a repository in your [GitHub](https://github.com/)
 account.

At the bottom of the page, you can press one of the following buttons:

*   **Save**: Saves the package definition and does nothing more.
*   **Install**: Saves the package definition, creates a ZIP file containing the package, and installs that ZIP file on the server.
*   **PyPI**: Publishes the package on [PyPI](https://pypi.python.org/pypi)
     so that other people can install it. This will not be displayed unless you have set up the [PyPI configuration](https://docassemble.org/docs/config.html#pypi)
    , and it will only succeed if the user has provided a valid username and password in the user’s [profile](https://docassemble.org/docs/users.html#profile)
    .
*   **GitHub**: Publishes the package on [GitHub](https://github.com/)
     so that you can manage versions of your package, and also so that other people can install it. This will not be displayed unless you have set up the [GitHub configuration](https://docassemble.org/docs/config.html#github)
    . For more information about how the GitHub button works, see [Publishing on GitHub](https://docassemble.org/docs/packages.html#github)
    .
*   **Delete**: Deletes the package definition. If you installed the package on the system, it does not uninstall the package.

When you press Save, Install, PyPI, or GitHub, **docassemble** creates a [Python](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Python_%28programming_language%29)
 package from the information on this page, but it also uses information about you, such as your name and e-mail address, to indicate the author of the package. To change the author information that is included in the package, edit your [profile](https://docassemble.org/docs/users.html#profile)
.

If the references to [PyPI](https://pypi.python.org/pypi)
 and [GitHub](https://github.com/)
 do not appear for you, an administrator on your system will need to enable these features. See [sharing packages on PyPI](https://docassemble.org/docs/config.html#pypi)
 [setting up GitHub integration](https://docassemble.org/docs/installation.html#github)
 for more information.

For more information on managing [Python packages](https://docassemble.org/docs/packages.html)
 within **docassemble**, see [packages](https://docassemble.org/docs/packages.html)
.

The Variables sidebar[¶](https://docassemble.org/docs/playground.html#variables)

---------------------------------------------------------------------------------

![Variables](https://docassemble.org/img/playground-variables.png)

The “Variables” sidebar is a coding aid. It contains a list of variables and other names that are available for use in your interview. Clicking on one of the names will insert the name into the text editor. You may find this helpful if you are not sure of the exact spelling of a variable or a function.

In addition, if you start typing a name and then press Ctrl-Space, the editor will attempt to auto-complete the name for you. If there are multiple possibilities, you can choose the name from the list.

The list is updated when the page loads, or when you press “Save” or “Save and Run.”

The area lists the following types of names (which are color-coded):

*   Undefined names: names that are mentioned in your interview, but do not appear to have blocks that define them. This is based on code parsing, so sometimes there are false positives.
*   Variables: variables that are mentioned in your questions and code blocks, or that have been included in the Python namespace through a [`modules`](https://docassemble.org/docs/initial.html#modules)
     block. Variables in light blue are **docassemble**’s [special variables](https://docassemble.org/docs/special.html)
    . Variables in orange are variables that exist in the interview but may not be in use – at least, **docassemble**’s automatic process was not able to detect that the variables were used. This can be helpful for removing obsolete questions from your interview.
*   Functions: functions that are available because they have been included in the Python namespace through a [`modules`](https://docassemble.org/docs/initial.html#modules)
     block.
*   Classes: classes that are available because they have been included in the Python namespace through a [`modules`](https://docassemble.org/docs/initial.html#modules)
     block.
*   Modules defined: modules that are available because they have been included in the Python namespace through a [`imports`](https://docassemble.org/docs/initial.html#imports)
     block.
*   Modules available in Playground: modules that are available to be included with [`modules`](https://docassemble.org/docs/initial.html#modules)
     or [`imports`](https://docassemble.org/docs/initial.html#imports)
     because they exist in the [modules](https://docassemble.org/docs/playground.html#modules)
     folder of the Playground.
*   Templates: template files available in the [Templates folder](https://docassemble.org/docs/playground.html#templates)
     of the Playground.
*   Static files: static files available in the [Static folder](https://docassemble.org/docs/playground.html#static)
     of the Playground.
*   Decorations: decorations defined with [`images`](https://docassemble.org/docs/initial.html#images)
     or [`image sets`](https://docassemble.org/docs/initial.html#image%20sets)
     that are available for inclusion with [`decoration`](https://docassemble.org/docs/modifiers.html#decoration)
     or [emoji](https://docassemble.org/docs/markup.html#emoji)
     markup.

By default, the “Variables” sidebar shows variables for the [YAML](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/YAML)
 file you are currently editing. However, if the [YAML](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/YAML)
 file you are editing is a component of an interview, the information in the “Variables” will not be complete. At the top of the “Variables” area, you can select a different file that should be used for purposes of populating the “Variables” section. Usually the file you want to select here is the “top level” file for your interview, which [`include`](https://docassemble.org/docs/initial.html#include)
s the file you are editing.

Meaning of icons[¶](https://docassemble.org/docs/playground.html#icons)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

*   Clicking next to a variable name will search the [text editor](https://docassemble.org/docs/playground.html#interview_files)
     for the variable name.
*   Clicking shows a pop-up containing information about the item and a link to the item’s documentation.
*   Clicking reveals the methods of an [object](https://docassemble.org/docs/objects.html)
    .
*   Clicking next to a category name shows information about that category.

The buttons[¶](https://docassemble.org/docs/playground.html#buttons)

---------------------------------------------------------------------

![Buttons](https://docassemble.org/img/playground-buttons.png)

The Save button will save the interview and do nothing more.

The Save and Run button will save the interview and run the interview in another window.

The Download button will download the file that is currently being edited. Note that if there are unsaved changes, the changes will _not_ be reflected in the downloaded file; you would need to press the Save button first in order to download the changed file.

The Delete button will remove the interview from the Playground.

To give a link to the interview to someone else, click the  Share button to copy the URL to your clipboard.

Note that users do not need to log in to be able to run interviews using these links. If you want to protect your interviews during development, you can add an initial block like this, which will stop anyone other than a developer or administrator from using the interview:

    metadata:
      required privileges:
        - admin
        - developer

Note that the “Playground” is a development platform. If you are going to put an interview into production, put it into a package. You can create a package in the [packages area](https://docassemble.org/docs/playground.html#packages)
. If you share a link to a Playground interviews with someone, that person should understand that the interview might change at any moment, or produce an error. The Playground is a development environment. If you want to share a link that will be stable, install a package and share a link to the interview in that package.

If you look at the URL available from the  Share link, you will see that it points to the `/interview` endpoint on your server and includes an `i` parameter. The `i` parameter (`i` for “interview”) refers to the name of your YAML file. It will be something like `docassemble.playground1:myinterview.yml` where `1` is your user ID. If your interview is in a “project” inside your Playground, and the project is called `testing`, the name of the interview will be something like `docassemble.playground1testing:myinterview.yml`. If you you put your interview into a [package](https://docassemble.org/docs/packages.html)
 called `docassemble.missouri`, the `i` parameter will be something like `docassemble.missouri:data/questions/myinterview.yml`. The `data/questions` refers to a subdirectory inside of the `docassemble.missouri` [package](https://docassemble.org/docs/packages.html)
.

The examples area[¶](https://docassemble.org/docs/playground.html#examples)

----------------------------------------------------------------------------

![Example area](https://docassemble.org/img/playground-example-area.png)

The part of the page below the text editor is an interactive area where you can browse example blocks that demonstrate various features of **docassemble**. You can use these examples as models or to remind you of what the valid **docassemble** syntax is. You can also insert blocks directly into the text editor.

The area consists of three parts:

*   The “Example blocks” part is a list, in outline form, of example blocks that you can view.
*   The “Preview” part is a screenshot demonstrating what the example block does. If you click on the preview, an example interview containing the example block will open in another tab. There is a “View documentation” button that will open the page of the **docassemble** documentation pertaining to the concept illustrated in the example.
*   The “Source” part contains the text of the example block. You can click “Insert” to copy it into the text editor. You can click “Show context of example” to see the other blocks that are necessary for the example block to run as part of a working interview. This working interview is what you will run when you click the “Preview” screenshot.

Switching Projects[¶](https://docassemble.org/docs/playground.html#projects)

-----------------------------------------------------------------------------

By default, your Playground is like the “desktop” on a computer; it is a single area where a variety of different files can be stored. As you “play,” you will accumulate a variety of files, some of which might be from short-lived experiments and others of which might be bundled into a [package](https://docassemble.org/docs/packages.html)
. You can give yourself a “blank slate” in your Playground by creating a “Project.”

To manage your projects, click “Playground ” in the upper left corner.

![Playground](https://docassemble.org/img/playground-projects-caret.png)

This will open a menu listing the projects that are enabled on your site.

![Playground Projects](https://docassemble.org/img/playground-projects-caret-open.png)

This control will allow you to switch among your projects. For example, if you click “custody,” you will be taken to your “custody” project. The contents of the Playground and all of the folders will be different. You can tell which project is active by looking the title of the page:

![Playground](https://docassemble.org/img/playground-projects-caret-other.png)

At first, your only project will be the “Default Playground” project, so you will only see “Manage Projects” on the projects menu. If you click “Manage Projects,” you will be taken to the Projects page.

![Projects](https://docassemble.org/img/playground-projects-manage.png)

On this page, you can “Add a New Project,” rename existing projects, delete existing projects, or choose a new active project.
