 called `drafts`. The actual variable `favorite_food` is not set until the user presses “Continue,” but the `track_drafts` code discovers the “draft” value by calling [`action_argument()`](https://docassemble.org/docs/functions.html#action_argument)
.

Note that the values returned by `action_argument()` and `action_arguments()` are raw, unprocessed strings that come directly from the values of the HTML input elements, such as \`‘23.2’, ‘True’, ‘False’, and ‘’. The values might not meet input validation requirements.

Two special values can be retrieved with `action_argument()`:

*   `_initial` will be `True` for the first `check in` request, and for all other requests will be `False`.
*   `_changed` will be `None` for any periodic `check in` requests, and will be set to the name of a field for any `check in` requests that happen because a field was changed.

The `action_arguments()` dictionary does not contain these values; they are only available by calling `action_argument()`.

Note that unlike [background tasks](https://docassemble.org/docs/background.html#background)
, [`code`](https://docassemble.org/docs/code.html)
 that runs from `check in` **can** directly make permenent changes to the interview answers. Because it has this privilege, [`code`](https://docassemble.org/docs/code.html)
 that runs from `check in` must run quickly (in less than four seconds).

Updating the screen[¶](https://docassemble.org/docs/background.html#target)

----------------------------------------------------------------------------

You can also use `check in` to communicate information back to the screen.

### Template blocks[¶](https://docassemble.org/docs/background.html#target_template)

In the example above, [`check in`](https://docassemble.org/docs/background.html#check%20in)
 referred to a [`code`](https://docassemble.org/docs/code.html)
 block. In the example below, [`check in`](https://docassemble.org/docs/background.html#check%20in)
 refers to a [`template`](https://docassemble.org/docs/initial.html#template)
 block. The `content` of the [`template`](https://docassemble.org/docs/initial.html#template)
 is inserted into the user’s screen in an area designated by the [`template`](https://docassemble.org/docs/initial.html#template)
’s `target`.

    question: |
      What is your favorite food?
    fields:
      - Favorite food: favorite_food
    under: |
      [TARGET feedback]
    check in: question_food
    ---
    template: question_food
    content: |
      % if action_argument('favorite_food'):
      _What?_  You like
      ${ action_argument('favorite_food').upper() }?
      % endif
    target: feedback

[GitHub](https://github.com/jhpyle/docassemble/blob/master/docassemble_base/docassemble/base/data/questions/examples/target-template.yml "Open on GitHub")

[![Screenshot of target-template example](https://docassemble.org/img/examples/target-template.png)](https://demo.docassemble.org/interview?i=docassemble.base:data/questions/examples/target-template.yml&reset=1 "Click to try this interview")

If the user types “apples,” the following message will appear:

![Real time feedback](https://docassemble.org/img/target-template.png)

The timeline for this process is as follows:

1.  When the screen is drawn, the `[TARGET feedback]` markup in the [`under`](https://docassemble.org/docs/questions.html#under)
     text area of the question (the area underneath the “Continue” button) is converted into an invisible placeholder area identified by the keyword `feedback`.
2.  The user types “apples” into the “Favorite food” field.
3.  The browser “checks in” with the server, sending the values of all the fields on the page.
4.  On the server, **docassemble** seeks a definition for `question_food`. It finds a [`template`](https://docassemble.org/docs/initial.html#template)
     that offers to define `question_food`. This [`template`](https://docassemble.org/docs/initial.html#template)
     has a `target`, so it will behave differently than ordinary [`template`](https://docassemble.org/docs/initial.html#template)
    s. Instead of setting the variable `question_food` to a [`DALazyTemplate`](https://docassemble.org/docs/objects.html#DALazyTemplate)
     object, **docassemble** will return the `content` to the browser, indicating that it is “targeted” for the area on the screen called `feedback`.
5.  The web browser takes the content and plugs it into the invisible placeholder area identified by the keyword `feedback`.

If you want to run [`code`](https://docassemble.org/docs/code.html)
 and then use a [`template`](https://docassemble.org/docs/initial.html#template)
 to communicate results back to the user, just include a reference to the [`template`](https://docassemble.org/docs/initial.html#template)
 at the end of your [`code`](https://docassemble.org/docs/code.html)
:

    question: |
      What is your favorite food?
    fields:
      - Favorite food: favorite_food
    under: |
      [TARGET feedback]
    check in: question_food
    ---
    event: question_food
    code: |
      if action_argument('favorite_food'):
        the_food = action_argument('favorite_food').upper()
      else:
        the_food = 'nothing'
      food_message
    ---
    template: food_message
    content: |
      _What?_  You like ${ the_food }?
    target: feedback

[GitHub](https://github.com/jhpyle/docassemble/blob/master/docassemble_base/docassemble/base/data/questions/examples/target-code-template.yml "Open on GitHub")

[![Screenshot of target-code-template example](https://docassemble.org/img/examples/target-code-template.png)](https://demo.docassemble.org/interview?i=docassemble.base:data/questions/examples/target-code-template.yml&reset=1 "Click to try this interview")

### Populating targets with background\_response()[¶](https://docassemble.org/docs/background.html#background_response_target)

Another way to send messages to the user’s screen is to use [`code`](https://docassemble.org/docs/code.html)
 to plug raw [HTML](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/HTML)
 into `[TARGET ...]` areas:

    question: |
      What is your favorite food?
    fields:
      - Favorite food: favorite_food
    under: |
      [TARGET feedback]
    check in: question_food
    ---
    event: question_food
    code: |
      food = action_argument('favorite_food')
      if food:
        content = "What? You like " + food.upper() + "?"
      else:
        content = ''
      background_response(target='feedback',
                          content=content)
    ---
    mandatory: True
    question: |
      Your favorite food is ${ favorite_food }.

[GitHub](https://github.com/jhpyle/docassemble/blob/master/docassemble_base/docassemble/base/data/questions/examples/target-code.yml "Open on GitHub")

[![Screenshot of target-code example](https://docassemble.org/img/examples/target-code.png)](https://demo.docassemble.org/interview?i=docassemble.base:data/questions/examples/target-code.yml&reset=1 "Click to try this interview")

Calling [`background_response()`](https://docassemble.org/docs/background.html#background_response)
 with the keyword arguments `target` and `content` will result in the `content` being plugged into the user’s screen in the `[TARGET ...]` area designated by the `target` argument. Unlike the method that uses [`template`](https://docassemble.org/docs/initial.html#template)
s, this method does not convert [Markdown](https://daringfireball.net/projects/markdown/)
 to [HTML](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/HTML)
; rather, the `content` is inserted as raw [HTML](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/HTML)
.

If you want to plug text into more than one `[TARGET ...]` area, you can do so by calling [`background_response()`](https://docassemble.org/docs/background.html#background_response)
 with a [list](https://docs.python.org/3.12/tutorial/datastructures.html)
 of [dict](https://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#dict)
s, where each [dict](https://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#dict)
 has the keys `target` and `content`.

    check in: question_food
    question: |
      What is your favorite food?  [TARGET feedback_one]
    fields:
      - Favorite food: favorite_food
    under: |
      [TARGET feedback_two]
    ---
    event: question_food
    code: |
      food = action_argument('favorite_food')
      if food:
        content_one = "  (Please don't be gross.)"
        content_two = "What? You like " + food.upper() + "?"
      else:
        content_one = ''
        content_two = ''
      background_response([{'target': 'feedback_one',\
                          'content': content_one},\
                          {'target': 'feedback_two',\
                          'content': content_two}])

[GitHub](https://github.com/jhpyle/docassemble/blob/master/docassemble_base/docassemble/base/data/questions/examples/target-code-multiple.yml "Open on GitHub")

[![Screenshot of target-code-multiple example](https://docassemble.org/img/examples/target-code-multiple.png)](https://demo.docassemble.org/interview?i=docassemble.base:data/questions/examples/target-code-multiple.yml&reset=1 "Click to try this interview")

### Populating fields with background\_response()[¶](https://docassemble.org/docs/background.html#background_response_fields)

Another way to communicate results to the user’s screen is to populate input elements. If you call `background_response()` with a [dictionary](https://docs.python.org/3/tutorial/datastructures.html#dictionaries)
 as the first parameter and `'fields'` as the second parameter, the fields on the screen will be populated with the values indicated in the dictionary. The keys of the dictionary should be variable names currently displaying on the screen (e.g., as they are defined in the [`fields`](https://docassemble.org/docs/fields.html#fields)
 specifier). The values of the dictionary should be the values that you want the fields to have. For example:

    question: |
      Let's calculate some stuff.
    fields:
      - A: var_a
        datatype: number
      - B: var_b
        datatype: number
      - C: var_c
        datatype: number
    check in: add_numbers
    ---
    mandatory: true
    question: |
      % if var_c == var_a * var_b:
      The correct answer is ${ var_c }.
      % else:
      That answer is not right!
      % endif
    ---
    event: add_numbers
    code: |
      if action_argument('var_a') != '' and action_argument('var_b') != '':
        answer = float(action_argument('var_a')) * float(action_argument('var_b'))
        background_response({'var_c': answer}, 'fields')
      background_response()

[GitHub](https://github.com/jhpyle/docassemble/blob/master/docassemble_base/docassemble/base/data/questions/examples/ajax-calc.yml "Open on GitHub")

[![Screenshot of ajax-calc example](https://docassemble.org/img/examples/ajax-calc.png)](https://demo.docassemble.org/interview?i=docassemble.base:data/questions/examples/ajax-calc.yml&reset=1 "Click to try this interview")

### Populating dropdown choices with background\_response()[¶](https://docassemble.org/docs/background.html#background_response_choices)

You can also use the `'fields'` form of `background_response()` to change the options in a dropdown list of a field on the screen. To do this, call `background_response()` as though you are setting the value of the dropdown field, but in place of the value, substitute a Python dictionary with the keys `choices` and `value`. The `choices` key should refer to a data structure containing the options that the dropdown list will have. The `value` key is optional, but if it is included it will cause the indicated value to be selected.

    question: |
      Where do you live?
    fields:
      - Country: country
        code: countries_list()
      - State: state
        code: |
          states_list(country_code=showifdef('country', 'US', prior=True)) \
          or \
          ['N/A']
        js enable if: val('country')
    check in: update_states_list
    ---
    event: update_states_list
    code: |
      if action_argument('_changed') == 'country':
        background_response(
          {
            'state':
              {
                'choices': \
                  states_list(country_code=action_argument('country')) \
                  or \
                  ['N/A']
              }
          }, 'fields')
      background_response()

[GitHub](https://github.com/jhpyle/docassemble/blob/master/docassemble_base/docassemble/base/data/questions/examples/country-state.yml "Open on GitHub")

[![Screenshot of country-state example](https://docassemble.org/img/examples/country-state.png)](https://demo.docassemble.org/interview?i=docassemble.base:data/questions/examples/country-state.yml&reset=1 "Click to try this interview")

The data structure indicated by `choices` can be:

*   a list of strings, in which case each string will be used as both the label and the value;
*   a dictionary mapping values to labels;
*   a list of single-item dictionaries mapping values to labels;
*   a list of two-item lists, where the first item is the value and the second item is the label.

In this example, `choices` is being set to the output of `states_list()`, which returns a Python `dict` that maps state abbreviations to state names. Since there are some countries that do not have states, it is possible that the Python `dict` will be empty. If a dropdown field is required but has no choices, the user will not be able to continue. In this example, the Python code `or {'N/A': country_name(action_argument('country'))}` follows after the call to `states_list()`, ensuring that there always at least one choice.

When designing a screen like this, it is important to keep in mind the different scenarios in which the screen might be shown, and to ensure that the screen displays correctly in each context. The contexts include:

1.  The user arrives at the screen for the first time, and the Python variables `country` and `state` are undefined.
2.  The user arrives at the screen by clicking the Back button on the next screen. In this scenario, the Python variables `country` and `state` are undefined, but **docassemble** will use the values the user previously entered as default values for the fields.
3.  The user arrives at the screen as part of a review process, and the Python variables `country` and `state` are defined.

When the screen first loads, the “State” dropdown field is populated with:

    states_list(country_code=showifdef('country', 'US', prior=True)) or ['N/A']

Obtaining the country code with [`showifdef()`](https://docassemble.org/docs/functions.html#showifdef)
 is important because the country may or may not be defined. If the Python variable `country` is not defined, the list of states for the country `'US'` is used. If `states_list()` returns an empty dictionary, the `or ['N/A']` ensures that there is at least one choice. It is important that the the “State” dropdown is populated with one or more choices when the screen first loads, because **docassemble**’s standard behavior when a dropdown has no choices is to omit the field from the screen and define the variable as `None` when the user presses “Continue.”

When the user arrives at the screen for the first time, and the “Country” field is not populated, the State dropdown will be disabled due to `js enable if: val('country')`. When the user selects a country, the `change` event on the Country field will be triggered, causing the State dropdown to become enabled and the `check in` action to run with the `_changed` argument set to `'country'`. When the `check in` action returns a response to the browser, the list of choices for the “State” field will be updated. Thus, it does not matter what the initial choices of the “State” field are, since the user will not see them. But it is important that there are initial choices, because otherwise **docassemble** will assume that the field should be omitted from the screen entirely.

The call to `showifdef()` uses the special `prior=True` keyword parameter, which means that if the user arrived at the screen after pressing the Back button, `showifdef()` will return the value of `country` that the user had previously entered. This is important because when the user arrives at the screen by pressing the Back button, **docassemble** will populate the “Country” field with the value of `country` that the user previously entered, and the list of states should correspond with that `country`.

Note that `check in` code runs whenever any field on the screen changes, and also runs every six seconds. To avoid unnecessary changes to the screen, the `check in` code only returns a substantial response if `action_argument('_changed') == 'country'`. It is important that screen updates only happen at the particular times when you want them to happen, because otherwise screen elements may update while the user is interacting with them, resulting in a glitchy user experience.

As you can see from the above example, updating screens by making asynchronous calls to the server is complicated. By using separate screens to gather related variables, you can save yourself a lot of time that could be put to a more productive use.

### Showing messages with background\_response()[¶](https://docassemble.org/docs/background.html#background_response_flash)

By setting the second parameter of `background_response()` to `'flash'` and the first parameter to a message, you can “flash” a message at the top of the user’s screen. In this example, a message is flashed as soon as the user enters a favorite fruit.

    question: |
      What is your favorite food?
    fields:
      - Favorite food: favorite_food
      - Favorite drink: favorite_drink
    check in: criticize_food
    ---
    code: |
      favorite_food_interim = ''
    ---
    event: criticize_food
    code: |
      if action_argument('favorite_food') and action_argument('favorite_food') != favorite_food_interim:
        favorite_food_interim = action_argument('favorite_food')
        background_response("What? You like " + favorite_food_interim.upper() + "?", 'flash')
      background_response()

[GitHub](https://github.com/jhpyle/docassemble/blob/master/docassemble_base/docassemble/base/data/questions/examples/ajax-flash.yml "Open on GitHub")

[![Screenshot of ajax-flash example](https://docassemble.org/img/examples/ajax-flash.png)](https://demo.docassemble.org/interview?i=docassemble.base:data/questions/examples/ajax-flash.yml&reset=1 "Click to try this interview")

### Running JavaScript with background\_response()[¶](https://docassemble.org/docs/background.html#background_response_javascript)

Another way to communicate results to the user’s screen is to use [JavaScript](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/JavaScript)
. If you call `background_response()` with some [JavaScript](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/JavaScript)
 code (as text) as the first parameter and `'javascript'` as the second parameter, the [JavaScript](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/JavaScript)
 code will be run in the browser.

    question: |
      Let's calculate some stuff.
    fields:
      - A: var_a
        datatype: number
      - B: var_b
        datatype: number
      - C: var_c
        datatype: number
    check in: add_numbers
    ---
    mandatory: true
    question: |
      % if var_c == var_a * var_b:
      The correct answer is ${ var_c }.
      % else:
      That answer is not right!
      % endif
    ---
    event: add_numbers
    code: |
      if action_argument('var_a') != '' and action_argument('var_b') != '':
        answer = float(action_argument('var_a')) * float(action_argument('var_b'))
        background_response('flash("Hint: C should be " + ' + str(answer) + ', "success")', 'javascript')
      background_response()

[GitHub](https://github.com/jhpyle/docassemble/blob/master/docassemble_base/docassemble/base/data/questions/examples/ajax-calc-javascript.yml "Open on GitHub")

[![Screenshot of ajax-calc-javascript example](https://docassemble.org/img/examples/ajax-calc-javascript.png)](https://demo.docassemble.org/interview?i=docassemble.base:data/questions/examples/ajax-calc-javascript.yml&reset=1 "Click to try this interview")

This example uses the [`flash()`](https://docassemble.org/docs/functions.html#flash)
 function in [JavaScript](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/JavaScript)
 to display a message for the user.

See the [Javascript functions](https://docassemble.org/docs/functions.html#javascript)
 section for more information about things you can do with [JavaScript](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/JavaScript)
.

### Refreshing the screen with background\_response()[¶](https://docassemble.org/docs/background.html#background_response_refresh)

Another strategy is to use `check in` code to cause a refresh of the user’s screen. If your `check in` code ends with `background_response('refresh')`, the user’s screen will reload the [`question`](https://docassemble.org/docs/questions.html#question)
 from the server. The following example shows how you can use this to dynamically update the list of choices in a radio button list.

    mandatory: True
    code: |
      number_of_things = 3
    ---
    question: |
      Tell me which thing you want.
    fields:
      - "How many things are there?": number_of_things
        datatype: integer
        min: 1
        max: 10
      - "Which thing do you want?": thing
        input type: radio
        code: |
          range(1, number_of_things + 1)
    check in: set_number_of_things
    ---
    event: set_number_of_things
    code: |
      if action_argument('number_of_things') and number_of_things != int(action_argument('number_of_things')):
        number_of_things = int(action_argument('number_of_things'))
        if number_of_things < 1:
          number_of_things = 1
        elif number_of_things > 10:
          number_of_things = 10
        background_response('refresh')
      background_response()

[GitHub](https://github.com/jhpyle/docassemble/blob/master/docassemble_base/docassemble/base/data/questions/examples/ajax-refresh.yml "Open on GitHub")

[![Screenshot of ajax-refresh example](https://docassemble.org/img/examples/ajax-refresh.png)](https://demo.docassemble.org/interview?i=docassemble.base:data/questions/examples/ajax-refresh.yml&reset=1 "Click to try this interview")

It is important that your `check in` code does not call `background_response('refresh')` every single time it runs. Otherwise, you will cause an infinite loop of screen refreshing. In this example, `background_response('refresh')` is only called when necessary (when the value of `number_of_things` changes).

Scheduled tasks[¶](https://docassemble.org/docs/background.html#scheduled)

===========================================================================

The “scheduled tasks” feature of **docassemble** allows your interviews to do things when the user is not using the interview.

For example, if your interview guides a user through a legal process that requires the user to file a document in court if the opposing party does not respond within 20 days, your interview can send an e-mail to the user after that 20 day period has expired, reminding the user to resume the interview so that he or she can prepare the appropriate legal document.

    mandatory: True
    code: |
      allow_cron = True
      multi_user = True
    ---
    initial: True
    code: |
      process_action()
    ---
    question: |
      When was the document filed?
    fields:
      - Filing Date: filing_date
        datatype: date
    ---
    question: |
      What is your e-mail address?
    fields:
      - E-mail: email_address
        datatype: email
    ---
    mandatory: True
    question: |
      Ok, I'll e-mail you at ${ email_address} 20 days
      from ${ format_date(filing_date) }.
    buttons:
      Leave: leave
    ---
    template: reminder_email
    subject: |
      Hey, it's been 20 days.
    content: |
      Don't forget about that thing you need to do!
    ---
    event: cron_daily
    code: |
      if task_not_yet_performed('20 day reminder') and date_difference(starting=filing_date).days > 20:
        send_email(to=email_address, template=reminder_email, task='20 day reminder')
      response()
    ---

Let’s go through this example step-by-step.

First, we set [`allow_cron`](https://docassemble.org/docs/special.html#allow_cron)
 to `True`, which allows scheduled tasks to run, and we set [`multi_user`](https://docassemble.org/docs/special.html#multi_user)
 to `True`, which disables [server-side encryption](https://docassemble.org/docs/security.html#server_encryption)
. (We need to disable this feature so that the computer can access the interview when the user is not logged in. The user’s data is still secure; it just does not have the layer of additional security provided by server-side encryption.)

    ---
    mandatory: True
    code: |
      allow_cron = True
      multi_user = True
    ---

Next, there are three standard [`question`](https://docassemble.org/docs/questions.html#question)
s that gather the `filing_date` and `email_address` variables and present a “final” screen to the user. Note that on the final screen, there is no [`exit`](https://docassemble.org/docs/questions.html#exit)
 button, only a [`leave`](https://docassemble.org/docs/questions.html#leave)
 button. If the user clicked an [`exit`](https://docassemble.org/docs/questions.html#exit)
 button, the interview session would be erased from the server. By contrast, clicking [`leave`](https://docassemble.org/docs/questions.html#leave)
 keeps the interview session on the server. This is important because we want the interview to persist on the server. We need the interview to exist twenty days after the `filing_date` so that it can send the reminder e-mail.

    ---
    question: |
      When was the document filed?
    fields:
      - Filing Date: filing_date
        datatype: date
    ---
    question: |
      What is your e-mail address?
    fields:
      - E-mail: email_address
        datatype: email
    ---
    mandatory: True
    question: |
      Ok, I'll e-mail you at ${ email_address} 20 days
      from ${ format_date(filing_date) }.
    buttons:
      Leave: leave
    ---

After this, we define the [`template`](https://docassemble.org/docs/initial.html#template)
 for the e-mail that will be sent.

    ---
    template: reminder_email
    subject: |
      Hey, it's been 20 days.
    content: |
      Don't forget about that thing you need to do!
    ---

Finally, we get to the “scheduled task.” The [`event`](https://docassemble.org/docs/fields.html#event)
 line designates the [special variable](https://docassemble.org/docs/special.html)
 [`cron_daily`](https://docassemble.org/docs/special.html#cron_daily)
. This code will run once per day.

    ---
    event: cron_daily
    code: |
      if task_not_yet_performed('20 day reminder') and date_difference(starting=filing_date).days > 20:
        send_email(to=email_address, template=reminder_email, task='20 day reminder')
      response()
    ---

The first thing the code does (wisely) is question whether the e-mail reminder has already been sent. If the e-mail has already been sent, it would be annoying to send the same e-mail again, every single day, so we prevent that from happening. (The [`task_not_yet_performed()`](https://docassemble.org/docs/functions.html#tasks)
 function is part of **docassemble**’s [task system](https://docassemble.org/docs/functions.html#tasks)
.)

Next, the code evaluates whether the 20 day period has passed, using the [`date_difference()`](https://docassemble.org/docs/functions.html#date_difference)
 function. If at least 20 days have passed, the e-mail is sent. The [`send_email()`](https://docassemble.org/docs/functions.html#send_email)
 function marks the “task” as “performed” if the e-mail successfully sends.

The [`response()`](https://docassemble.org/docs/functions.html#response)
 function is necessary to indicate that the task has finished. If this is not included, the interview logic will be evaluated after the [`code`](https://docassemble.org/docs/code.html)
 block finishes. In some cases, this will be what you want, but in most cases this is not what you want.

In the context of the web application, the [`response()`](https://docassemble.org/docs/functions.html#response)
 function stops code execution and returns an HTTP response to the browser, but in the context of a scheduled task, it merely stops code execution. If you pass text to [`response()`](https://docassemble.org/docs/functions.html#response)
, the text will be printed to the output.

By default, any changes made to the interview answers will be saved. Thus, your scheduled task can affect what the user sees in the web application. However, if your code does not change the answers, or you do not need the answers to be saved, you can call `response(null=True)`. This will tell **docassemble** not to save the answers. Doing this can help conserve system resources. This behavior of the [`response()`](https://docassemble.org/docs/functions.html#response)
 function only works in the context of scheduled tasks.

If you are using [Docker](https://docassemble.org/docs/docker.html)
 and you want to access the log of output of scheduled tasks, use [`docker exec`](https://docs.docker.com/engine/reference/commandline/exec/)
 to enter the container and look at the file `/var/spool/mail/mail`. The log consists of e-mail messages. You can also view these messages using the `mail` command.

Enabling scheduled tasks[¶](https://docassemble.org/docs/background.html#enabling)

-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Scheduled tasks need to be triggered by some external source.

If you run **docassemble** on [Docker](https://docassemble.org/docs/docker.html)
, you do not have to worry about how the scheduled tasks are triggered; the tasks operate automatically. The tasks enabled in the [Docker](https://docassemble.org/docs/docker.html)
 setup are:

*   `cron_hourly`
*   `cron_daily`
*   `cron_weekly`
*   `cron_monthly`

If you are not using [Docker](https://docassemble.org/docs/docker.html)
, you will have to set up a system of running the [`docassemble.webapp.cron`](https://github.com/jhpyle/docassemble/blob/master/docassemble_webapp/docassemble/webapp/cron.py)
 module at regular intervals.

On Linux, the trigger can be a script installed as part of the [cron](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cron)
 system. For example, a script in `/etc/cron.daily` could run:

    python -m docassemble.webapp.cron -type cron_daily

A script in `/etc/cron.weekly` could run:

    python -m docassemble.webapp.cron -type cron_weekly

(And so on.)

The details of how exactly this command should be invoked depend on how you have [installed](https://docassemble.org/docs/installation.html)
 **docassemble**. For example, the command should run under the same user as the web server, and if you have installed Python using a [virtualenv](https://docs.python-guide.org/dev/virtualenvs/)
, you need to invoke [Python](https://www.python.org/)
 appropriately.

Note that you can use any variable name you want in the `-type` argument to the [`docassemble.webapp.cron`](https://github.com/jhpyle/docassemble/blob/master/docassemble_webapp/docassemble/webapp/cron.py)
 module. The variable name is passed to the interview exactly as though it were the name of an [action](https://docassemble.org/docs/background.html#background)
 given by [`url_action()`](https://docassemble.org/docs/functions.html#url_action)
.

What the “cron” module does[¶](https://docassemble.org/docs/background.html#cron)

----------------------------------------------------------------------------------

The [`docassemble.webapp.cron`](https://github.com/jhpyle/docassemble/blob/master/docassemble_webapp/docassemble/webapp/cron.py)
 module does two things:

1.  It cleans out inactive interviews if `-type` is `cron_daily`.
2.  It runs scheduled tasks in interviews, invoking them as [actions](https://docassemble.org/docs/background.html#background)
    .

### Deleting interviews after a period of inactivity[¶](https://docassemble.org/docs/background.html#deleting)

If the type of scheduled task is `cron_daily`, the [`docassemble.webapp.cron`](https://github.com/jhpyle/docassemble/blob/master/docassemble_webapp/docassemble/webapp/cron.py)
 module will delete interviews that have been inactive for 90 days or longer. (This period can be [configured](https://docassemble.org/docs/config.html#interview%20delete%20days)
.) Activity is measured by whether the interview answers have been updated within the period. This applies to all interviews stored in the system that have not yet been deleted.

Note that interviews can be deleted from the system two other ways:

1.  When the user clicks an [`exit`](https://docassemble.org/docs/questions.html#exit)
     button; and
2.  If the user goes to the Interviews page and clicks a “Delete” button next to a listed interview, or clicks “Delete all.”

### Running scheduled tasks[¶](https://docassemble.org/docs/background.html#running%20scheduled)

The [`docassemble.webapp.cron`](https://github.com/jhpyle/docassemble/blob/master/docassemble_webapp/docassemble/webapp/cron.py)
 looks at every interview in the system for which [server-side encryption](https://docassemble.org/docs/security.html#server_encryption)
 has been turned off. (Disabling [server-side encryption](https://docassemble.org/docs/security.html#server_encryption)
 is performed by setting the [`multi_user`](https://docassemble.org/docs/special.html#multi_user)
 variable in the interview to `True`). The module then inspects the interview data to see if [`allow_cron`](https://docassemble.org/docs/special.html#allow_cron)
 is set to `True`. If is, it will see if the interview uses the variable given with the `-type` argument. For example, if the type is [`cron_weekly`](https://docassemble.org/docs/special.html#cron_weekly)
, the module will check if the interview has a block that offers to define the variable [`cron_weekly`](https://docassemble.org/docs/special.html#cron_weekly)
. If there is such a block, the module will run the interview with the [action](https://docassemble.org/docs/background.html#background)
 `cron_weekly` (and no [action](https://docassemble.org/docs/background.html#background)
 arguments). Any changes made to the interview variables will be saved.

Interviews containing scheduled tasks will run regularly, and the interview variables will be updated. This means that even if there is no activity from the original user, there will appear to be regular activity in the interview, which means that the [interview deletion](https://docassemble.org/docs/background.html#deleting)
 feature will never delete such interviews. Usually, it is a good thing that the [interview deletion](https://docassemble.org/docs/background.html#deleting)
 feature does not automatically delete interviews with scheduled tasks; you might have an interview that does something after a period of several months have passed.

However, you might not want your interviews to run scheduled tasks indefinitely. For example, in the example interview above, the interview will persist after the e-mail sends, and will stay on the server forever. The [`cron_daily`](https://docassemble.org/docs/special.html#cron_daily)
 code will run on a daily basis forever, doing nothing useful.

To get around this problem, you can instruct your scheduled task to delete the interview when it is no longer necessary to keep the interview alive. For example, you can include the following:

    ---
    event: cron_monthly
    code: |
      if last_access_days() > 365:
        command('exit')
    ---

This will run on a monthly basis, and will use the [`last_access_days()`](https://docassemble.org/docs/functions.html#last_access_days)
 function to check whether interview has been accessed by a real user (that is, a user other than the [cron user](https://docassemble.org/docs/background.html#cron%20user)
) in the past year. If it has not, the interview will [`exit`](https://docassemble.org/docs/questions.html#exit)
, meaning that the interview will be deleted from the server.

The cron user[¶](https://docassemble.org/docs/background.html#cron%20user)

---------------------------------------------------------------------------

Scheduled tasks do not run as the user who started the interview; they always run using the special “cron user.” Therefore, if you want your scheduled task to send an e-mail to “the user,” make sure you collect the real user’s e-mail address into a variable beforehand. During the operation of a scheduled task, a call to [`user_info()`](https://docassemble.org/docs/functions.html#user_info)
 will retrieve information about the “cron user,” which is not what you want.

If your interview is a [multi-user interview](https://docassemble.org/docs/roles.html)
, make sure that the “cron user” is not inadvertently kicked out of your interview before [`process_action()`](https://docassemble.org/docs/functions.html#process_action)
 is run. For example, an interview with this code could prevent scheduled tasks from running:

    ---
    default role: organizer
    code: |
      multi_user = True
      role = 'organizer'
      if introduction_made and participants_invited:
        if user_logged_in():
          if user_info().email == first_person_email:
            role = 'first_person'
          elif user_info().email == second_person_email:
            role = 'second_person'
    ---
    initial: True
    code: |
      if role != first_person' and not ready_for_other_people:
        say_goodbye_to_user
    ---
    initial: True:
    code: |
      process_action()
    ---
    event: cron_daily
    code: |
      do_something_important()
    ---

When the “cron user” accesses this interview, it will not be able to run the `cron_daily` action, because it will immediately be presented with the `say_goodbye_to_user` screen.

One way to get around this problem is to move the [`initial`](https://docassemble.org/docs/logic.html#initial)
 [`code`](https://docassemble.org/docs/code.html)
 block that runs [`process_action()`](https://docassemble.org/docs/functions.html#process_action)
 so that it appears before the block that runs `say_goodbye_to_user`:

    ---
    initial: True:
    code: |
      process_action()
    ---
    initial: True
    code: |
      if role != first_person' and not ready_for_other_people:
        say_goodbye_to_user
    ---

However, this opens up the possibility that someone in the role of `second_person` could run [actions](https://docassemble.org/docs/background.html#background)
 without being screened. If this is a problem, you could alternatively do:

    ---
    initial: True
    code: |
      if (role != first_person' and not user_has_privilege('cron')) and not ready_for_other_people:
        say_goodbye_to_user
    ---

The “cron user” is the only user on the system with the [privilege](https://docassemble.org/docs/users.html)
 of `cron`, so you can use the [`user_has_privilege()`](https://docassemble.org/docs/functions.html#user_has_privilege)
 function to detect whether the user is the “cron user.”

Long-running scheduled tasks[¶](https://docassemble.org/docs/background.html#cron%20long)

------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

If your scheduled tasks take more than a couple of seconds to run (for example if they download information from the internet), then they should run the long-running code as a [background task](https://docassemble.org/docs/background.html#background)
. While the scheduled task is running, the task holds the interview variables in memory and writes them to the SQL server when it finishes. If a user accesses the interview through the web interface at the same time as the schedule task is running, the user’s changes to the interview could be wiped out. (The web interface will wait for four seconds if it sees that the interview variables are in use, but after four seconds have elapsed, it will assume the task that was using the interview variables has failed, and it will start using the interview variables.)

To run [`code`](https://docassemble.org/docs/code.html)
 in the background from within a scheduled task, just combine what you have learned in the [scheduled tasks](https://docassemble.org/docs/background.html#scheduled)
 section with what you learned in the [background tasks](https://docassemble.org/docs/background.html#background)
 section.

    event: cron_daily
    code: |
      background_action('long_task', None)
      response()
    ---
    event: long_task
    code: |
      result = do_something_time_consuming()
      background_response_action('finalize_long_task', result=result)
    ---
    event: finalize_long_task
    code: |
      the_status = action_argument('result')
      response()

E-mailing the interview[¶](https://docassemble.org/docs/background.html#email)

===============================================================================

An interview can allow users to send e-mails to it.

Here is how it works:

*   The interview calls [`interview_email()`](https://docassemble.org/docs/functions.html#interview_email)
     to obtain a unique e-mail address for the interview session, such as `kgjeir@help.example.com`, and shares that e-mail address with the user.
*   When an e-mail is sent to `kgjeir@help.example.com`, the server stores the e-mail.
*   The interview uses [`get_emails()`](https://docassemble.org/docs/functions.html#get_emails)
     to retrieve a list of e-mails that have been sent to `kgjeir@help.example.com`.

    question: |
      E-mailing to the interview
    subquestion: |
      When you press continue, you will be
      provided with an e-mail address to
      which you can send an email.
    field: intro_seen
    ---
    need: intro_seen
    mandatory: true
    question: |
      E-mails sent to case
    subquestion: |
      Send an e-mail to
      ${ interview_email() }
      and click Refresh.
      
      Be patient, though.  It takes a
      few seconds for e-mails to travel
      and be processed.
      
      % for item in get_emails():
        % if len(item.emails):
      Subject lines of all e-mails sent
      to ${ item.address }:
      
          % for mail_item in item.emails:
      * ${ mail_item.subject }
          % endfor
        % endif
      % endfor
    buttons:
      - Refresh: refresh

[GitHub](https://github.com/jhpyle/docassemble/blob/master/docassemble_base/docassemble/base/data/questions/examples/email-to-case-simple.yml "Open on GitHub")

[![Screenshot of email-to-case-simple example](https://docassemble.org/img/examples/email-to-case-simple.png)](https://demo.docassemble.org/interview?i=docassemble.base:data/questions/examples/email-to-case-simple.yml&reset=1 "Click to try this interview")

In order for this feature to work, your server must be configured to receive e-mails. If you are using [Docker](https://docassemble.org/docs/docker.html)
, this involves:

*   Disabling any e-mail server already running on the [Docker](https://docassemble.org/docs/docker.html)
     host.
*   Including `-p 25:25 -p 465:465` in the `docker run` statement when you start your [Docker](https://docassemble.org/docs/docker.html)
     container, so that communications to your server on [port 25](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Simple_Mail_Transfer_Protocol)
     and [port 465](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/SMTPS)
     are forwarded to the [Docker](https://docassemble.org/docs/docker.html)
     container;
*   Setting the [MX record](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/MX_record)
     for your domain to point to your server. (If you are using a [multi-server arrangement](https://docassemble.org/docs/docker.html#multi%20server%20arrangement)
    , make sure to point it specifically to the machine that operates with the `mail` container role);
*   Ensuring that the firewall rules (a/k/a “security groups”) protecting your server allow incoming connections on [port 25](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Simple_Mail_Transfer_Protocol)
     and/or [port 465](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/SMTPS)
    .
*   Setting the [`incoming mail domain`](https://docassemble.org/docs/config.html#incoming%20mail%20domain)
     directive in the [configuration](https://docassemble.org/docs/config.html)
     to the e-mail domain you want to use, unless the domain you want to use for e-mailing is the same as the domain used for your web server ([`external hostname`](https://docassemble.org/docs/config.html#external%20hostname)
    ).

See the [e-mail setup](https://docassemble.org/docs/installation.html#setup_email)
 section of the [installation](https://docassemble.org/docs/installation.html)
 instructions for details about how the e-mail receiving feature works.

Running code in the background when an e-mail arrives[¶](https://docassemble.org/docs/background.html#code%20email)

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

If you want, you can set up your interview to run [`code`](https://docassemble.org/docs/code.html)
 in the background whenever an e-mail is sent to one of your interview’s sessions. This can be helpful if an e-mail arrives at a time when the user is not using the interview, but the user may need to take action based on the e-mail.

When an e-mail arrives, **docassemble** will attempt to run a [background action](https://docassemble.org/docs/background.html#background)
 within the interview session. The name of the “action” will be [`incoming_email`](https://docassemble.org/docs/special.html#incoming_email)
 and it will have one keyword argument, `email`, which will be a [`DAEmail`](https://docassemble.org/docs/objects.html#DAEmail)
 object representing the e-mail that was received.

Below is an example that uses a [background action](https://docassemble.org/docs/background.html#background)
 to process an incoming e-mail. The [background action](https://docassemble.org/docs/background.html#background)
 simply sets the interview variable `email` to the e-mail itself (which is a [`DAEmail`](https://docassemble.org/docs/objects.html#DAEmail)
 object).

    mandatory: true
    code: |
      multi_user = True
      email = None
    ---
    mandatory: true
    code: |
      intro_seen
      if email is None:
        waiting_screen
      else:
        all_done
    ---
    question: |
      E-mailing to the interview
    subquestion: |
      When you press continue, you will be
      provided with an e-mail address to
      which you can send an email.
    field: intro_seen
    ---
    event: waiting_screen
    question: |
      E-mailing to the interview
    subquestion: |
      Send an e-mail to:
      
      > ${ interview_email() }
      
      Once the e-mail is received and processed,
      you can click refresh to see the e-mail.
    buttons:
      - Refresh: refresh
    ---
    event: all_done
    question: |
      E-mails received
    subquestion: |
      The latest e-mail was:
      
      * Subject: ${ email.subject }
      * To: ${ email.to_address }
      * From: ${ email.from_address }
      * Reply to: ${ email.reply_to }
      * Return path: ${ email.return_path }
      % if email.body_text:
      * Body text: ${ email.body_text }
      % endif
      % if email.body_html:
      * Body HTML: ${ email.body_html.slurp() }
      % endif
      % if len(email.attachment):
      * Attachment: ${ email.attachment[0] }
      % endif
    ---
    event: incoming_email
    code: |
      # Time-consuming code that processes the
      # e-mail can go here.
      background_response_action('save_email', email=action_argument('email'))
    ---
    event: save_email
    code: |
      email = action_argument('email')
      background_response()

[GitHub](https://github.com/jhpyle/docassemble/blob/master/docassemble_base/docassemble/base/data/questions/examples/email-to-case.yml "Open on GitHub")

[![Screenshot of email-to-case example](https://docassemble.org/img/examples/email-to-case.png)](https://demo.docassemble.org/interview?i=docassemble.base:data/questions/examples/email-to-case.yml&reset=1 "Click to try this interview")

Note that:

*   Your interview must set [`multi_user`](https://docassemble.org/docs/special.html#multi_user)
     to `True`. Disabling server-side encryption for the interview session is necessary because if the user is not currently using the interview when the e-mail arrives, **docassemble** has no access to the user’s password, which is the key that decrypts the interview variables. (The same limitation applies to [scheduled tasks](https://docassemble.org/docs/background.html#scheduled)
    .)
*   Your interview needs a [`code`](https://docassemble.org/docs/code.html)
     block that will respond to an [action](https://docassemble.org/docs/background.html#background)
     by the name of [`incoming_email`](https://docassemble.org/docs/special.html#incoming_email)
     that has a keyword argument `email`.
*   If it is necessary to make permanent changes to the interview variables as a result of processing the e-mail, the [`code`](https://docassemble.org/docs/code.html)
     that runs the [action](https://docassemble.org/docs/background.html#background)
     needs to use [`background_response_action()`](https://docassemble.org/docs/background.html#background_response_action)
     to call a separate [action](https://docassemble.org/docs/background.html#background)
    , the sole purpose of which is to save values to variables. When **docassemble** runs the [`incoming_email`](https://docassemble.org/docs/special.html#incoming_email)
     action, it is as though [`background_action()`](https://docassemble.org/docs/background.html#background_action)
     was called, which means that the only way to make permanent changes to the interview variables is through [`background_response_action()`](https://docassemble.org/docs/background.html#background_response_action)
    . This is helpful because the [`code`](https://docassemble.org/docs/code.html)
     that processes the incoming e-mail might take a long time to run. For example, the code might call [`ocr_file()`](https://docassemble.org/docs/functions.html#ocr_file)
     on each of the attachments.

If your interview uses [roles](https://docassemble.org/docs/roles.html)
, note that the [`incoming_email`](https://docassemble.org/docs/special.html#incoming_email)
 action will be run using the privileges and user identity of the user who originally obtained the e-mail address from [`interview_email()`](https://docassemble.org/docs/functions.html#interview_email)
. This is different from [scheduled tasks](https://docassemble.org/docs/background.html#scheduled)
, which are run using the privileges and user identity of the [cron user](https://docassemble.org/docs/background.html#cron%20user)
.

* * *

---

# Overview of the development process

[Edit](https://github.com/jhpyle/docassemble/blob/gh-pages/_docs/development.md "Edit this page on GitHub")

Overview of the development process
===================================

Contents
--------

*   [1 Workflow for a quick start](https://docassemble.org/docs/development.html#quickstart)

    *   [5.2 Managing the production upgrade process](https://docassemble.org/docs/development.html#production%20upgrade)
        
*   [6 Workflow for collaboration](https://docassemble.org/docs/development.html#collaboration)
    *   [6.1 Sharing files with Google Drive](https://docassemble.org/docs/development.html#googledrive)
        
    *   [6.2 Using version control](https://docassemble.org/docs/development.html#versioncontrol)
        
    *   [6.3 Using separate packages](https://docassemble.org/docs/development.html#separatepackages)
        
*   [7 Using private GitHub repositories](https://docassemble.org/docs/development.html#githubprivate)
    
*   [8 Editing package files in a text editor on a local machine](https://docassemble.org/docs/development.html#cli)
    
*   [9 Workflow for making changes to the core docassemble code](https://docassemble.org/docs/development.html#core)
    
*   [10 Ensuring quality](https://docassemble.org/docs/development.html#quality)
    *   [10.1 Behavior-driven development](https://docassemble.org/docs/development.html#bdd)
        
    *   [10.2 Random-input testing](https://docassemble.org/docs/development.html#random)
        
    *   [10.3 Input-output testing](https://docassemble.org/docs/development.html#prepoplate)
        
    *   [10.4 Unit testing](https://docassemble.org/docs/development.html#unittesting)
        
    *   [10.5 Using Behave](https://docassemble.org/docs/development.html#behave)
        
    *   [10.6 Improving quality with non-technical staff](https://docassemble.org/docs/development.html#nontechnical)
        

There are a variety of workflows that **docassemble** developers can use. Which one is best for you will depend on your circumstances.

Workflow for a quick start[¶](https://docassemble.org/docs/development.html#quickstart)

========================================================================================

If you are new to **docassemble**, we recommend that you start by installing **docassemble** on your personal laptop or desktop using [Docker](https://docassemble.org/docs/docker.html)
. Then you can access **docassemble** at `http://localhost`. You can log in using the default username of `admin@example.com` and the default password of `password`. After you change your password, you can use the menu in the upper-right hand corner to navigate to the User List, where you can click “Edit” next to the `admin@example.com` user and change its e-mail address to a real e-mail address. Then you can use the menu to go to the [Playground](https://docassemble.org/docs/playground.html)
, where you can try modifying the default interview, or go through the steps of the [tutorial](https://docassemble.org/docs/helloworld.html)
.

Using the [Playground](https://docassemble.org/docs/playground.html)
, you can start developing and testing interviews of your own, with the help of the [documentation](https://docassemble.org/docs.html)
 and the [examples area](https://docassemble.org/docs/playground.html#examples)
.

Workflow for speedy development[¶](https://docassemble.org/docs/development.html#speedy)

=========================================================================================

The [Playground](https://docassemble.org/docs/playground.html)
 allows you to edit your interview in the browser and then immediately test the interview by pressing “Save and Run.”

Even when you are in the middle of testing an interview, you can make changes to the interview source, reload the screen in the tab of your web browser containing your test interview, and immediately see the effect of your changes. (Note, however, that there are some circumstances when you will need to backtrack or restart your interview to see changes, for example if you change a [`mandatory`](https://docassemble.org/docs/logic.html#mandatory)
 block that your interview has already processed.)

If you are using [DOCX templates](https://docassemble.org/docs/documents.html#docx%20template%20file)
 and you are making frequent changes to your DOCX template, you may find it cumbersome to repetitively save and upload the template. You can make this process faster by configuring [Google Drive integration](https://docassemble.org/docs/playground.html#google%20drive)
. That way, you can see all of the files in your [Playground](https://docassemble.org/docs/playground.html)
 in your Google Drive folder on your hard drive, and can edit them there. Then, when you are ready to test your interview, press the “Sync” button to synchronize your [Playground](https://docassemble.org/docs/playground.html)
 with your Google Drive.

You may also wish to use [Google Drive integration](https://docassemble.org/docs/playground.html#google%20drive)
 if you have a favorite text editor that you like to use to edit text files like [YAML](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/YAML)
 interview files and [Markdown](https://daringfireball.net/projects/markdown/)
 templates.

Workflow for upgrading docassemble[¶](https://docassemble.org/docs/development.html#upgrading)

===============================================================================================

As you continue to use **docassemble**, you will probably want to take advantage of updates to the software. To see what version you are running, go to “Configuration” from the menu. You can find the current version of **docassemble** on the [PyPI page](https://pypi.python.org/pypi/docassemble.webapp/)
 or the [GitHub page](https://github.com/jhpyle/docassemble/releases)
.

Most **docassemble** software upgrades can be accomplished by going to “Package Management” from the menu and clicking the “Upgrade” button.

However, sometimes new versions of the **docassemble** software require an update to the whole system. You will see a notification on the screen if the underlying system needs an upgrade. The problem with doing an update to the underyling system is that if your user profiles and [Playground](https://docassemble.org/docs/playground.html)
 data are stored in the **docassemble** [Docker](https://docassemble.org/docs/docker.html)
 container, then removing and reinstalling the container will delete all that data.

You can back up your [Playground](https://docassemble.org/docs/playground.html)
 data by creating a [package](https://docassemble.org/docs/packages.html)
 containing all of your work product, then downloading that [package](https://docassemble.org/docs/packages.html)
 as a ZIP file. You can then [stop](https://docs.docker.com/engine/reference/commandline/stop/)
 and [remove](https://docs.docker.com/engine/reference/commandline/rm/)
 the [Docker](https://docassemble.org/docs/docker.html)
 container, [pull](https://docs.docker.com/engine/reference/commandline/pull/)
 the latest version, [run](https://docs.docker.com/engine/reference/commandline/run/)
 a new version of **docassemble**, and upload that ZIP file into the [Playground](https://docassemble.org/docs/playground.html)
 on the new system. This will restore your [Playground](https://docassemble.org/docs/playground.html)
 work product, but it will not keep your user profiles or any data from interview sessions.

There are other, less cumbersome ways to ensure that your [Playground](https://docassemble.org/docs/playground.html)
 data and other data persist through the process of removing and reinstalling the [Docker](https://docassemble.org/docs/docker.html)
 container:

1.  You can sign up with [Amazon Web Services](https://aws.amazon.com/)
     and create an [S3 bucket](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonS3/latest/dev/UsingBucket.html)
     to store the contents of your [Playground](https://docassemble.org/docs/playground.html)
    , so that the contents persist “in the cloud” after you remove the **docassemble** container. This requires figuring out how [AWS](https://aws.amazon.com/)
     and its access keys work. [AWS](https://aws.amazon.com/)
     is a bit complicated, but this is a good learning curve to climb, because [AWS](https://aws.amazon.com/)
     is used in many different contexts. A big advantage of transitioning to [S3](https://docassemble.org/docs/docker.html#persistent%20s3)
     storage is that you can continue to use your personal laptop or desktop, but when you want to transition your **docassemble** server to the cloud, the process of transitioning will be seamless.
2.  Instead of using [S3](https://docassemble.org/docs/docker.html#persistent%20s3)
    , you could use [Azure blob storage](https://docassemble.org/docs/docker.html#persistent%20azure)
    , another cloud storage service.
3.  Instead of storing your information in the cloud, you could store it in a [Docker volume](https://docassemble.org/docs/docker.html#persistent)
     on the same computer that runs your [Docker](https://docassemble.org/docs/docker.html)
     container. The disadvantage is that the data will be located in a [hard-to-find directory](https://docs.docker.com/engine/reference/commandline/volume_inspect/)
     on the computer’s hard drive, and if you want to move **docassemble** to a different server, you will need to manually move this directory.

To transition to using [S3](https://docassemble.org/docs/docker.html#persistent%20s3)
 for persistent storage, you need to create a [bucket](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonS3/latest/dev/UsingBucket.html)
 on [S3](https://docassemble.org/docs/docker.html#persistent%20s3)
, add an [`s3`](https://docassemble.org/docs/config.html#s3)
 directive to your [configuration](https://docassemble.org/docs/config.html)
 that refers to the [bucket](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonS3/latest/dev/UsingBucket.html)
, and then immediately [stop](https://docs.docker.com/engine/reference/commandline/stop/)
 the container and [start](https://docs.docker.com/engine/reference/commandline/start/)
 it again. Similarly, to transition to [Azure blob storage](https://docassemble.org/docs/docker.html#persistent%20azure)
, you need to create a container on the [Azure Portal](https://portal.azure.com/)
, add an [`azure`](https://docassemble.org/docs/config.html#azure)
 directive to your [configuration](https://docassemble.org/docs/config.html)
 that refers to the container, and then immediately [stop](https://docs.docker.com/engine/reference/commandline/stop/)
 the container and [start](https://docs.docker.com/engine/reference/commandline/start/)
 it again.

Transitioning to using a [Docker volume](https://docassemble.org/docs/docker.html#persistent)
 for persistent storage is not as seamless. Start by creating a [package](https://docassemble.org/docs/packages.html)
 in your [Playground](https://docassemble.org/docs/playground.html)
 containing all of the work you have developed so far. Then download this package as a ZIP file. This will back up your work. Then you can stop and remove the container, pull the new **docassemble** image from [Docker Hub](https://hub.docker.com/)
, and run it with the configuration necessary to use one of the above [data storage](https://docassemble.org/docs/docker.html#data%20storage)
 techniques. Then, log in to the Playground, go to the [packages folder](https://docassemble.org/docs/playground.html#packages)
, and upload the ZIP file. You will need to recreate your user accounts on the new system.

Once you set up persistent storage, all you need to do to upgrade the full system is [stop](https://docs.docker.com/engine/reference/commandline/stop/)
 your [Docker](https://docassemble.org/docs/docker.html)
 container, [remove](https://docs.docker.com/engine/reference/commandline/rm/)
 it, [pull](https://docs.docker.com/engine/reference/commandline/pull/)
 the new image from [Docker Hub](https://hub.docker.com/)
, and [run](https://docs.docker.com/engine/reference/commandline/run/)
 the new image. Your user profiles, [Playground](https://docassemble.org/docs/playground.html)
 data, and installed packages will automatically appear in the new container.

Workflow for manual testing[¶](https://docassemble.org/docs/development.html#manualtesting)

============================================================================================

Manual testing of interviews should be part of your workflow. Automated testing is also important – there is a [separate section](https://docassemble.org/docs/development.html#testing)
 on that – but you need to put yourself in your user’s shoes to see how your interview operates. People other than yourself should also try out the interviews, because they will likely use the system in a different way than you do and thereby uncover problems that you would not encounter.

If your development server is accessible over the network, you can involve testers in your interview while it is still in the [Playground](https://docassemble.org/docs/playground.html)
. Every interview in the [Playground](https://docassemble.org/docs/playground.html)
 is accessible at a hyperlink. To get this hyperlink, right-click on the “ Share” button in the [Playground](https://docassemble.org/docs/playground.html)
, copy the URL to your clipboard, then paste the URL into an e-mail to your testers.

If your development server is your desktop computer, and you access it in your browser at `http://localhost`, other users will not be able to run your interviews by going to `http://localhost`. However, if you can figure out your computer name, and if your computer’s firewall does not block access to the HTTP port (port 80), then other people should be able to access **docassemble** at a URL like `http://johnsmithcomputer` or `http://johnsmith.mycompany.local`.

If you have testers who do not have access to your local area network, you should consider putting your development server on a server on the internet. If you run **docassemble** on your desktop computer, you could configure your firewall to direct traffic from an external IP address to your desktop, but for security reasons this is probably not a good idea. It is better to put your **docassemble** server on a dedicated machine (or virtual machine) that is connected to the internet. When you do so, you should enable HTTPS so that passwords are encrypted.

If you are using a local machine for hosting but using [S3](https://docassemble.org/docs/docker.html#persistent%20s3)
 or [Azure blob storage](https://docassemble.org/docs/docker.html#persistent%20azure)
 for storage, moving from a local server to a cloud server is relatively easy because your configuration and data is already in the cloud. You just need to [stop](https://docs.docker.com/engine/reference/commandline/stop/)
 your local [Docker](https://docassemble.org/docs/docker.html)
 container and then start a [Docker](https://docassemble.org/docs/docker.html)
 container on the cloud server using environment variables that point to your persistent storage in the cloud (e.g., [`S3ENABLE`](https://docassemble.org/docs/docker.html#S3ENABLE)
, [`S3BUCKET`](https://docassemble.org/docs/docker.html#S3BUCKET)
, [`AZUREENABLE`](https://docassemble.org/docs/docker.html#AZUREENABLE)
, [`AZUREACCOUNTNAME`](https://docassemble.org/docs/docker.html#AZUREACCOUNTNAME)
, [`AZURECONTAINER`](https://docassemble.org/docs/docker.html#AZURECONTAINER)
, etc.).

If you are not using cloud storage or [Docker volume](https://docassemble.org/docs/docker.html#persistent)
s, you can move your server from your local machine to a machine in the cloud using [Docker](https://docassemble.org/docs/docker.html)
 tools. You will need to [stop](https://docs.docker.com/engine/reference/commandline/stop/)
 your container, [commit](https://docs.docker.com/engine/reference/commandline/commit/)
 your container to an image, [save](https://docs.docker.com/engine/reference/commandline/save/)
 the image as a file, move the file to the new server, [load](https://docs.docker.com/engine/reference/commandline/load/)
 the file on the new server to create the image there, and then [run](https://docs.docker.com/engine/reference/commandline/run/)
 the image.

Production environment workflow[¶](https://docassemble.org/docs/development.html#production)

=============================================================================================

Separating production from development[¶](https://docassemble.org/docs/development.html#dev)

---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

If end users are using your interviews, you will need to make sure that they are reliable, so that your users do not encounter the types of problems that tend to appear unexpectedly in a development environment.

Therefore, it is recommended that you run two servers:

1.  a development server; and
2.  a production server.

On your development server, you will make sure your interviews run as intended, and then you will put your interview into a [package](https://docassemble.org/docs/packages.html)
 and save that package somewhere: on [PyPI](https://pypi.python.org/pypi)
, on [Github](https://github.com/)
, or in a ZIP file. You will then install that [package](https://docassemble.org/docs/packages.html)
 on the production server by logging into the production server as an administrator and going to “Package Management” from the menu. Your users will access the interviews at links like `https://docassemble.example.com/interview?i=docassemble.bankruptcy:data/questions/chapter7.yml`, where `docassemble.example.com` is the address of your production server, `docassemble.bankruptcy` is the name of your [package](https://docassemble.org/docs/packages.html)
, and `chapter7.yml` is the main [YAML](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/YAML)
 file of your interview.

This way, your users will always see a “stable” version of your software. When you are actively developing an interview, you never know when a change that you make might have unanticipated side effects.

To minimize the risk that your end users will see errors, you should make sure the development server and the production server are as similar as possible. Ideally, they should both be running the same version of **docassemble**, they should both have the same [configuration](https://docassemble.org/docs/config.html)
, except for minor differences like server name, and they should both have the same set of [Python](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Python_%28programming_language%29)
 packages installed, with the same version numbers for each package. This protects against the risk that an interview will fail on your production server when it works without a problem on your development server.

For example, if you forget to specify a [Python](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Python_%28programming_language%29)
 package as a dependency in your **docassemble** [extension package](https://docassemble.org/docs/packages.html)
, your package will still work on the development server even though it will fail on the production server. Problems could also occur if your interview depends on a [configuration](https://docassemble.org/docs/config.html)
 setting that exists on your development server but not on your production server. There could be other, hard-to-predict reasons why an interview might work on one server but not on another. If you ensure that your development server is virtually identical to your production server, you will protect against these types of problems.

It is also important to separate the development server and the production server because there is a risk that the process of developing a new interview could interfere with the operation of existing interviews. A **docassemble** user who has developer privileges can run any arbitrary [Python](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Python_%28programming_language%29)
 code on the server, can install any [Python](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Python_%28programming_language%29)
 package, and can change the contents of many files on which **docassemble** depends for its stable operation. A user who has administrator privileges can edit the [configuration](https://docassemble.org/docs/config.html)
, and it is possible to edit the [configuration](https://docassemble.org/docs/config.html)
 in such a way that the system will crash.

Since a development server is often used for experimentation, it can be difficult to keep its configuration matched with that of the production server. It may be easier to use three servers:

1.  a development server where you develop interviews using the [Playground](https://docassemble.org/docs/playground.html)
    ,
2.  a production server; and
3.  a testing server which is virtually identical to the production server and exists primarily to test the installation of your interview [packages](https://docassemble.org/docs/packages.html)
     to make sure they work as intended before you install them on the production server.

It is a good idea to use the [`metadata`](https://docassemble.org/docs/initial.html#metadata)
 block or the [README area](https://docassemble.org/docs/playground.html#README)
 of your package to make a note about where the original development files are located. For convenience, you may find yourself using multiple servers for development and experimentation, and if time passes, you may forget where the authoritative version of the package lives.

Managing the production upgrade process[¶](https://docassemble.org/docs/development.html#production%20upgrade)

---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Since **docassemble** sessions can be saved and resumed later, it is possible that a user could start an interview in January, log out, and then you upgrade the software behind that interview in February, and then the user logs back in again in March.

This could lead to problems. Suppose that in the first version of your interview, you had a variable named `recieved_income`. But in the second version of the interview, you changed the name of the variable to `received_income`. If the user had already answered the question that defined `recieved_income`, then when they log in and use the second version of the interview, they may be asked the same question again, since `received_income` is not defined in their interview answers.

Or, if the user started an interview in December, and then resumed it in January, but in the meantime an applicable law changed, the interview may have made legal determinations that are now outdated and need to be reconsidered.

So if you will be upgrading your software as your users are using it, you will need to be careful about ensuring that your changes are “backwards-compatible.”

There are a variety of techniques that you can use to prevent problems caused by software updates.

1.  You could include a version number in your package. So if users start using the interview `docasemble.bankruptcy102:data/questions/controversy.yml`, you can upgrade your software by publishing `docasemble.bankruptcy103:data/questions/controversy.yml`, and the users with existing sessions will continue to use version 102.
2.  When you upgrade, you can add a [`mandatory`](https://docassemble.org/docs/logic.html#mandatory)
     code block early on in your interview that performs upgrade-related functions, like renaming the variable `recieved_income` to `received_income`. (Note that each [`mandatory`](https://docassemble.org/docs/logic.html#mandatory)
     code block must have a unique `id`. Otherwise inserting a new [`mandatory`](https://docassemble.org/docs/logic.html#mandatory)
     code block could cause logic problems for users who have existing sessions.)
3.  In every version of your interview, you can include a [`mandatory`](https://docassemble.org/docs/logic.html#mandatory)
     code block that sets a variable like `interview_version` to whatever the current interview version is. Then, if the user resumes an old session, your code can be aware of the fact that the session was started under the old version.

Workflow for collaboration[¶](https://docassemble.org/docs/development.html#collaboration)

===========================================================================================

Sharing files with Google Drive[¶](https://docassemble.org/docs/development.html#googledrive)

----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

If you are working as part of a team of developers on a single interview, you can use [Google Drive integration](https://docassemble.org/docs/playground.html#google%20drive)
 so that all members of the team share the same [Playground](https://docassemble.org/docs/playground.html)
, even though you log in under different accounts. One developer would set up [Google Drive integration](https://docassemble.org/docs/playground.html#google%20drive)
, and then share his or her “docassemble” folder with the other developers. The other developers would each create a “shortcut” from the root of their Google Drive to the shared folder. They would then set up [Google Drive integration](https://docassemble.org/docs/playground.html#google%20drive)
 and select the shared folder (the shortcut to it) as the folder to use.

If more than one developer tries to edit the same file at the same time, there will be problems; one developer’s synchronization may overwrite files another developer was editing. However, if the interview is split up into separate files, and each developer works only on designated files, this should not be a problem.

It is important that developers use different **docassemble** accounts to log into the [Playground](https://docassemble.org/docs/playground.html)
. If two web browsers use the [Playground](https://docassemble.org/docs/playground.html)
 at the same time, there is a danger that one developer’s changes could be erased by another developer’s activity.

Using version control[¶](https://docassemble.org/docs/development.html#versioncontrol)

---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

As you work on interview development, you should use version control to track your changes.

If you enable the [GitHub integration](https://docassemble.org/docs/packages.html#github)
 feature, you will have a “GitHub” button on your [packages folder](https://docassemble.org/docs/playground.html#packages)
 page. Every time you want to take a snapshot of your code, press the “GitHub” button, type a “commit message” that describes what changed in the latest snapshot, and then press “Commit.” Your changes will be “committed” to your package’s repository on [GitHub](https://github.com/)
.

You can also bring files from a package’s [GitHub](https://github.com/)
 repository into the [Playground](https://docassemble.org/docs/playground.html)
 using the [“Pull” button](https://docassemble.org/docs/playground.html#pull)
.

This enables a workflow like the following (assuming you know how to use [git](https://git-scm.com/)
):

1.  Start a [package](https://docassemble.org/docs/packages.html)
     in the [Playground](https://docassemble.org/docs/playground.html)
    .
2.  Push the [package](https://docassemble.org/docs/packages.html)
     to [GitHub](https://github.com/)
     using the “GitHub” button in the [packages folder](https://docassemble.org/docs/playground.html#packages)
    .
3.  On your computer, [clone](https://git-scm.com/docs/git-clone)
     the [GitHub](https://github.com/)
     repository and make changes to the package by editing files with a text editor, by copying files into the `data` folders, or other means.
4.  When you want to use the [Playground](https://docassemble.org/docs/playground.html)
     again for testing, [push](https://git-scm.com/docs/git-push)
     your changes to [GitHub](https://github.com/)
    , and then go into the [packages folder](https://docassemble.org/docs/playground.html#packages)
     and use the [“Pull” button](https://docassemble.org/docs/playground.html#pull)
     to bring the updated package files into the [Playground](https://docassemble.org/docs/playground.html)
    .

This also facilitates collaboration:

1.  You could do all your development in the [Playground](https://docassemble.org/docs/playground.html)
    , while committing snapshots to [GitHub](https://github.com/)
     as you go.
2.  If another person has an idea for a change to your package, he or she could open a [pull request](https://help.github.com/articles/about-pull-requests/)
     on the [GitHub](https://github.com/)
     repository for your package.
3.  If you like the changes that person made, you could [merge](https://help.github.com/articles/merging-a-pull-request/)
