However, **docassemble** does not recurse through all of the list items and object attributes of every object it stores, so it is up to you to avoid setting list items or object attributes to values that are not [pickleable](https://docs.python.org/3/library/pickle.html#what-can-be-pickled-and-unpickled)
.

> Docassemble has finished executing all code blocks marked as initial or mandatory, and finished asking all questions marked as mandatory (if any). It is a best practice to end your interview with a question that says goodbye and offers an Exit button.

If you see this message, it is because **docassemble** was able to go through all the questions of your interview and reach the end without encountering any questions to ask of the user.

Perhaps you forgot to mark any questions or code blocks as mandatory. Or perhaps you forgot to create a user-friendly question for the end of the interview.

You can prevent this error from appearing by adding a question such as the following:

    ---
    question: Goodbye, world!
    mandatory: True
    buttons
      - Exit: exit
    ---

> AssertionError: A blueprint’s name collision occurred between <flask.blueprints.Blueprint object at 0x7fab5a780850> and <flask.blueprints.Blueprint object at 0x7fab59da1b90>. Both share the same name “flask\_user”. Blueprints that are created on the fly need unique names.

If you get this error, try restarting the web server. Normally, “touch”ing the [WSGI](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Web_Server_Gateway_Interface)
 file succeeds at restarting the server, but sometimes it does not, and this error appears.

If that does not solve the problem, try reinstalling the [Flask](https://flask.pocoo.org/)
 packages. Also make sure that you do not have multiple versions of [Flask](https://flask.pocoo.org/)
 installed at the same time (e.g., one version in `/usr/lib` and another in `/usr/local/lib`).

Unexplained errors
==================

If you run into a problem where you think you did everything right, but you are getting a strange error, the problem might be that your interview uses a [variable name](https://docassemble.org/docs/fields.html#variable%20names)
 that is being used for some other purpose. See [reserved variable names](https://docassemble.org/docs/special.html#reserved)
 for a list of variable names that you cannot use. If this is the problem, the solution is simply to change the name of your variable to something else.

Get help
========

To get help from the community of developers and other users, you can submit questions to the [docassemble mailing list](https://mail.python.org/mm3/mailman3/lists/docassemble.python.org/)
 or chat in real time with other users on the [**docassemble** Slack group](https://join.slack.com/t/docassemble/shared_invite/zt-2cspzjo9j-YyE7SrLmi5muAvnPv~Bz~A)
.

Report an issue
===============

If you encounter an error that you think is a problem with **docassemble** rather than with your interview, please create an “issue” on the [issues page](https://github.com/jhpyle/docassemble/issues)
. The developers will be happy to sort out the problem.

* * *

---

# Package management

[Edit](https://github.com/jhpyle/docassemble/blob/gh-pages/_docs/packages.md "Edit this page on GitHub")

Package management
==================

Contents
--------

*   [1 How docassemble uses packages](https://docassemble.org/docs/packages.html#how)
    
*   [2 Using the Playground to make packages](https://docassemble.org/docs/packages.html#playground)
    
*   [3 Anatomy of a docassemble package](https://docassemble.org/docs/packages.html#anatomy)
    
*   [4 Dependencies](https://docassemble.org/docs/packages.html#dependencies)
    
*   [5 Installing a package](https://docassemble.org/docs/packages.html#installing)
    *   [5.1 Installing through GitHub](https://docassemble.org/docs/packages.html#github_install)
        
    *   [5.2 Installing through a .zip file](https://docassemble.org/docs/packages.html#zip_install)
        
    *   [5.3 Installing through PyPI](https://docassemble.org/docs/packages.html#pypi_install)
        
    *   [5.4 Permissions on packages](https://docassemble.org/docs/packages.html#package%20permissions)
        
*   [6 Running interviews from installed packages](https://docassemble.org/docs/packages.html#running)
    
*   [7 Updating Python packages](https://docassemble.org/docs/packages.html#updating)
    
*   [8 Publishing a package](https://docassemble.org/docs/packages.html#publishing)
    *   [8.1 Publishing on PyPI](https://docassemble.org/docs/packages.html#pypi)
        
    *   [8.2 Publishing on GitHub](https://docassemble.org/docs/packages.html#github)
        
    *   [8.3 How GitHub integration works](https://docassemble.org/docs/packages.html#github_how_works)
        
*   [9 Best practices for packaging your interviews](https://docassemble.org/docs/packages.html#bestpractices)
    
*   [10 How docassemble loads modules](https://docassemble.org/docs/packages.html#autoloading)
    

How **docassemble** uses packages[¶](https://docassemble.org/docs/packages.html#how)

=====================================================================================

**docassemble** interviews can be packaged into [Python packages](https://docs.python.org/3/tutorial/modules.html#packages)
 that bundle together:

*   [YAML](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/YAML)
     files representing interview files that start interviews, or [YAML](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/YAML)
     files that are [`include`](https://docassemble.org/docs/initial.html#include)
    d in other files;
*   Any [document templates](https://docassemble.org/docs/documents.html)
     that your interviews use;
*   Static content (images, downloadable files) that you want to include with your interview;
*   [Python modules](https://docs.python.org/3/tutorial/modules.html)
    , which include any [classes](https://docs.python.org/3/tutorial/classes.html)
     and other code that you might write to go along with your interviews; and
*   Data files ([translations](https://docassemble.org/docs/config.html#words)
    , [machine learning](https://docassemble.org/docs/ml.html)
     training data) on which your interview depends.

A [Python package](https://docs.python.org/3/tutorial/modules.html#packages)
 also contains metadata such as author information, a brief description, and a list of other [Python packages](https://docs.python.org/3/tutorial/modules.html#packages)
 on which the package depends. It also contains a “README” file, typically styled with [Markdown](https://daringfireball.net/projects/markdown/)
, which describes the package in detail.

A package containing **docassemble** interviews must be a [subpackage](https://packaging.python.org/guides/packaging-namespace-packages/)
 of the `docassemble` package. In practice, this means that the official package name with `docassemble.`; e.g., `docassemble.missourifamilylaw`. The `docassemble` package itself is just a shell (a [namespace package](https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0420/)
) that contains subpackages. These subpackages include **docassemble**’s core components (`docassemble.base`, `docassemble.webapp`) as well as user-created packages (e.g., `docassemble.missourifamilylaw`).

Using the Playground to make packages[¶](https://docassemble.org/docs/packages.html#playground)

================================================================================================

One of the features of the **docassemble** web application is the [Playground](https://docassemble.org/docs/playground.html)
, a browser-based development platform for developing and testing interviews. This is a “sandbox” in which you can make changes to an interview and immediately test the effect of those changes. You can use the [Playground](https://docassemble.org/docs/playground.html)
 to create a variety of resources (interview files, template files, static files, data files) and then [build packages](https://docassemble.org/docs/playground.html#packages)
 out particular resources.

Every interview that you run on **docassemble** is part of a package. When you are running an interview in the [Playground](https://docassemble.org/docs/playground.html)
, the package is called `docassemble.playground1` or something similar; the `1` at the end is the user ID of the person who is using the [Playground](https://docassemble.org/docs/playground.html)
.

Packages like `docassemble.playground1` should be considered temporary and should only be used for development and testing. When you want your users to actually use your interview, you should move your interview out of the [Playground](https://docassemble.org/docs/playground.html)
 and into a package with an appropriate name.

Suppose you have an interview file called `custody.yml`, which you are developing in the [Playground](https://docassemble.org/docs/playground.html)
. When you right click the “ Share” button, and copy the link URL, or you look at your browser location bar when you are testing the interview, you will see that the URL for the interview contains`?i=docassemble.playground1:custody.yml`. This means that the interview (`i`) is in the `docassemble.playground1` package and is called `custody.yml`. This link always runs the version of your interview that is current in the [Playground](https://docassemble.org/docs/playground.html)
. So if you make a change that accidentally introduces a bug, anyone who uses the interview will see an error message.

When you have actual users, you want to be able to make “upgrades” to your interviews without causing users to see error messages. You need to have a distinction between your “development” version and your “production” version. Here is a workflow for accomplishing this:

1.  Develop your `custody.yml` interview in the [Playground](https://docassemble.org/docs/playground.html)
     and keep improving it until it is ready for use by users.
2.  In the [“Packages” folder](https://docassemble.org/docs/playground.html#packages)
     of the [Playground](https://docassemble.org/docs/playground.html)
    , create a package called, e.g., `johnsmithlaw`. Give it a version number like 0.0.1. Include within the package the `custody.yml` interview file and any other resources on which `custody.yml` depends.
3.  This will create a [Python package](https://docs.python.org/3/tutorial/modules.html#packages)
     called `docassemble.johnsmithlaw`.
4.  In the [“Packages” folder](https://docassemble.org/docs/playground.html#packages)
     of the [Playground](https://docassemble.org/docs/playground.html)
    , click the “Install” button. This will install the `docassemble.johnsmithlaw` package on your server.
5.  Your users can now access your interview at a URL that ends with `/interview?i=docassemble.johnsmithlaw:data/questions/custody.yml`.
6.  Now you can continue making changes to the `custody.yml` interview in the [Playground](https://docassemble.org/docs/playground.html)
    , and even if you break something, your users will not get an error, because they will be using the installed version of the package, not the version in your [Playground](https://docassemble.org/docs/playground.html)
    .
7.  When the new version of your interview is ready, you can go to the [“Packages” folder](https://docassemble.org/docs/playground.html#packages)
     and press “Install” again.
8.  Your users will still use the same URL to access the interview (one that ends with `/interview?i=docassemble.johnsmithlaw:data/questions/custody.yml`), but now they will be using the new version of your interview.

For more information, see [how you run a **docassemble** interview](https://docassemble.org/docs/interviews.html#invocation)
, the [Playground](https://docassemble.org/docs/playground.html)
, the [packaging your interview](https://docassemble.org/docs/helloworld.html#packaging)
 section of the [hello world](https://docassemble.org/docs/helloworld.html)
 example, and the [development workflows](https://docassemble.org/docs/development.html)
 section.

From the [“Packages” folder](https://docassemble.org/docs/playground.html#packages)
, you can also press “ Download” to obtain a ZIP file of the [Python](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Python_%28programming_language%29)
 package. If you open this file, you can see what the structure of a **docassemble** extension package looks like.

Anatomy of a docassemble package[¶](https://docassemble.org/docs/packages.html#anatomy)

========================================================================================

Here is the file structure of a (fictional) **docassemble** package called `docassemble.baseball`.

    docassemble-baseball
    |-- docassemble
    |   |-- baseball
    |   |   |-- baseballstats.py
    |   |   |-- data
    |   |   |   |-- questions
    |   |   |   |   |-- baseball-questions.yml
    |   |   |   |   `-- hitters.yml
    |   |   |   |-- static
    |   |   |   |   |-- catcher.jpg
    |   |   |   |   `-- pitcher.jpg
    |   |   |   |-- sources
    |   |   |   |   `-- words.yml
    |   |   |   `-- templates
    |   |   |       `-- game-summary.md
    |   |   `-- __init__.py
    |   `-- __init__.py
    |-- LICENSE
    |-- README.md
    |-- setup.cfg
    `-- setup.py

The package is known as `docassemble.baseball` within [Python](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Python_%28programming_language%29)
 code, but the name `docassemble-baseball`, replacing the dot with a hyphen, is used in other contexts, such as when referring to a package published on the [PyPI](https://pypi.python.org/pypi)
 site, or a folder on your system that contains the source code of the package.

There are a lot of subdirectories (this is the nature of [namespace packages](https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0420/)
). There are reasons for all of these subdirectories.

1.  The top-level directory, `docassemble-baseball`, is important because a complete [Python](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Python_%28programming_language%29)
     package should be all in one directory. If you are publishing a package on [GitHub](https://github.com/)
    , this directory should be the root of the repository; `docassemble-baseball/.git` will contain the [git](https://git-scm.com/)
    \-related information for the package.
2.  Within that, the `docassemble` directory is necessary so that the package is a subpackage of `docassemble`.
3.  Within that, the `baseball` directory is necessary because when packages within the `docassemble` [namespace package](https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0420/)
     are installed on a system, [Python](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Python_%28programming_language%29)
     needs them to be in a subdirectory under a directory called `docassemble`.
4.  Within `baseball`, you have `baseballstats.py`, which contains [Python](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Python_%28programming_language%29)
     code. Files in this directory correspond with files in the [“Modules” folder](https://docassemble.org/docs/playground.html#modules)
     of the [Playground](https://docassemble.org/docs/playground.html)
    . The `__init.py__` file is necessary for declaring `baseball` to be a package; you never have to edit that file. The `__init.py__` file is mostly empty except for a `__version__` definiton, but its presence is still important.
5.  There is also a `data` directory with subdirectories `questions`, `static`, `sources`, and `templates`. These are for [interviews](https://docassemble.org/docs/interviews.html)
    , [static files](https://docassemble.org/docs/playground.html#static)
    , [data files](https://docassemble.org/docs/playground.html#sources)
    , and [document templates](https://docassemble.org/docs/documents.html)
    . The `questions` directory contains the [YAML](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/YAML)
     files that are in the main part of the [Playground](https://docassemble.org/docs/playground.html)
    .

Note that in the [Playground](https://docassemble.org/docs/playground.html)
, there is a [“Modules” folder](https://docassemble.org/docs/playground.html#modules)
 along with a [“Templates” folder](https://docassemble.org/docs/playground.html#templates)
, a [“Static” folder](https://docassemble.org/docs/playground.html#static)
, etc., but in a [Python](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Python_%28programming_language%29)
 package, there is no `docassemble/baseball/data/modules` folder, even though there is a `docassemble/baseball/data/templates` folder and a `docassemble/baseball/data/static` folder. The modules files (`.py` files) are located in a different place: directly under `docassemble/baseball`. Since the main purpose of a [Python](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Python_%28programming_language%29)
 package is to store [Python](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Python_%28programming_language%29)
 modules, modules files are the “main attraction” and everything else is just associated “data.” A [Python module](https://docs.python.org/3/tutorial/modules.html)
 that you refer to as `docassemble.baseball.baseballstats` must be a file `baseballstats.py` that is located in the subdirectory `docassemble/baseball/`. (This is how [Python](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Python_%28programming_language%29)
 works.)

When installed on the server, the interview `hitters.yml` can be run by going to a link like `https://example.com/interview?i=docassemble.baseball:data/questions/hitters.yml`.

In your own interviews, you can include resources from this package by writing things like the following:

    ---
    include:
      - docassemble.baseball:baseball-questions.yml
    ---
    yesno: person_is_a_catcher
    question: |
      Did the person look like this?
    subquestion: |
      [FILE docassemble.baseball:catcher.jpg]
    ---
    question: |
      Here is how the game went down.
    sets: user_given_summary
    attachment:
      - name: Summary of ${ game }
        filename: game_summary
        content file: docassemble.baseball:game-summary.md
    ---
    modules:
      - docassemble.baseball.baseballstats

The first block uses [`include`](https://docassemble.org/docs/initial.html#include)
 to incorporate by reference a [YAML](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/YAML)
 interview file located in the `data/questions` directory of the package.

The second block uses a [file reference](https://docassemble.org/docs/markup.html#inserting%20images)
 to refer to an image file in the `data/static` directory of the package.

The third block uses [`content file`](https://docassemble.org/docs/documents.html#content%20file)
 within an [`attachment`](https://docassemble.org/docs/documents.html#attachment)
 to refer to a [Markdown](https://daringfireball.net/projects/markdown/)
 file in the `data/templates` directory of the package.

The fourth block uses [`modules`](https://docassemble.org/docs/initial.html#modules)
 to import [Python](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Python_%28programming_language%29)
 names from the `baseballstats.py` file.

Since a [Python](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Python_%28programming_language%29)
 package is just a collection of files in a particular structure, you can maintain your **docassemble** extension packages “offline” (outside of the [Playground](https://docassemble.org/docs/playground.html)
) if you want. This allows you to expand the directory structure beyond what the [Playground](https://docassemble.org/docs/playground.html)
 supports.

Dependencies[¶](https://docassemble.org/docs/packages.html#dependencies)

=========================================================================

If your package uses code from other [Python](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Python_%28programming_language%29)
 packages that are not distributed with the standard **docassemble** installation, you will need to indicate that these packages are “dependencies” of your package.

This will ensure that if you share your package with someone else and they install it on their system, the packages that your package needs will be automatically installed. Otherwise, that person will get errors when they try to use your package.

If you maintain your package in the [Packages area](https://docassemble.org/docs/playground.html#packages)
 of the [Playground](https://docassemble.org/docs/playground.html)
, you can indicate the dependencies of your package by selecting them from a multi-select list. (If you the Python package you need is not listed, you need to install it on your system using “Package Management” on the menu.)

If you maintain your package off-line, you will need to edit the `setup.py` file and change the line near the end that begins with `install_requires`. This refers to a list of [Python](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Python_%28programming_language%29)
 packages. For example:

    install_requires=['docassemble.helloworld', 'kombu'],

This line indicates that the package relies on the **docassemble** extension package `docassemble.helloworld`, as well as the [Python](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Python_%28programming_language%29)
 package `kombu`. When someone tries to install `docassemble.baseball` on their system, `docassemble.helloworld` and `kombu` will be installed first, and any packages that these packages depend on will also be installed.

Note that if your package depends on a package that exists on [GitHub](https://github.com/)
 but not on [PyPI](https://pypi.python.org/pypi)
, you will also need to add an extra line so that the system knows where to find the package. For example, if `docassemble.helloworld` did not exist on [PyPI](https://pypi.python.org/pypi)
, you would need to include:

    install_requires=['docassemble.helloworld @ https://github.com/jhpyle/docassemble-helloworld/archive/refs/tags/v1.0.zip', 'kombu'],

Different dependency situations might require a more advanced understanding of [pip’s `install_requires` dependency specifiers](https://packaging.python.org/en/latest/specifications/dependency-specifiers/#dependency-specifiers)
.

The [Packages area](https://docassemble.org/docs/playground.html#packages)
 of the Playground is capable of generating a rudimentary `setup.py` file that you can start with, but if you need to manually modify your `setup.py` file, the [Packages area](https://docassemble.org/docs/playground.html#packages)
 will not be able to generate a `setup.py` containing your modifications. You can still install your package into the Playground (provided it does not use subdirectories under folders like `data/static` and `data/templates`), but the [Packages area](https://docassemble.org/docs/playground.html#packages)
 only knows how to generate `install_requires` lines that list packages available on [PyPI](https://pypi.python.org/pypi)
.

Installing a package[¶](https://docassemble.org/docs/packages.html#installing)

===============================================================================

You can install a **docassemble** extension package, or any other [Python](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Python_%28programming_language%29)
 package, using the **docassemble** web application.

From the menu, go to “Package Management.”

**docassemble** installs packages using the [pip](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pip_%28package_manager%29)
 package manager. This installation process may take a long time. A log of the output of [pip](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pip_%28package_manager%29)
 will be shown when the installation is complete. The server will restart so that any old versions of the package that are still in memory will be refreshed.

Installing through GitHub[¶](https://docassemble.org/docs/packages.html#github_install)

----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

One way to install [Python](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Python_%28programming_language%29)
 packages on a server is through [GitHub](https://github.com/)
.

1.  Find the [GitHub](https://github.com/)
     URL of the package you want to install. This is in the location bar when you are looking at a [GitHub](https://github.com/)
     repository. For example, the [GitHub](https://github.com/)
     URL of the `docassemble-baseball` package may be `https://github.com/jhpyle/docassemble-baseball`. (No such package actually exists.)
2.  In the **docassemble** web app, go to Package Management.
3.  Enter `https://github.com/jhpyle/docassemble-baseball` into the “GitHub URL” field.
4.  The “GitHub Branch” field will be updated with the default branch of the repository (usually `master`). You can select another branch if you wish to install a different branch of the repository.
5.  Click “Update.”

![GitHub Install](https://docassemble.org/img/github-install.png)

If you want to install from a private [Github](https://github.com/)
 repository, you will need a URL that points to your repository and that includes authentication information. To make such a URL, you will need a “personal access token” from [Github](https://github.com/)
. If you do not already have a personal access token, log into [Github](https://github.com/)
, go to your “Settings,” go to “Developer settings,” and go to the “Personal access tokens” tab. Click “Generate new token.” You can set the “Token description” to whatever you like (e.g. “for installing on docassemble”). Check the “repo” checkbox, so that all of the capabilities under “repo” are selected. Then click “Generate token.” Copy the “personal access token” and keep it in a safe place.

If your token is `e8cc02bec7061de98ba4851263638d7483f63d41`, your GitHub username is `johnsmith`, and your package is called `docassemble-missouri-familylaw`, then the GitHub URL for your private repository will be:

    https://e8cc02bec7061de98ba4851263638d7483f63d41:x-oauth-basic@github.com/johnsmith/docassemble-missouri-familylaw

You can enter this into the “GitHub URL” field.

Installing through a .zip file[¶](https://docassemble.org/docs/packages.html#zip_install)

------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

You can also install [Python](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Python_%28programming_language%29)
 packages from ZIP files. For example, if you have a package `docassemble-baseball`, the ZIP file will be called `docassemble-baseball.zip`. It will contain a single directory called `docassemble-baseball`, which in turn contains `setup.py`, a subdirectory called `docassemble`, and other files.

1.  In the **docassemble** web app, go back to Package Management.
2.  Under “Zip File,” upload the `.zip` file you want to install.
3.  Click “Update.”

![Zip Install](https://docassemble.org/img/zip-install.png)

Installing through PyPI[¶](https://docassemble.org/docs/packages.html#pypi_install)

------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

You can also install [Python](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Python_%28programming_language%29)
 packages from [PyPI](https://pypi.python.org/pypi)
. [PyPI](https://pypi.python.org/pypi)
 is the central repository for [Python](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Python_%28programming_language%29)
 software. Anyone can register on [PyPI](https://pypi.python.org/pypi)
 and upload software to it. For example, if you want to install the `docassemble-baseball` package:

1.  Make sure the `docassemble-baseball` package does not already exist on [PyPI](https://pypi.python.org/pypi)
     (note: it doesn’t; it is just a fictional package).
2.  In the **docassemble** web app, go to Package Management.
3.  Type `docassemble.baseball` into the “Package on PyPI” field.
4.  Click “Update.”

![PyPI Install](https://docassemble.org/img/pypi-install.png)

Once a version of a package is installed on [PyPI](https://pypi.python.org/pypi)
, it exists there permanently. When you install a version of a package on [PyPI](https://pypi.python.org/pypi)
, the version will automatically increment.

Permissions on packages[¶](https://docassemble.org/docs/packages.html#package%20permissions)

---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

The “Package Management” system remembers which user installed which package. Thus, if developer Fred installs a package called `docassemble.guardianship`, and developer George tries to install a package called `docassemble.guardianship`, George will get an error. These permissions do not apply to package dependencies, however.

A user with `admin` privileges can install or uninstall any package.

The purpose of the permission system is to facilitate the use of a single server by a team of developers by implementing some minor safeguards that prevent developers from interfering with each others’ work. It is not the case that each developer works in an insulated “sandbox.” All interviews on the system run with the same system [UID](http://www.linfo.org/uid.html)
 (`www-data`).

Running interviews from installed packages[¶](https://docassemble.org/docs/packages.html#running)

==================================================================================================

Once a **docassemble** extension package is installed, you can start using its interviews. For example, if you installed `docassemble.baseball`, and there was an interview file in that package called `questions.yml`, you would point your browser to `http://localhost/interview?i=docassemble.baseball:data/questions/questions.yml` (substituting the actual domain and base URL of your **docassemble** site). Note that a URL like this is different from the URL you see when you are running an interview in the [Playground](https://docassemble.org/docs/playground.html)
 ([see above](https://docassemble.org/docs/packages.html#playground)
).

For more information about starting **docassemble** interviews, see [how you run a **docassemble** interview](https://docassemble.org/docs/interviews.html#invocation)
.

Updating Python packages[¶](https://docassemble.org/docs/packages.html#updating)

=================================================================================

To upgrade a package that you installed from a [GitHub](https://github.com/)
 URL or from [PyPI](https://pypi.python.org/pypi)
, you can press the “Update” button next to the package name on the “Package Management” screen. You will only see these Update buttons if you are an administrator or if you are the person who caused the packages to be installed. Also, the “Update” buttons will not appear if the package was installed using a ZIP file.

Publishing a package[¶](https://docassemble.org/docs/packages.html#publishing)

===============================================================================

Publishing on PyPI[¶](https://docassemble.org/docs/packages.html#pypi)

-----------------------------------------------------------------------

The best place to publish a **docassemble** extension packages is on [PyPI](https://pypi.python.org/pypi)
, the central repository for [Python](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Python_%28programming_language%29)
 software.

In order to publish to [PyPI](https://pypi.python.org/pypi)
, you will first need to create an account on [PyPI](https://pypi.python.org/pypi)
. You will need to choose a username and password and verify your e-mail address.

Then, go to “Configuration” on the menu and enable the PyPI publishing feature in **docassemble** [configuration](https://docassemble.org/docs/config.html)
 like so:

    pypi: True

After you save the [configuration](https://docassemble.org/docs/config.html)
, go to “Profile” on the menu and fill in “PyPI Username” and “PyPI Password” with the username and password you obtained from [PyPI](https://pypi.python.org/pypi)
.

Next, go to the [“Packages” folder](https://docassemble.org/docs/playground.html#packages)
 of the **docassemble** [Playground](https://docassemble.org/docs/playground.html)
 and open the package you want to publish (e.g., `docassemble-baseball`).

Press the [PyPI](javascript:return(0))
 button to publish the package to [PyPI](https://pypi.python.org/pypi)
.

If your package already exists on [PyPI](https://pypi.python.org/pypi)
, then pressing the Publish button will increment the version of your package. This is necessary because you cannot overwrite files that already exist on [PyPI](https://pypi.python.org/pypi)
.

When the publishing is done, you will see an informational message with the output of the uploading commands. Check this message to see if there are any errors.

If the publishing was successful, then at the bottom of the page describing your package, you should see a message that the package now exists on [PyPI](https://pypi.python.org/pypi)
.

![PyPI Published](https://docassemble.org/img/playground-packages-pypi-published.png)

You can click on the link to see what the package looks like on [PyPI](https://pypi.python.org/pypi)
.

![PyPi Page](https://docassemble.org/img/playground-packages-on-pypi.png)

Now, on the **docassemble** menu (of this server or another server), you can go to Package Management and [install the package](https://docassemble.org/docs/packages.html#pypi_install)
 by typing in “docassemble.baseball” into the “Package on PyPI” field.

![PyPi Install](https://docassemble.org/img/pypi-install.png)

Publishing on GitHub[¶](https://docassemble.org/docs/packages.html#github)

---------------------------------------------------------------------------

You can publish your package on [GitHub](https://github.com/)
 in addition to (or instead of) publishing it on [PyPI](https://pypi.python.org/pypi)
. (Publishing on both sites is recommended. [PyPI](https://pypi.python.org/pypi)
 is the simplest and safest way to distribute [Python](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Python_%28programming_language%29)
 packages, while [GitHub](https://github.com/)
 is a version control system with many features for facilitating sharing and collaboration.)

To configure integration with [GitHub](https://github.com/)
, follow the steps in the [setting up GitHub integration](https://docassemble.org/docs/installation.html#github)
 section, and edit the [GitHub section of the configuration](https://docassemble.org/docs/config.html#github)
.

When that configuration is done, each user who is a developer will need to connect their [GitHub](https://github.com/)
 accounts with their accounts on your **docassemble** server. From the menu, the user should go to “Profile,” click “GitHub integration,” and follow the instructions. If the user is not currently logged in to [GitHub](https://github.com/)
 in the same browser, [GitHub](https://github.com/)
 will ask for log in information. (Users without [GitHub](https://github.com/)
 accounts can [create one](https://github.com/join)
.) Users will need to give consent to giving the **docassemble** server to have privileges of making changes to repositories and SSH keys within the [GitHub](https://github.com/)
 account.

(Note: it is not possible to connect more than one **docassemble** account on a single **docassemble** server with the same [GitHub](https://github.com/)
 account. However, it is possible to connect accounts on multiple servers with the same [GitHub](https://github.com/)
 account, so long as the [`appname`](https://docassemble.org/docs/config.html#appname)
 on each **docassemble** server is different.)

To publish a package on [GitHub](https://github.com/)
, go to the [Packages area](https://docassemble.org/docs/playground.html#packages)
 of the [Playground](https://docassemble.org/docs/playground.html)
 and press the [GitHub](javascript:return(0))
 button. You will be asked for a “commit message.” This is a brief, one-line message that describes the changes made to your package since the last time you “committed” changes. Each “commit” is like a snapshot, and the history of “commit” messages is a record of the development of your project.

![Commit](https://docassemble.org/img/playground-packages-github-commit.png)

When you press the “Commit” button after writing the commit message, your package will be “pushed” to a [GitHub](https://github.com/)
 repository in your account. If a repository does not already exist on [GitHub](https://github.com/)
 with the name of your package, a new repository will be created.

![GitHub published](https://docassemble.org/img/playground-packages-github-published.png)

You can follow the hyperlink to your package’s page on [GitHub](https://github.com/)
.

![GitHub repository](https://docassemble.org/img/playground-packages-github-repository.png)

After your first commit, [GitHub](https://github.com/)
 reports that there have been two commits; this is because the initial creation of the repository caused a commit (containing a `LICENSE` file only) and then the addition of the files of your package caused a second commit.

Once your package is on [GitHub](https://github.com/)
, then on the **docassemble** menu, you can go to Package Management and [install the package](https://docassemble.org/docs/packages.html#github_install)
 using its [GitHub](https://github.com/)
 URL.

![GitHub Install](https://docassemble.org/img/github-install.png)

How GitHub integration works[¶](https://docassemble.org/docs/packages.html#github_how_works)

---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

When you commit changes to [GitHub](https://github.com/)
, **docassemble** will first decide whether to create a new repository or commit the changes to an existing repository. It will use the [GitHub](https://github.com/)
 API to look for a repository that has the same name as your package. That is, if your package is named `familylaw`, and your GitHub username is `jsmith`, it will look for the repository `https://github.com/jsmith/docassemble-familylaw`. If that repository does not exist, **docassemble** will look through the repositories to which you are permitted to commit (`https://api.github.com/user/repos`). If no repository called `docassemble-familylaw` is found, it will search through [GitHub](https://github.com/)
 “organizations” of which you are a member (`https://api.github.com/user/orgs`). For example, if you are a member of an organization called `abcinc`, it will look for a repository called `https://github.com/abcinc/docassemble-familylaw`.

When [GitHub](https://github.com/)
 integration is enabled, then at the bottom of the Packages screen, you will see a box that says “This package is not yet published on your GitHub account” or “This package is published on GitHub.” If it says “This package is not yet published on your GitHub account,” this means that **docassemble** was not able to find a package called `docassemble-familylaw` among your repositories or repositories to which you have access. Thus, when you press the Commit button, a new repository `https://github.com/jsmith/docassemble-familylaw` will be created.

If you expect to be able to push changes to a repository in another account, but **docassemble** is reporting “This package is published on GitHub,” make sure that you actually have access to that repository. One known issue is that if a repository belongs to an organization, and the administrator of that organization adds you as a collaborator on that repository, [GitHub](https://github.com/)
 will not list that repository in its response to `https://api.github.com/user/orgs`. Thus, if you want to be able to make commits to a repository owned by an organization, ask the administrator of the organization to make you a member of the organization.

When you press the “Commit” button, the Git actions that are performed are as follows:

*   `git clone` is used to copy the files from GitHub to the server.
*   If a commit or pull had previously been performed using the Playground, `git checkout` is used to switch to the commit that was current as of the time the commit or pull took place.
*   The Playground files that are selected to be part of the package are copied on top of the cloned files.
*   A new branch is created and committed.
*   The new branch is merged into the master branch of the repository and pushed.

This means that the changes you have made in the Playground will not overwrite changes that have been made to the remote repository since the last time you did a “pull” or “commit” in the Playground.

This means that if you delete a file in your Playground, and that file is already part of a [GitHub](https://github.com/)
 repository, then the next time you do a “pull” or “commit,” that file will be recreated in your Playground. There is no feature in docassemble that implements `git rm`. Thus, if you want to delete such a file, delete it both on GitHub and in the Playground.

Best practices for packaging your interviews[¶](https://docassemble.org/docs/packages.html#bestpractices)

==========================================================================================================

It is a good practice to bundle related interviews in a single package. Think about making it easy for other people to install your packages on their system and make use of your [questions](https://docassemble.org/docs/questions.html)
 and [code](https://docassemble.org/docs/code.html)
.

It is also a good practice to separate your interview into at least three files, separately containing:

*   [mandatory](https://docassemble.org/docs/logic.html#mandatory)
     and [initial](https://docassemble.org/docs/logic.html#initial)
     code
*   [initial blocks](https://docassemble.org/docs/logic.html#mandatory)
    
*   [question](https://docassemble.org/docs/questions.html#question)
     and [code](https://docassemble.org/docs/code.html)
     blocks

This way, other people can take advantage of your work product in interviews that might have a very different purpose.

How **docassemble** loads modules[¶](https://docassemble.org/docs/packages.html#autoloading)

=============================================================================================

During the **docassemble** web app startup process, the packages in the `docassemble` namespace are scanned and modules are `import`ed if they contain any class definitions or if the `update_language_function()` function is called. The early loading of class definitions helps to prevent problems when unpickling data from the SQL database. The `update_language_function()` has a global effect, and it is important that **docassemble**’s linguistic behavior is uniform over time and does not vary depending on which interviews the server has used. This also means that you have to be careful about which packages you install on your server – even a package you never use could have an effect on the way your server operates.

If you want to disable the auto-loading of a module, put this line near the top of the .py file (before any class definitions or references to `update_language_function()`):

    # do not pre-load

If this line is present, then the server will not pre-load the module file.

* * *

---

# Overview of docassemble administration

[Edit](https://github.com/jhpyle/docassemble/blob/gh-pages/_docs/admin.md "Edit this page on GitHub")

Overview of docassemble administration
======================================

Contents
--------

*   [1 Menu items for users with special privileges](https://docassemble.org/docs/admin.html#specialmenu)
    *   [1.1 Monitor](https://docassemble.org/docs/admin.html#monitor)
        
    *   [1.2 Train](https://docassemble.org/docs/admin.html#train)
        
    *   [1.3 Package Management](https://docassemble.org/docs/admin.html#package%20management)
        
    *   [1.4 Logs](https://docassemble.org/docs/admin.html#logs)
        
    *   [1.5 Playground](https://docassemble.org/docs/admin.html#playground)
        
    *   [1.6 Utilities](https://docassemble.org/docs/admin.html#utilities)
        
    *   [1.7 User List](https://docassemble.org/docs/admin.html#user%20list)
        
    *   [1.8 Configuration](https://docassemble.org/docs/admin.html#configuration)
        
*   [2 Menu items for all logged-in users](https://docassemble.org/docs/admin.html#menu)
    *   [2.1 Available Interviews](https://docassemble.org/docs/admin.html#available%20interviews)
        
    *   [2.2 My Interviews](https://docassemble.org/docs/admin.html#my%20interviews)
        
    *   [2.3 Profile](https://docassemble.org/docs/admin.html#profile)
        
    *   [2.4 Sign Out](https://docassemble.org/docs/admin.html#sign%20out)
        
*   [3 Endpoints for monitoring](https://docassemble.org/docs/admin.html#endpoints)
    
*   [4 Troubleshooting](https://docassemble.org/docs/admin.html#troubleshooting)
    

When you log in to a **docassemble** server, the options available to you in the menu in the upper-right corner will depend on what [privileges](https://docassemble.org/docs/users.html)
 have been enabled for your account.

There are four special privileges built in to **docassemble**.

*   `admin`: for people who need complete control over the server.
*   `developer`: for people who need to use the server to develop, test, and debug interviews, but do not need to be able to access user data.
*   `advocate`: for people who need to be able to use the [Monitor](https://docassemble.org/docs/livehelp.html#monitor)
     to provide remote assistance to users, or use interviews or APIs that provide access to user data.
*   `trainer`: for people who need to be able to [train machine learning models](https://docassemble.org/docs/ml.html#train)
    .

By default, when a new user account is created, the user is given only the privilege of `user`. This is the “lowest” level of privilege; a user without the `user` privilege can do everything a user with `user` privileges can do.

A user with privileges of `admin` can control the privileges of other users using the [User List](https://docassemble.org/docs/admin.html#user%20list)
 screen.

Menu items for users with special privileges[¶](https://docassemble.org/docs/admin.html#specialmenu)

=====================================================================================================

Monitor[¶](https://docassemble.org/docs/admin.html#monitor)

------------------------------------------------------------

The [Monitor](https://docassemble.org/docs/livehelp.html#monitor)
 is a feature of **docassemble**’s [Live Help system](https://docassemble.org/docs/livehelp.html)
 that allows the user to chat with or share a screen with an active user. Users with privileges of `admin` or `advocate` can access the [Monitor](https://docassemble.org/docs/livehelp.html#monitor)
.

Train[¶](https://docassemble.org/docs/admin.html#train)

--------------------------------------------------------

The “Train” menu item allows the user to [train machine learning models](https://docassemble.org/docs/ml.html#train)
. This is part of **docassemble**’s [machine learning system](https://docassemble.org/docs/ml.html)
. Users with privileges of `admin` or `trainer` can access it.

Package Management[¶](https://docassemble.org/docs/admin.html#package%20management)

------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

The “Package Management” screen allows the user to install, update, or uninstall [Python packages](https://www.learnpython.org/en/Modules_and_Packages)
 that exist on the server. It has three parts:

*   Upgrade docassemble
*   Install or update a package
*   Update or uninstall an existing package

[Python packages](https://www.learnpython.org/en/Modules_and_Packages)
 play an important role in **docassemble**. The core functionality of **docassemble** resides in two [Python packages](https://www.learnpython.org/en/Modules_and_Packages)
: `docassemble.webapp` and `docassemble.base`. (There is also a “namespace” package called `docassemble`, which is necessary but contains no substantive code.) [Python packages](https://www.learnpython.org/en/Modules_and_Packages)
 are the mechanism by which **docassemble** interviews are published and shared. Also, if any of your interviews needs to perform a non-standard function, you can install a third-party [Python](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Python_%28programming_language%29)
 package that provides that functionality. For example, suppose you wanted your interview to integrate with your organization’s [Slack](https://slack.com/)
 server. You could install the [Python](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Python_%28programming_language%29)
 package called [`slackclient`](https://pypi.org/project/slackclient/)
, and then use an [`imports`](https://docassemble.org/docs/initial.html#imports)
 block to incorporate the functionality of [`slackclient`](https://pypi.org/project/slackclient/)
 into your interview.

The “Package Management” screen is where users with `admin` or `developer` privileges can manage [Python](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Python_%28programming_language%29)
 packages. The screen effectively serves as a front end to [Python](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Python_%28programming_language%29)
’s [pip](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pip_%28package_manager%29)
 utility.

### Upgrade docassemble[¶](https://docassemble.org/docs/admin.html#upgrade)

Under the “Upgrade docassemble” part, you can see what version of **docassemble** you are running. If a new version of **docassemble** is available, it will also show you current version. You can press the “Upgrade” button to upgrade to the latest version of **docassemble** from [PyPI](https://pypi.python.org/pypi)
.

This updates only **docassemble**’s [Python](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Python_%28programming_language%29)
 packages. It does not upgrade any of the system files that **docassemble** uses. A few times a year, you may see a notification on the screen saying:

> A new docassemble system version is available. If you are using Docker, install a new Docker image.

This means that non-Python files in the **docassemble** system have been upgraded. If you are using [Docker](https://docassemble.org/docs/docker.html)
 with [persistent storage](https://docassemble.org/docs/docker.html#data%20storage)
, then you should then [upgrade your container](https://docassemble.org/docs/docker.html#upgrading)
.

### Install or update a package[¶](https://docassemble.org/docs/admin.html#install)

Under the “Install or update a package” part, you can install a new [Python](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Python_%28programming_language%29)
 package on the system.

There are three ways you can provide a package to **docassemble**:

*   GitHub
*   ZIP file
*   PyPI

[PyPI](https://pypi.python.org/pypi)
 is the standard place on the internet where [Python](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Python_%28programming_language%29)
 packages are published. However, some Python packages are hosted directly on [GitHub](https://github.com/)
, and some are distributed as ZIP files.

When you provide a URL to a [GitHub](https://github.com/)
 repository, you can choose which branch of that repository you wish to install.

The [Playground](https://docassemble.org/docs/playground.html#packages)
 allows you to package one or more **docassemble** interviews as a [Python](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Python_%28programming_language%29)
 package and publish it on [GitHub](https://github.com/)
, publish it on [PyPI](https://pypi.python.org/pypi)
, or save it as a ZIP file. Thus, you can use the [Playground](https://docassemble.org/docs/playground.html#packages)
 on a development server to create and publish a package, and then use “Package Management” on a production server to install the package.

If you do not want your package to be available for anyone in the world to read, you can store it in a private repository on [GitHub](https://github.com/)
, and then use [GitHub](https://github.com/)
 to obtain a special URL for the repository that embeds an “OAuth” authentication code. These URLs can be used in the “Github URL” field of “Package Management.”

### Update or uninstall an existing package[¶](https://docassemble.org/docs/admin.html#update)

In this part of the “Package Management” screen, you can see a list of all of the [Python](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Python_%28programming_language%29)
 packages that are installed on your system already.

You can click “Uninstall” to remove a package, and you can click “Update” to update a package to the latest version. However, be careful; some of the listed packages are dependencies for other packages, and those other packages may depend on a specific older version of the package. Thus, you could break your system if you click “Update” on the wrong package.

However, if the only packages you “Update” or “Uninstall” are **docassemble** extension packages, or dependency packages that you installed originally, then there is little risk that you will cause a problem with your system.

Logs[¶](https://docassemble.org/docs/admin.html#logs)

------------------------------------------------------

The “Logs” screen provides a web interface to some of the log files on the underlying system. In a [multi-server arrangement](https://docassemble.org/docs/docker.html#multi%20server%20arrangement)
, log messages are consolidated from all servers, and “Logs” provides a way to see all of them together.

The log messages shown in the box on the screen are just the tail end of the actual log file. To see the complete log file, you will need to download it.

Note that the log files are managed by [logrotate](https://manpages.debian.org/stretch/logrotate/logrotate.8.en.html)
. Generally, the current log file for the `docassemble.log` file is simply `docassemble.log`, and the next most recent is `docassemble.log.1`, and then `docassemble.log.2`, etc. As a result, the log messages you want to view may not be in `docassemble.log`, but could be in `docassemble.log.1`. Also, given the way log rotation interacts with open file handles, note that the very most recent log messages may be in `docassemble.log.1` rather than `docassemble.log`.

Here is a summary of what the different log files represent.

*   `access.log` contains entries for every request made to the web server.
*   `docassemble.log` contains log messages and most errors generated by the web application.
*   `error.log` contains error messages generated by the web server. Most error messages in **docassemble** are trapped before they can be raised by the web server, so `docassemble.log` should generally be the first place you look for an error. If you get an “500 Internal Server Error” in the browser, however, the error message is likely in `error.log`.
*   `worker.log` contains log messages and error messages generated by [background tasks](https://docassemble.org/docs/background.html#background)
    .

In unusual situations, you may need to review other log files. For more information about how to find other log files, see the [troubleshooting](https://docassemble.org/docs/docker.html#troubleshooting)
 section.

Playground[¶](https://docassemble.org/docs/admin.html#playground)

------------------------------------------------------------------

The [Playground](https://docassemble.org/docs/playground.html#packages)
 is an area where users who have privileges of `admin` or `developer` can develop and test interviews using the web browser. Interviews can also be developed “off-line” by assembling a [package](https://docassemble.org/docs/packages.html)
 containing [YAML](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/YAML)
 files and other files in the appropriate locations.

Documentation for the [Playground](https://docassemble.org/docs/playground.html#packages)
 can be found in the [Playground section](https://docassemble.org/docs/playground.html#packages)
.

Utilities[¶](https://docassemble.org/docs/admin.html#utilities)

----------------------------------------------------------------

The “Utilities” screen provides two miscellaneous services that do not fit in anywhere else. They are available to users with `admin` or `developer` privileges.

### Get list of fields from PDF/DOCX template[¶](https://docassemble.org/docs/admin.html#pdf%20fields)

If you are assembling a [document](https://docassemble.org/docs/documents.html)
 using the [`pdf attachment file`](https://docassemble.org/docs/documents.html#pdf%20attachment%20file)
 or the [`docx attachment file`](https://docassemble.org/docs/documents.html#docx%20attachment%20file)
 options, you can use this utility to generate a first draft of a [`question`](https://docassemble.org/docs/questions.html#question)
 that fills in all of the fields referenced in the PDF or Word file.

If you have a Word file that you are referencing with [`docx attachment file`](https://docassemble.org/docs/documents.html#docx%20attachment%20file)
, you probably do not need to use this utility, because your template can be populated directly using variables in your interview. Populating the template with a [list of fields](https://docassemble.org/docs/documents.html#particfields)
 is optional.

This utility is primarily useful when you are using the [`pdf attachment file`](https://docassemble.org/docs/documents.html#pdf%20attachment%20file)
 option. This option requires you to provide a dictionary of [`fields`](https://docassemble.org/docs/documents.html#pdf%20attachment%20file)
 (or [`field code`](https://docassemble.org/docs/documents.html#template%20code)
, or [`field variables`](https://docassemble.org/docs/documents.html#template%20code)
) in which the keys are the names of fields in the underlying PDF file. This utility provides a handy way of obtaining the list of fields.

If your PDF field names use non-[ASCII](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/ASCII)
 characters such as characters with accent marks, the various software packages that **docassemble** uses to fill PDF forms may not be able to fill those fields properly. If you find that some fields are being filled and others are not, check to see if the fields that are not being filled have accented characters. In the PDF template, try replacing the accented characters with non-accented characters, and see if you get a better result.

### Translate system phrases into another language[¶](https://docassemble.org/docs/admin.html#translate)

The second utility on the “Utilities” screen is helpful if your site does not use English, or you are developing [multi-lingual interviews](https://docassemble.org/docs/language.html)
.

There are many words and phrases that appear to the user on various screens and in various circumstances. By default, **docassemble** only provides these words and phrases in English. However, **docassemble** allows you to map each English word or phrase to a word or phrase in another language. For more information about how this feature works, see the [`words`](https://docassemble.org/docs/config.html#words)
 directive.

This utility will produce a draft [YAML](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/YAML)
, [XLSX](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Office_Open_XML)
, or [XLIFF](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/XLIFF)
 file that you can then edit, store within a [package](https://docassemble.org/docs/packages.html)
, and reference from the [`words`](https://docassemble.org/docs/config.html#words)
 directive of your [Configuration](https://docassemble.org/docs/config.html)
.

To use this utility, provide a language in the form of a lowercase [ISO-639-1](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_ISO_639-1_codes)
 code (e.g., `fr` for French) and press “Translate.” If you choose “YAML” as the file format, you will be provided with a text box containing a [YAML](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/YAML)
 data structure that you can copy and paste into a text file. If you choose “XLSX,” “XLIFF 1.2,” or “XLIFF 2.0”, you will be provided with a file download.

If you have configured a Google `api key` inside your [`google`](https://docassemble.org/docs/config.html#google)
 directive in the [Configuration](https://docassemble.org/docs/config.html)
, and you have enabled the [Google Cloud Translation API](https://cloud.google.com/translate/)
 inside your [Google Developers Console](https://console.developers.google.com/)
, then **docassemble** will run each word or phrase through the [Google Cloud Translation API](https://cloud.google.com/translate/)
.

If you have already configured a [`words`](https://docassemble.org/docs/config.html#words)
 directive, this utility will pass through all of the words defined in existing [`words`](https://docassemble.org/docs/config.html#words)
 files, and will only try to translate phrases that do not already exist in existing [`words`](https://docassemble.org/docs/config.html#words)
 files.

### Download an interview phrase translation file[¶](https://docassemble.org/docs/admin.html#translation%20file)

The third utility on the “Utilities” screen is helpful if you are developing [multi-lingual interviews](https://docassemble.org/docs/language.html)
. It allows you to download an Excel spreadsheet for side-by-side translation of the phrases used in a given interview. Once translated, the spreadsheet can be included in a [package](https://docassemble.org/docs/packages.html)
 and mentioned in a [`translations`](https://docassemble.org/docs/initial.html#translations)
 block. Then **docassemble** will use the translations in the file when it needs a translation of a given phrase.

To download a translation file from the “Utilities” screen, you need to provide the name of an interview (e.g., `docassemble.demo:data/questions/questions.yml`) and the target language in [ISO-639-1](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_ISO_639-1_codes)
 or [ISO-639-3](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_ISO_639-3_codes)
 format (e.g., `fr` for French).

The resulting spreadsheet will contain a row for each unique phrase used in the interview file, including interview files incorporated by reference.

The columns of the spreadsheet are:

*   interview: the name of the YAML file containing the question that contained the phrase.
*   question\_id: the [`id`](https://docassemble.org/docs/modifiers.html#id)
     of the question the phrase, or the generic name of the question (which could change if the interview changes).
*   index\_num: an number that indexes the phrase within a given question.
*   hash: an [MD5 hash](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/MD5#MD5_hashes)
     of the phrase (which can be used to test whether the text in the “orig\_text” column was edited, which it should not be)
*   orig\_lang: the original language of the phrase, as indicated by the [`language`](https://docassemble.org/docs/modifiers.html#language)
     specifier of the [`question`](https://docassemble.org/docs/questions.html#question)
    . If the [`question`](https://docassemble.org/docs/questions.html#question)
     does not specify a [`language`](https://docassemble.org/docs/modifiers.html#language)
    , the default language of the YAML file as indicated by the [`default language`](https://docassemble.org/docs/initial.html#default%20language)
     block will be used to determine the language of the [`question`](https://docassemble.org/docs/questions.html#question)
    . If no [`default language`](https://docassemble.org/docs/initial.html#default%20language)
     is specified, the [`default language` specifier in the interview `metadata`](https://docassemble.org/docs/initial.html#metadata%20default%20language)
     will be used. If no such specifier exists in the [`metadata`](https://docassemble.org/docs/initial.html#metadata)
    , the default language of the server (as indicated by the [`langauge` Configuration directive](https://docassemble.org/docs/config.html#language)
    ) will be used.
*   tr\_lang: the language into which the phrase should be translated.
*   orig\_text: the text of the phrase
*   tr\_text: the translated phrase (which is blank if the phrase has not yet been translated.

You can then give the spreadsheet to a translator who will fill in the “tr\_text” column with a translation of the text in the “orig\_text” column.

The spreadsheet with the completed translations can then be uploaded to the [sources](https://docassemble.org/docs/playground.html#sources)
 folder of a package and included in the interview using a [`translations`](https://docassemble.org/docs/initial.html#translations)
 block. If the target language of the spreadsheet is French (`fr`), then the French translations of phrases will be used if the current language in the interview (as determined by the [`set_language()`](https://docassemble.org/docs/functions.html#set_language)
 function is French.

If the interview contains a [`translations`](https://docassemble.org/docs/initial.html#translations)
 block, the file or files referenced in the [`translations`](https://docassemble.org/docs/initial.html#translations)
 block will be scanned and the translations specified in those files will be included as default translations.

If the files referenced in the [`translations`](https://docassemble.org/docs/initial.html#translations)
 block contain phrases that are not present in the interview, perhaps because they used to be present but are no longer present, these extra phrases will be listed at the end of the interview and their “index\_num” values will be numbered starting with 1000.

When using interview phrase translation files, it is important to set the [`default language` specifier in the interview `metadata`](https://docassemble.org/docs/initial.html#metadata%20default%20language)
.

### Word add-in manifest XML file[¶](https://docassemble.org/docs/admin.html#word%20addin%20manifest)

The fourth utility is “Download Office add-in manifest file.” You will need this if you want to enable a [Playground](https://docassemble.org/docs/playground.html#packages)
\-like task pane inside Microsoft Word.

In Microsoft Office, third party add-ins are enabled through XML “manifest” files. Through this utility, you can download a manifest file that is customized for your server. You need to download this XML file and then install it in your Microsoft Office setup.

For more instructions on how to do this, see the [Word add-in section](https://docassemble.org/docs/playground.html#word%20addin)
.

User List[¶](https://docassemble.org/docs/admin.html#user%20list)

------------------------------------------------------------------

The “User List” is available to users with `admin` privileges. It lists all of the user accounts on the system and allows you to edit a user’s information.

When you click the “Edit” button for a user account, you can edit that user’s profile. Under the “Menu,” you can “Add a user,” “Invite a user,” or “Edit privileges.”

### Edit a user profile[¶](https://docassemble.org/docs/admin.html#edit%20user%20profile)

When you are editing a user profile, you can disable the user’s account by unchecking the “Active” checkbox.

You can edit a user’s [privileges](https://docassemble.org/docs/users.html)
. Note that the selector is a multi-select; you can assign more than one privilege to a user. Privileges are additive; a user who has `developer` and `advocate` privileges can do anything a developer can do and everything an advocate can do.

The profile fields that you can edit include:

*   E-mail: this must be unique; no two users can have the same e-mail address.
*   First name
*   Last name
*   Country code: an uppercase [ISO 3166](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/ISO_3166-1_alpha-2)
     country code
*   State
*   County
*   Municipality
*   Organization
*   Language: a lowercase [ISO-639-1](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_ISO_639-1_codes)
     or [ISO-639-3](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_ISO_639-3_codes)
     language code representing the user’s language.

The words “State,” “County,” and “Municipality” are actually translated phrases defined in `docassemble.base:data/sources/us-words.yml`, which is included as a [`words`](https://docassemble.org/docs/config.html#words)
 file in the default [Configuration](https://docassemble.org/docs/config.html)
:

    en:
      First subdivision: State
      Second subdivision: County
      Third subdivision: Municipality

You can use the [`user_info()`](https://docassemble.org/docs/functions.html#user_info)
 and [`set_user_info()`](https://docassemble.org/docs/functions.html#set_user_info)
 function to retrieve and set the user profile attributes, where the state, county, and municipality are known by these attributes:

*   `subdivision_first`
*   `subdivision_second`
*   `subdivision_third`

Under “Other settings,” you have the option to “Delete account but keep shared sessions.” This will delete the user’s account and all of their data. However, if any of the user’s sessions are [multi-user interview](https://docassemble.org/docs/special.html#multi_user)
 that had been joined by another user, those sessions will not be deleted.

You also have the option under “Other settings” to “Delete account and shared sessions.” This will delete the user’s account and all of their data, including any [multi-user interview](https://docassemble.org/docs/special.html#multi_user)
 the user had joined.

These account deletion options can be turned off using the [`admin can delete account`](https://docassemble.org/docs/config.html#admin%20can%20delete%20account)
 directive in the [Configuration](https://docassemble.org/docs/config.html)
.

### Add a user[¶](https://docassemble.org/docs/admin.html#add%20user)

On the “Add a user” page, you can create a new user account by entering an e-mail address and password. Optionally, you can set the user’s first and last names. You can also select the user’s [privileges](https://docassemble.org/docs/users.html)
 with a multiselect selector.

Note that when a user is added using this tool, the user is _not_ notified that an account has been created.

### Invite a user[¶](https://docassemble.org/docs/admin.html#invite%20user)

On the “Invite a user” page, you can send an e-mail invitation to a prospective new user. The privileges of the prospective user can be set in advance. The e-mail will contain a link with an embedded code. Visiting the link will start the process of registration; until that process is completed, no user account will actually exist.

### Edit privileges[¶](https://docassemble.org/docs/admin.html#edit%20privileges)

The “Edit privileges” page allows you to manage the [privileges](https://docassemble.org/docs/users.html)
 that exist on the system. The built-in [privileges](https://docassemble.org/docs/users.html)
 (`user`, `admin`, `developer`, `advocate`, `cron`, and `trainer`) cannot be removed. There is one built-in privilege, `customer`, which has no special meaning within `docassemble` itself, so you are able to delete it.

You can create custom privileges using “Edit privileges,” assign them to users, and then use the [`user_has_privilege()`](https://docassemble.org/docs/functions.html#user_has_privilege)
 and [`user_privileges()`](https://docassemble.org/docs/functions.html#user_privileges)
 in your interviews to do different things depending on what privileges the user has.

User privilege assignment can be controlled by:

1.  Manually [editing a user’s profile](https://docassemble.org/docs/admin.html#edit%20user%20profile)
     from the [User List](https://docassemble.org/docs/admin.html#user%20list)
     page.
2.  Calling the [`set_user_info()`](https://docassemble.org/docs/functions.html#set_user_info)
     function with the `privileges` keyword parameter (calling this function requires `admin` [privileges](https://docassemble.org/docs/users.html)
    ); or
3.  Calling the [`/api/user/<user_id>/privileges`](https://docassemble.org/docs/api.html#user_privilege_add)
     API.

For example, you may wish to use the [privileges](https://docassemble.org/docs/users.html)
 system to keep track of which user accounts are paying customers, or to keep track of different tiers of paying customers.

Configuration[¶](https://docassemble.org/docs/admin.html#configuration)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

The “Configuration” page allows a user with `admin` privileges to edit the server [Configuration](https://docassemble.org/docs/config.html)
. It provides a [YAML](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/YAML)
 text editor in the browser. When the [Configuration](https://docassemble.org/docs/config.html)
 is saved, the services on the server that depend on the [Configuration](https://docassemble.org/docs/config.html)
 are restarted.

The configuration file for a given server is found at `/usr/share/docassemble/config/config.yml`. If you are using cloud-based [data storage](https://docassemble.org/docs/docker.html#data%20storage)
, the `config.yml` file is also stored in the cloud whenever the [Configuration](https://docassemble.org/docs/config.html)
 is saved. If you are using [persistent volumes](https://docassemble.org/docs/docker.html#persistent)
, the configuration is copied to the persistent volume when the server shuts down. Then, when the server starts, the `config.yml` file is restored from storage.

Menu items for all logged-in users[¶](https://docassemble.org/docs/admin.html#menu)

====================================================================================

The following menu items are available to all logged-in users regardless of privileges.

Available Interviews[¶](https://docassemble.org/docs/admin.html#available%20interviews)

----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

The “Available Interviews” page, which is at the URL `/list`, shows a list of interviews that users can run. The list can be configured using the [`dispatch`](https://docassemble.org/docs/config.html#dispatch)
 directive in the [Configuration](https://docassemble.org/docs/config.html)
. If there is no `dispatch` directive, this page will redirect to the system’s default interview, which can be configured using the [`default interview`](https://docassemble.org/docs/config.html#default%20interview)
 directive in the [Configuration](https://docassemble.org/docs/config.html)
.

By default, the “Available Interviews” menu item is not shown. It can be enabled by setting [`show dispatch link`](https://docassemble.org/docs/config.html#show%20dispatch%20link)
 to `True` in the [Configuration](https://docassemble.org/docs/config.html)
.

If an interview is listed under [`dispatch`](https://docassemble.org/docs/config.html#dispatch)
, but the [`metadata`](https://docassemble.org/docs/initial.html#metadata)
 of the interview contains the specifier [`unlisted`](https://docassemble.org/docs/initial.html#unlisted)
 set to `True`, then the interview will not be listed in the `/list`, although it will still be usable with a `/start` shortcut.

The URL parameter `tag` can be used to filter the list of available interviews. For example, if you set `tag=estates`, then the only interviews that will be listed are those that have `estates` as one of the [`tags`](https://docassemble.org/docs/initial.html#tags)
 in the interview [`metadata`](https://docassemble.org/docs/initial.html#metadata)
.

You can use the [`required privileges`](https://docassemble.org/docs/initial.html#required%20privileges)
 specifier in the [`metadata`](https://docassemble.org/docs/initial.html#metadata)
 of each interview listed under [`dispatch`](https://docassemble.org/docs/config.html#dispatch)
 to control whether the interview should appear in the list, depending on the [privileges](https://docassemble.org/docs/users.html)
 of the user.

For information about how to customize the “Available Interviews” page, see the [Configuration](https://docassemble.org/docs/config.html)
 directives that begin with [`start page`](https://docassemble.org/docs/config.html#customization)
, or configure the [`start page template`](https://docassemble.org/docs/config.html#start%20page%20template)
, or replace the page with an interview using [`dispatch interview`](https://docassemble.org/docs/config.html#dispatch%20interview)
.

My Interviews[¶](https://docassemble.org/docs/admin.html#my%20interviews)

--------------------------------------------------------------------------

The “My Interviews” page, which is at the URL `/interviews`, shows a list of the user’s existing interview sessions.

For more information about how sessions work in **docassemble**, see the subsections on [how you run a **docassemble** interview](https://docassemble.org/docs/interviews.html#invocation)
 and [leaving an interview and coming back](https://docassemble.org/docs/interviews.html#comingback)
.

The “My Interviews” menu item can be hidden from the menu using the [`show interviews link`](https://docassemble.org/docs/config.html#show%20interviews%20link)
 directive in the [Configuration](https://docassemble.org/docs/config.html)
.

For information about how to customize the “My Interviews” page, see the [Configuration](https://docassemble.org/docs/config.html)
 directives that begin with [`interview page`](https://docassemble.org/docs/config.html#customization)
, or configure the [`interview page template`](https://docassemble.org/docs/config.html#interview%20page%20template)
, or replace the page with an interview using [`session list interview`](https://docassemble.org/docs/config.html#session%20list%20interview)
.

Profile[¶](https://docassemble.org/docs/admin.html#profile)

------------------------------------------------------------

The “Profile” page allows the user to edit their user account profile. The following fields can be changed:

*   First name
*   Last name

If the user registered using the [phone login](https://docassemble.org/docs/config.html#phone%20login)
 method, the user can also edit his or her e-mail address. However, users who registered with other methods cannot edit their e-mail address.

In addition, users with privileges of `admin` or `developer` can edit the following fields:

*   Country code: an uppercase [ISO 3166](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/ISO_3166-1_alpha-2)
     country code
*   State
*   County
*   Municipality
*   Organization
*   Language: a lowercase [ISO-639-1](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_ISO_639-1_codes)
     or [ISO-639-3](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_ISO_639-3_codes)
     language code representing the user’s language.
*   PyPI Username
*   PyPI Password

From the “Other settings” menu, the user can:

*   Change their password
*   Configure [Google Drive synchronization](https://docassemble.org/docs/installation.html#google%20drive)
    
*   Configure [OneDrive synchronization](https://docassemble.org/docs/installation.html#onedrive)
    
*   Configure [multi-factor authentication](https://docassemble.org/docs/config.html#mfa)
    
*   Configure [GitHub integration](https://docassemble.org/docs/installation.html#github)
    
*   [Manage API keys](https://docassemble.org/docs/api.html#manage_api)
    
*   Manage their account

Whether these commands are available depends on the [Configuration](https://docassemble.org/docs/config.html)
. The “Manage account” setting allows the user to delete their account. To disable this, edit the [`user can delete account`](https://docassemble.org/docs/config.html#user%20can%20delete%20account)
 directive in the [Configuration](https://docassemble.org/docs/config.html)
.

Sign Out[¶](https://docassemble.org/docs/admin.html#sign%20out)

----------------------------------------------------------------

The “Sign Out” menu item logs the user out and expires the user’s session cookies. If the user was in the process of using an interview, the interview session will not be deleted.

Endpoints for monitoring[¶](https://docassemble.org/docs/admin.html#endpoints)

===============================================================================

If you call `/health_check`, it will return the text `OK` if the web app is operating. If you call `/health_check?ready=1`, it will return the text `OK` only if the web app is operating and the [Docker](https://docassemble.org/docs/docker.html)
