 initialization process has completed, meaning that associated services have been started. If the initialized process has not completed, a 400 response will be returned.

If you call `/health_status`, it will return a JSON object with the following keys:

*   `ok` - indicates that the [Docker](https://docassemble.org/docs/docker.html)
     initialization process has completed.
*   `server_start_time` - indicates the timestamp (in epoch seconds) at which the web app last started. This changes after each server restart.
*   `version` - indicates the version of **docassemble** running on the system.

Troubleshooting[¶](https://docassemble.org/docs/admin.html#troubleshooting)

============================================================================

For tips on troubleshooting your **docassemble** system, see the [troubleshooting](https://docassemble.org/docs/docker.html#troubleshooting)
 subsection in the [Docker](https://docassemble.org/docs/docker.html)
 section.

* * *

---

# Contributing

[Edit](https://github.com/jhpyle/docassemble/blob/gh-pages/_docs/contributing.md "Edit this page on GitHub")

Contributing
============

If you would like to contribute to the **docassemble** project, the most impactful thing you can do is use the software. You are also encouraged to participate in the [Slack](https://join.slack.com/t/docassemble/shared_invite/zt-2cspzjo9j-YyE7SrLmi5muAvnPv~Bz~A)
 group and contribute bug reports and feature requests.

If you would like to help fix bugs, pull requests on GitHub are welcome.

If you would like to contribute code for new features, the best practice is to first write your features as an [add-on package](https://docassemble.org/docs/packages.html)
 and let the community know about it on [Slack](https://join.slack.com/t/docassemble/shared_invite/zt-2cspzjo9j-YyE7SrLmi5muAvnPv~Bz~A)
. If it makes sense to make the feature part of core **docassemble**, it can be moved into the core.

If you would like to contribute money, note that **docassemble** is not an entity and is not sponsored by any entity, so it is not possible to fund the development of **docassemble** directly. However, you are welcome to [make a financial contribution](https://www.merlinfoundation.org/donate)
 to the [Merlin Legal Open Source Foundation](https://www.merlinfoundation.org/)
, which is a non-profit that supports the cause of free and open-source software in the legal field.

* * *

---

# Policies

[Edit](https://github.com/jhpyle/docassemble/blob/gh-pages/_docs/policies.md "Edit this page on GitHub")

Policies
========

**docassemble** is licensed under the [MIT License](https://docassemble.org/docs/license.html)
. The policies listed here do not modify that license or create any contract.

*   [Data privacy policy](https://docassemble.org/policies/data-privacy.html)
    
*   [Business continuity policy](https://docassemble.org/policies/business-continuity.html)
    
*   [Cybersecurity policy](https://docassemble.org/policies/cyber-security.html)

# Security

[Edit](https://github.com/jhpyle/docassemble/blob/gh-pages/_docs/security.md "Edit this page on GitHub")

Security
========

Contents
--------

*   [1 Security features](https://docassemble.org/docs/security.html#tocAnchor-1-1)
    *   [1.1 Server-side information storage](https://docassemble.org/docs/security.html#server_encryption)
        
    *   [1.2 Sign-in security](https://docassemble.org/docs/security.html#signin)
        
*   [2 Importance of using HTTPS](https://docassemble.org/docs/security.html#https)
    
*   [3 Separate development and production servers](https://docassemble.org/docs/security.html#separateservers)
    
*   [4 Avoiding sharing session keys](https://docassemble.org/docs/security.html#sessionkeys)
    
*   [5 Security in multi-user interviews](https://docassemble.org/docs/security.html#multiuser)
    
*   [6 Deleting user information](https://docassemble.org/docs/security.html#deleting)
    
*   [7 Issues to look out for](https://docassemble.org/docs/security.html#issues)
    

**docassemble** is designed with user privacy in mind.

Security features
=================

Server-side information storage[¶](https://docassemble.org/docs/security.html#server_encryption)

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

User passwords are encrypted. [Flask-Login](https://flask-login.readthedocs.io/en/latest/)
 is the basis for the username/password system.

Interview answers are stored in a SQL database in encrypted form, unless the [`multi_user`](https://docassemble.org/docs/special.html#multi_user)
 variable is set to `True`, in which case the answers are not encrypted.

If users log in with [external authentication methods](https://docassemble.org/docs/config.html#oauth)
 or the [phone login](https://docassemble.org/docs/config.html#phone%20login)
 feature, interview answers are still encrypted (unless the [`multi_user`](https://docassemble.org/docs/special.html#multi_user)
 variable is set to `True`), but a resourceful hacker could figure out the encryption key. Therefore, if server-side encryption is important to you, it is recommended that you do not enable [external authentication methods](https://docassemble.org/docs/config.html#oauth)
 or the [phone login](https://docassemble.org/docs/config.html#phone%20login)
 feature.

Uploaded and assembled documents are stored on the server (and on [Amazon S3](https://aws.amazon.com/s3/)
, if [S3](https://aws.amazon.com/s3/)
 is being used, or [Microsoft Azure](https://azure.microsoft.com/)
, if [Azure blob storage](https://azure.microsoft.com/en-us/services/storage/blobs/)
 is being used) without encryption. These documents cannot be accessed from the internet without an appropriate access key in the cookie. In some situations, the user is provided with a temporary URL to an [S3](https://aws.amazon.com/s3/)
 resource that allows anyone who has the URL to access the document. In other situations, the **docassemble** server retrieves the file from [S3](https://aws.amazon.com/s3/)
 and serves it.

When a user clicks an “Exit” button (or the `/exit` path is visited, or the `/exit_logout` path is visited, or `command('exit')` is run, or `command('exit_logout')` is run), **docassemble** will delete all of the information related to the interview from the server, including the database, uploaded documents, and assembled documents. See [deleting user information](https://docassemble.org/docs/security.html#deleting)
 for more information.

Sign-in security[¶](https://docassemble.org/docs/security.html#signin)

-----------------------------------------------------------------------

**docassemble** blocks brute-force password-guessing attacks.

**docassemble** also supports [two-factor authentication](https://docassemble.org/docs/config.html#mfa)
.

By default, users do not need to verify their e-mail addresses, but you can require them to do so. See the documentation for the [`email confirmation privileges`](https://docassemble.org/docs/config.html#email%20confirmation)
 configuration directive.

Importance of using HTTPS[¶](https://docassemble.org/docs/security.html#https)

===============================================================================

It is important for security purposes to deploy **docassemble** using HTTPS rather than HTTP. If you use HTTP, passwords and security keys will be sent over the network in plain text.

Separate development and production servers[¶](https://docassemble.org/docs/security.html#separateservers)

===========================================================================================================

Users with “developer” access can run arbitrary code on the server. For this reason, it is recommended that you not allow developer accounts on production servers, and that you only install **docassemble** add-on packages that you have carefully reviewed.

On a production server, you should make sure the [Configuration](https://docassemble.org/docs/config.html)
 contains the following:

    debug: False
    allow demo: False
    enable playground: False

The [`debug`](https://docassemble.org/docs/config.html#debug)
 directive will turn off the “Source” tab, so that users cannot see any source code of the interviews. This will also improve site performance.

The [`allow demo`](https://docassemble.org/docs/config.html#allow%20demo)
 directive will prevent users from accessing any of the sample interviews in the `docassemble.base` and `docassemble.demo` packages. These are helpful on a development server because developers can access them from the Examples page of the [Playground](https://docassemble.org/docs/playground.html)
. However, on a production server there is no reason why users should be able to run such interviews.

The [`enable playground`](https://docassemble.org/docs/config.html#enable%20playground)
 directive will make the [Playground](https://docassemble.org/docs/playground.html)
 inoperable. Any attempt by a user to use a [Playground](https://docassemble.org/docs/playground.html)
 interview will fail. Users with developer and administrator accounts will not see the [Playground](https://docassemble.org/docs/playground.html)
\-related options in the menus.

Avoiding sharing session keys[¶](https://docassemble.org/docs/security.html#sessionkeys)

=========================================================================================

Each session of a **docassemble** interview is uniquely identified by a session key, which is a series of randomly-generated characters. If server-side encryption is turned off (by setting [`multi_user`](https://docassemble.org/docs/special.html#multi_user)
 to `True`), then anyone who possesses the encryption key can access the interview session. Thus there is a risk to e-mailing a user a link to an interview generated by [`interview_url()`](https://docassemble.org/docs/functions.html#interview_url)
, since the URL will contain the session key. This URL is like the URL to a Google Docs file where “anyone with the link can edit.” You can mitigate this risk somewhat by using the `temporary` keyword argument or the `once_temporary` keyword argument to [`interview_url()`](https://docassemble.org/docs/functions.html#interview_url)
. Then, the link that you share will not contain the actual interview session key, and the link will expire after a period of time.

Security in multi-user interviews[¶](https://docassemble.org/docs/security.html#multiuser)

===========================================================================================

If you have a [multi-user interview](https://docassemble.org/docs/roles.html)
, you will set [`multi_user`](https://docassemble.org/docs/special.html#multi_user)
 to `True` in order to turn off server-side encryption. Then anyone with the session key can access a session.

In the ordinary course of a [multi-user interview](https://docassemble.org/docs/roles.html)
, each user will only see the screens your interview expects them to see. For example, you could design a divorce mediation interview in which two parties to a divorce each share confidential information into the same session, and one spouse will not be able to see information entered by the other spouse.

However, **docassemble**’s [front end interface](https://docassemble.org/docs/frontend.html)
 is designed to be open and flexible, so that developers have the freedom to innovate. By default, the [JavaScript](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/JavaScript)
 function [`get_interview_variables()`](https://docassemble.org/docs/functions.html#js_get_interview_variables)
 is available, which will retrieve all of the interview answers in [JSON](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/JSON)
 format from the server.

If one of the users who has access to the session key is not fully trusted, you may worry that they will teach themselves how **docassemble** works and take steps to “snoop around” in the interview and access information they shouldn’t, for example by running [`get_interview_variables()`](https://docassemble.org/docs/functions.html#js_get_interview_variables)
 in the [JavaScript](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/JavaScript)
 console or visiting the `/vars?i=...` page (which is called by [`get_interview_variables()`](https://docassemble.org/docs/functions.html#js_get_interview_variables)
 to obtain a [JSON](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/JSON)
 version of the interview answers for the current session).

You can take steps to “harden” your interview against such unauthorized snooping. You can include the following at the beginning of your interview:

    mandatory: True
    code: |
      set_status(variable_access=False)

This will disable the [`get_interview_variables()`](https://docassemble.org/docs/functions.html#js_get_interview_variables)
 feature.

Another way that a user could snoop around is by using the [JavaScript](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/JavaScript)
 function [`url_action()`](https://docassemble.org/docs/functions.html#js_url_action)
 to access screens they are not supposed to see. For example, if your two users are represented by two [`Individual`](https://docassemble.org/docs/objects.html#Individual)
s in your interview answers called `petitioner` and `respondent`, the respondent might try to call `url_action("petitioner.ssn")` from the [JavaScript](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/JavaScript)
 console.

You can provide security over the [actions](https://docassemble.org/docs/functions.html#actions)
 mechanism by calling [`process_action()`](https://docassemble.org/docs/functions.html#process_action)
 manually inside of a [`code`](https://docassemble.org/docs/code.html#code)
 block that has [`initial`](https://docassemble.org/docs/logic.html#initial)
 set to `True`. By default, **docassemble** will run [`process_action()`](https://docassemble.org/docs/functions.html#process_action)
 for you, but if it sees that you have a [`code`](https://docassemble.org/docs/code.html#code)
 block that calls [`process_action()`](https://docassemble.org/docs/functions.html#process_action)
, it will refrain from calling it automatically. You can use the [`action_argument()`](https://docassemble.org/docs/functions.html#action_argument)
 function to obtain the action being requested.

    initial: True
    code: |
      if action_argument() in ['revisit_income', 'revisit_expenses']:
        process_action()

Deleting user information[¶](https://docassemble.org/docs/security.html#deleting)

==================================================================================

By default, interview sessions are deleted after 90 days of inactivity. This period can be modified using the [`interview delete days`](https://docassemble.org/docs/config.html#interview%20delete%20days)
 directive in the [Configuration](https://docassemble.org/docs/config.html)
.

Users who are logged in can delete their interview sessions by going to [My Interviews](https://docassemble.org/docs/admin.html#my%20interviews)
 and clicking the “Delete All” button or by deleting individual interviews. The interview session can also be deleted through the use of an `exit` [button](https://docassemble.org/docs/questions.html#special%20buttons)
, an `exit_logout` [button](https://docassemble.org/docs/questions.html#special%20buttons)
, a call to [`command()`](https://docassemble.org/docs/functions.html#command)
 with `'exit'` or `'exit_logout'` as the parameter, or if the user visits `/exit` or `/exit_logout`.

Users who are logged in can delete their entire account by going to [Profile](https://docassemble.org/docs/admin.html#profile)
, “Other settings,” “Manage account.” A URL for the “Manage account” page can be obtained by calling `url_of('manage')`. When a user deletes their account, this has same effect as “Delete All” on the [My Interviews](https://docassemble.org/docs/admin.html#my%20interviews)
 page, and it also deletes other information, such as the user profile data. By default, account deletion does not delete multi-user interview sessions that have been entered by another user. However, if you want users to have the power to delete multi-user interview sessions, you can set [`delete account deletes shared`](https://docassemble.org/docs/config.html#delete%20account%20deletes%20shared)
 in the [Configuration](https://docassemble.org/docs/config.html)
 to `True`.

Users who are not logged in can also delete their data by going to the `/user/manage` page (the URL for which can be obtained by calling `url_of('manage')`).

Administrators can delete user accounts on the [User List](https://docassemble.org/docs/admin.html#user%20list)
 page by editing an individual user. Administrators have the option to delete the account but leave shared interviews alone, or delete the account and all of its shared interviews.

When **docassemble** deletes an interview session, it deletes:

*   All steps of the interview answers (from SQL).
*   All files associated with the interview session (e.g., uploaded files, generated files) except for files with the `persistent` attribute set.
*   Cached speech-to-text audio files.
*   E-mail addresses generated by [`interview_email()`](https://docassemble.org/docs/functions.html#interview_email)
    .
*   E-mails sent to addresses generated by [`interview_email()`](https://docassemble.org/docs/functions.html#interview_email)
    .
*   [Live Help](https://docassemble.org/docs/livehelp.html)
     chat logs from the session.

When **docassemble** deletes an interview session, it does **not** delete:

*   Records created during the session using [`DAStore`](https://docassemble.org/docs/objects.html#DAStore)
    , [`DARedis`](https://docassemble.org/docs/objects.html#DARedis)
    , or [`write_record()`](https://docassemble.org/docs/functions.html#write_record)
    .
*   Records created in the machine learning system from the session.
*   Files with the `persistent` attribute set to true.

When **docassemble** deletes a user account, it deletes:

*   All interview sessions started by the user (except for shared interview sessions joined by another user, which are not deleted unless the administrator chooses to include them in the deletion process).
*   Records stored by the user with [`DAStore`](https://docassemble.org/docs/objects.html#DAStore)
    .
*   The name, organization, and other information in the user’s [Profile](https://docassemble.org/docs/admin.html#profile)
    .
*   The contents of the user’s [Playground](https://docassemble.org/docs/playground.html)
    .
*   Other information associated with the user, such as authentication tokens for integration with the GitHub API and authentication tokens created for the user’s e-mail address with [`DAOAuth`](https://docassemble.org/docs/objects.html#DAOAuth)
    .

When **docassemble** deletes a user account, it does not delete:

*   Records created by the user using [`DARedis`](https://docassemble.org/docs/objects.html#DARedis)
     or [`write_record()`](https://docassemble.org/docs/functions.html#write_record)
    .
*   Records created by the user in the machine learning system.
*   Files containing user data that set the `persistent` attribute set to true.

If you are using a [multi-server arrangement](https://docassemble.org/docs/docker.html#multi%20server%20arrangement)
 with cloud storage, then when you delete a file, copies of the file in a cache directory on /tmp on some servers may persist until cleaned out by a cron job. The [Docker](https://docassemble.org/docs/docker.html)
 hourly cron job deletes files from the cache if they have not been accessed in the past two hours.

Issues to look out for[¶](https://docassemble.org/docs/security.html#issues)

=============================================================================

**docassemble** makes extensive use of [eval](https://docs.python.org/3/library/functions.html#eval)
 and [exec](https://docs.python.org/3/reference/simple_stmts.html#exec)
, including to process information supplied by the user. There are protections in place to prevent code injection, but more work could be done to harden **docassemble** against such threats.

The options for allowing users to log in with Google, Facebook, Twitter, Azure, and mobile phone numbers are provided for convenience, but you may wish not to enable these features because the encryption of interview answers on the server is less effective when users log in without a password. This is because the password is used as the encryption key, which is advantageous because it is not stored anywhere on the server; the user remembers it and it is stored in a cookie in the user’s browser. When users log in with something other than a password, however, there is no such key available. As a result, the key for encrypting user answers on the server is constructed based on information that is stored on the server. As a result, an intruder with access to the server could reverse-engineer the encrpytion key for an interview belonging to a user who logs in with something other than a password.

* * *

---

# Scalability of docassemble

[Edit](https://github.com/jhpyle/docassemble/blob/gh-pages/_docs/scalability.md "Edit this page on GitHub")

Scalability of docassemble
==========================

Contents
--------

*   [1 Multi-server configuration on Kubernetes](https://docassemble.org/docs/scalability.html#kubernetes)
    
*   [2 Multi-server configuration on EC2 Container Service](https://docassemble.org/docs/scalability.html#ecs)
    *   [2.1 Overview](https://docassemble.org/docs/scalability.html#ecs%20overview)
        
    *   [2.2 Instructions](https://docassemble.org/docs/scalability.html#ecs%20instructions)
        
*   [3 Single-server configuration on EC2 Container Service](https://docassemble.org/docs/scalability.html#singleserver)
    
*   [4 How it works](https://docassemble.org/docs/scalability.html#tocAnchor-1-6)
    *   [4.1 Log file aggregation](https://docassemble.org/docs/scalability.html#log)
        
    *   [4.2 Auto-discovery of services](https://docassemble.org/docs/scalability.html#autodiscovery)
        
    *   [4.3 File sharing](https://docassemble.org/docs/scalability.html#fileshare)
        
*   [5 EC2 hostname discovery](https://docassemble.org/docs/scalability.html#tocAnchor-1-10)
    
*   [6 SQL server](https://docassemble.org/docs/scalability.html#tocAnchor-1-11)
    
*   [7 E-mail sending](https://docassemble.org/docs/scalability.html#email)
    
*   [8 Using S3 without passing access keys in the configuration](https://docassemble.org/docs/scalability.html#tocAnchor-1-13)
    
*   [9 Performance](https://docassemble.org/docs/scalability.html#tocAnchor-1-14)
    
*   [10 PostgreSQL concurrency](https://docassemble.org/docs/scalability.html#tocAnchor-1-15)
    

**docassemble** can easily be scaled in the cloud. A cluster of web servers can serve responses to client browsers, while communicating with centralized services. The limit to scalability will then be a question of how responsive a single SQL server can be and how responsive a single [Redis](https://redis.io/)
 server can be.

You probably do not need to deploy **docassemble** with multiple application servers. A single application server can handle a lot of traffic, and if it starts to get overloaded, you can always upgrade to a more powerful machine. Deploying **docassemble** on a single server involves far fewer steps than setting up a multi-server arrangement. The slightest deviation from the instructions in this section could cause your cluster not to function, and the reasons could be mysterious. Unless you really understand the technologies, it might be difficult for you to debug issues with multi-server arrangements.

If you want to run **docassemble** in a scalable [multi-server arrangement](https://docassemble.org/docs/docker.html#multi%20server%20arrangement)
, there are a variety of ways to do so. This section of the documentation discusses two possible methods, one that uses [Kubernetes](https://kubernetes.io/)
 and [Helm](https://helm.sh/)
, and the other that uses [Amazon Web Services](https://aws.amazon.com/)
 ([AWS](https://aws.amazon.com/)
).

Multi-server configuration on Kubernetes[¶](https://docassemble.org/docs/scalability.html#kubernetes)

======================================================================================================

The recommended method of deploying **docassemble** in a multi-server configuration is to use [Kubernetes](https://kubernetes.io/)
 and [Helm](https://helm.sh/)
. This deployment method is documented separately in the [`jhpyle/charts`](https://github.com/jhpyle/charts)
 repository on [GitHub](https://github.com/)
.

Multi-server configuration on EC2 Container Service[¶](https://docassemble.org/docs/scalability.html#ecs)

==========================================================================================================

Overview[¶](https://docassemble.org/docs/scalability.html#ecs%20overview)

--------------------------------------------------------------------------

In this example, one server will perform central functions (SQL, [Redis](https://redis.io/)
, [RabbitMQ](https://www.rabbitmq.com/)
, and log message aggregation) and two separate servers will act as application servers. The application servers will act as web servers, receiving requests that are distributed by a [load balancer](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Load_balancing_(computing))
. These servers will also operate as [Celery](https://www.celeryproject.org/)
 nodes, running [background processes](https://docassemble.org/docs/background.html#background)
 for **docassemble** interviews. All three of the servers will be [EC2](https://aws.amazon.com/ec2/)
 virtual machines situated within a private network on [AWS](https://aws.amazon.com/)
 called a [Virtual Private Cloud](https://aws.amazon.com/vpc/)
 ([VPC](https://aws.amazon.com/vpc/)
).

Users will point their browsers at a URL like `https://docassemble.example.com`, where the [DNS](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Domain_Name_System)
 entry for `docassemble.example.com` is a [CNAME](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/CNAME_record)
 that points to an [Application Load Balancer](https://aws.amazon.com/elasticloadbalancing/applicationloadbalancer/)
 (at an address like `myloadbalancer-198225082.us-west-1.elb.amazonaws.com`). The [Application Load Balancer](https://aws.amazon.com/elasticloadbalancing/applicationloadbalancer/)
 will take care of generating and serving SSL certificates for the encrypted connection.

The [Application Load Balancer](https://aws.amazon.com/elasticloadbalancing/applicationloadbalancer/)
 will convert the HTTPS requests into HTTP requests and send them to the web servers. From the perspective of the web servers, all incoming requests will use the HTTP scheme. But from the perspective of the user’s web browser, the web site uses the HTTPS scheme.

Within the [VPC](https://aws.amazon.com/vpc/)
, the web servers will communicate with the central server using a variety of TCP ports.

The three servers will all be part of an [Auto Scaling Group](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AutoScaling/latest/DeveloperGuide/AutoScalingGroup.html)
. This means that as more people use your site, you can expand beyond three virtual machines to any number of virtual machines. The new virtual machines that are created will act as web servers, and the [Application Load Balancer](https://aws.amazon.com/elasticloadbalancing/applicationloadbalancer/)
 will distribute the traffic among them. If traffic dies down, some of those virtual machines can be turned off.

The software will be deployed on the servers by the [EC2 Container Service](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonECS/latest/developerguide/Welcome.html)
 ([ECS](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonECS/latest/developerguide/Welcome.html)
). [ECS](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonECS/latest/developerguide/Welcome.html)
 is [Amazon](https://amazon.com/)
’s system for automatically deploying [Docker](https://docassemble.org/docs/docker.html)
 containers on [EC2](https://aws.amazon.com/ec2/)
 instances.

In the vocabulary of [ECS](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonECS/latest/developerguide/Welcome.html)
, there will be one “[cluster](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonECS/latest/developerguide/ECS_clusters.html)
” (the default cluster) running two services: “backend” and “app.”

The “backend” service consists of a single “task,” where the task is defined as a single [Docker](https://docassemble.org/docs/docker.html)
 “container” running the “image” `jhpyle/docassemble` with the environment variable [`CONTAINERROLE`](https://docassemble.org/docs/scalability.html#CONTAINERROLE)
 set to `sql:redis:rabbitmq:log:cron:mail`. You will ask for one of these services to run (i.e. the “desired count” of this service is set to 1).

The “app” service consists of a single “task,” where the task is defined as a single [Docker](https://docassemble.org/docs/docker.html)
 “container” running the “image” `jhpyle/docassemble` with the environment variable [`CONTAINERROLE`](https://docassemble.org/docs/scalability.html#CONTAINERROLE)
 set to `web:celery`. You will ask for two of these services to run (i.e. the “desired count” of this service is set to 2). (You will be able to change this count in the future.)

The result of this configuration will be that three [EC2](https://aws.amazon.com/ec2/)
 virtual machines will exist, all of which will be running [Docker](https://docassemble.org/docs/docker.html)
. Each virtual machine will “run” the same [Docker](https://docassemble.org/docs/docker.html)
 container, except that the “backend” instance will run with different environment variables than the “app” instances.

To shutdown your **docassemble** setup, you would first edit the “app” service and set the “count” to zero. Then, after a few minutes, the [Docker](https://docassemble.org/docs/docker.html)
 containers will stop. Once the “app” services are no longer running, you will then do the same with the “backend” service. Then, if you want to turn off your [EC2](https://aws.amazon.com/ec2/)
 virtual machines, you would edit the [Auto Scaling Group](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AutoScaling/latest/DeveloperGuide/AutoScalingGroup.html)
 and set the desired number of instances to zero.

You can then restart your **docassemble** system and it will pick up exactly where it left off. This is because **docassemble** will back up SQL, [Redis](https://redis.io/)
, and other information to [S3](https://aws.amazon.com/s3/)
 (or [Azure blob storage](https://azure.microsoft.com/en-us/services/storage/blobs/)
 if you prefer) when the containers shut down, and restore from the backups when they start up again. To restart, you would edit the [Auto Scaling Group](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AutoScaling/latest/DeveloperGuide/AutoScalingGroup.html)
 to set the desired number of instances to 3. When they are up and running, you would then update the “backend” service and set the “desired count” to 1. Once that service is up and running, you would update the “app” service and set the “desired count” to 2.

Instructions[¶](https://docassemble.org/docs/scalability.html#ecs%20instructions)

----------------------------------------------------------------------------------

The following instructions will guide you through the process of using [AWS](https://aws.amazon.com/)
 to set up a **docassemble** installation. [AWS](https://aws.amazon.com/)
 itself is beyond the scope of this documentation. If you have never used [AWS](https://aws.amazon.com/)
 before, you are encouraged to consult the [AWS](https://aws.amazon.com/)
 documentation.

These instructions assume that you own a [domain name](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Domain_name)
 and can add [DNS](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Domain_Name_System)
 entries for the domain. (If you do not have a [domain name](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Domain_name)
, you can purchase one from a site like [GoDaddy](https://www.godaddy.com/)
 or [Google Domains](https://domains.google.com/)
.) In this example, we will use `docassemble.example.com` as the example hostname, but you will need to replace this with whatever hostname you are going to use.

First, sign up for an [Amazon Web Services](https://aws.amazon.com/)
 account.

Log in to the [AWS Console](https://aws.amazon.com/console/)
. If you have not used [ECS](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonECS/latest/developerguide/Welcome.html)
 before, navigate to the service called “EC2 Container Service” and click “Get Started” to follow the steps of the introductory “wizard.” Check the option for “deploy a sample application onto an Amazon ECS Cluster.” Follow the default selections. The wizard will create a [cluster](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonECS/latest/developerguide/ECS_clusters.html)
 called “default,” an [IAM](https://aws.amazon.com/iam/)
 role called `ecsInstanceRole`, and several resources, including a [CloudFormation](https://aws.amazon.com/cloudformation/)
 stack, an [Internet Gateway](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonVPC/latest/UserGuide/VPC_Internet_Gateway.html)
, [VPC](https://aws.amazon.com/vpc/)
, a [Route Table](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonVPC/latest/UserGuide/VPC_Route_Tables.html)
, a [VPC Attached Gateway](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonVPC/latest/UserGuide/VPC_Internet_Gateway.html#Add_IGW_Attach_Gateway)
, two [subnets](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonVPC/latest/UserGuide/VPC_Subnets.html)
, a [Security Group](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AWSEC2/latest/UserGuide/using-network-security.html)
 for an [Elastic Load Balancer](https://aws.amazon.com/elasticloadbalancing/)
, a [Security Group](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AWSEC2/latest/UserGuide/using-network-security.html)
 for [ECS](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonECS/latest/developerguide/Welcome.html)
 instances, a [Launch Configuration](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/autoscaling/latest/userguide/LaunchConfiguration.html)
, and an [Auto Scaling Group](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AutoScaling/latest/DeveloperGuide/AutoScalingGroup.html)
.

The resources the wizard creates are helpful for educational purposes, but in the long term they are not needed and should be deleted. The running [EC2](https://aws.amazon.com/ec2/)
 instance may also cost you money. To delete the unnecessary resources:

1.  Go to the [ECS Console](https://console.aws.amazon.com/ecs/home)
    , find the service “sample-webapp,” update it, and set the “Number of tasks” to 0. Then you can delete “sample-webapp.” Also go into “Task Definitions” and delete the one [Task Definition](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonECS/latest/developerguide/task_defintions.html)
     there.
2.  Go to the [EC2 Console](https://console.aws.amazon.com/ec2/home)
    , go into the “Instances” section, and change the “Instance State” of the one running instance to “Terminate.”
3.  Then go into the “Auto Scaling Groups” section and delete the one [Auto Scaling Group](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AutoScaling/latest/DeveloperGuide/AutoScalingGroup.html)
    .
4.  Then go into the “Launch Configurations” section and delete the one [Launch Configuration](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/autoscaling/latest/userguide/LaunchConfiguration.html)
    .
5.  Then go to the [VPC Console](https://console.aws.amazon.com/vpc/home)
    , select the [VPC](https://aws.amazon.com/vpc/)
     for which “Default VPC” is “No,” and delete it. (Be careful not to delete the default VPC!)

Next, we need to create an [S3](https://aws.amazon.com/s3/)
 “bucket” in which your **docassemble** system can store files, backups, and other things. To do this, go to the [S3 Console](https://console.aws.amazon.com/s3/home)
, click “Create Bucket,” and pick a name. If your site is at docassemble.example.com, a good name for the bucket is `docassemble-example-com`. (Client software will have trouble accessing your bucket if it contains `.` characters.) Under “Region,” pick the region nearest you.

Next, we need to create an [IAM](https://aws.amazon.com/iam/)
 role for the [EC2](https://aws.amazon.com/ec2/)
 instances that will run **docassemble**. This role will empower the server to operate on [ECS](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonECS/latest/developerguide/Welcome.html)
 and to access the [S3](https://aws.amazon.com/s3/)
 “bucket” you created. To do this, go to the [IAM Console](https://console.aws.amazon.com/iam)
 and go to “Roles” -> “Create New Role.” Call your new role “docassembleInstanceRole” or some other name of your choosing. Under “AWS Service Roles,” select “Amazon EC2 Role for EC2 Container Service.” On the “Attach Policy” screen, check the checkbox next to “AmazonEC2ContainerServiceforEC2Role,” and continue on to create the role.

Once the role is created, go into the “Inline Policies” area of the role and create a new inline policy. Select “Custom Policy,” enter a “Policy Name” like “S3DocassembleExampleCom,” and set the “Policy Document” to the following (substituting the name of the bucket you created in place of `docassemble-example-com` in the two places it appears):

    {
        "Version": "2012-10-17",
        "Statement": [\
            {\
                "Effect": "Allow",\
                "Action": [\
                    "s3:ListBucket"\
                ],\
                "Resource": [\
                    "arn:aws:s3:::docassemble-example-com"\
                ]\
            },\
            {\
                "Effect": "Allow",\
                "Action": [\
                    "s3:PutObject",\
                    "s3:GetObject",\
                    "s3:DeleteObject"\
                ],\
                "Resource": [\
                    "arn:aws:s3:::docassemble-example-com/*"\
                ]\
            }\
        ]
    }

(Note that instead of creating an inline policy that limits access to a particular bucket, you could attach the “AmazonS3FullAccess” policy to the role and give [EC2](https://aws.amazon.com/ec2/)
 instances full access to all of your account’s [S3](https://aws.amazon.com/s3/)
 buckets.)

This inline policy allows any application running on a machine given the `docassembleInstanceRole` role to access your [S3](https://aws.amazon.com/s3/)
 bucket. This saves you from having to include long and complicated secret keys in the environment variables you pass to **docassemble** [Docker](https://docassemble.org/docs/docker.html)
 containers. All of the [EC2](https://aws.amazon.com/ec2/)
 instances you create in later steps will be given the `docassembleInstanceRole` [IAM](https://aws.amazon.com/iam/)
 role.

Go to [VPC Console](https://console.aws.amazon.com/vpc/home)
 and inspect the description of the default [VPC](https://aws.amazon.com/vpc/)
. (If you have more than one [VPC](https://aws.amazon.com/vpc/)
, look for the [VPC](https://aws.amazon.com/vpc/)
 where “Default VPC” is “Yes.”) Make a note of the [VPC CIDR](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonVPC/latest/UserGuide/VPC_Subnets.html)
 address for the VPC, which will be something like `172.68.0.0/16` or `10.1.0.0/16`.

Go to the [EC2 Console](https://console.aws.amazon.com/ec2/home)
 and set up a new [Security Group](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AWSEC2/latest/UserGuide/using-network-security.html)
 called `docassembleSg` with two rules. One rule should allow traffic of “Type” SSH from “Source” Anywhere. The other rule should allow “All traffic” from “Source” Custom IP, where the address is the [CIDR](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Classless_Inter-Domain_Routing)
 address you noted. This will provide the “firewall rules” for your servers so that you can connect to them via SSH and so that they can communicate among each other.

Go to the “Launch Configuration” section of the [EC2 Console](https://console.aws.amazon.com/ec2/home)
 and create a new [Launch Configuration](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/autoscaling/latest/userguide/LaunchConfiguration.html)
 called `docassembleLc`. When it asks what [AMI](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AWSEC2/latest/UserGuide/AMIs.html)
 you want to use, go to “Community AMIs,” search for the keyword “ecs-optimized” and pick the most recent [AMI](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AWSEC2/latest/UserGuide/AMIs.html)
 that comes up. The [AMI](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AWSEC2/latest/UserGuide/AMIs.html)
s will not be listed in any useful order, so you have to look carefully at the names, which contain the dates the [AMI](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AWSEC2/latest/UserGuide/AMIs.html)
s were created. There is also a [page on Amazon’s web site](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonECS/latest/developerguide/ecs-optimized_AMI.html)
 that lists the ECS-optimized AMIs by region. As of this writing, the most recent ECS-optimzed [AMI](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AWSEC2/latest/UserGuide/AMIs.html)
 is “amzn-ami-2018.03.p-amazon-ecs-optimized.” If you use a different AMI, make sure that you give the machine at least 30GB of storage.

In the “Choose Instance Type” section, select an instance type that has at least 2GB of RAM (e.g., t2.small).

In the “Configure details” section, set the “IAM role” of the [Launch Configuration](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/autoscaling/latest/userguide/LaunchConfiguration.html)
 to `docassembleInstanceRole`, the [IAM](https://aws.amazon.com/iam/)
 role you created earlier. open the “Advanced Details” and add the following as “User data”:

    Content-Type: multipart/mixed; boundary="===============BOUNDARY=="
    MIME-Version: 1.0
    
    --===============BOUNDARY==
    MIME-Version: 1.0
    Content-Type: text/x-shellscript; charset="us-ascii"
    Content-Transfer-Encoding: 7bit
    Content-Disposition: attachment; filename="standard_userdata.txt"
    
    #!/bin/bash
    sudo chkconfig sendmail off
    sudo service sendmail stop
    
    --===============BOUNDARY==
    MIME-Version: 1.0
    Content-Type: text/cloud-boothook; charset="us-ascii"
    Content-Transfer-Encoding: 7bit
    Content-Disposition: attachment; filename="boothook.txt"
    
    #cloud-boothook
    cloud-init-per once docker_options echo 'OPTIONS="${OPTIONS} --storage-opt dm.basesize=20G"' >> /etc/sysconfig/docker
    
    --===============BOUNDARY==--

The first part of this “user data” is enable the [e-mail receiving](https://docassemble.org/docs/background.html#email)
 feature to work. If [sendmail](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sendmail)
 is running on the EC2 host, as it is by default, then a [Docker](https://docassemble.org/docs/docker.html)
 container running on the host will not be able to bind to the [SMTP](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Simple_Mail_Transfer_Protocol)
 port. The shell script stops and disables the [sendmail](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sendmail)
 service on the host computer. The second part expands the capacity of the [Docker](https://docassemble.org/docs/docker.html)
 container’s “hard drive” from 10GB to 20GB. The default size of 10GB can be too small for some **docassemble** servers.

In the “Configure Security Group” section, set the security group to `docassembleSg`, the [Security Group](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AWSEC2/latest/UserGuide/using-network-security.html)
 you created earlier.

Then go to the “Auto Scaling Groups” section of the [EC2 Console](https://console.aws.amazon.com/ec2/home)
 and create a new [Auto Scaling Group](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AutoScaling/latest/DeveloperGuide/AutoScalingGroup.html)
 called `docassembleAsg`. Connect it with `docassembleLc`, the [Launch Configuration](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/autoscaling/latest/userguide/LaunchConfiguration.html)
 you just created. Use a fixed number of instances without scaling policies that respond to [CloudWatch](https://aws.amazon.com/cloudwatch/)
 alarms. Set the desired number of instances to 3. Once the [Auto Scaling Group](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AutoScaling/latest/DeveloperGuide/AutoScalingGroup.html)
 is saved, [AWS](https://aws.amazon.com/)
 should start running three [EC2](https://aws.amazon.com/ec2/)
 instances. Since you chose an [ECS](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonECS/latest/developerguide/Welcome.html)
\-optimized AMI, the instances should automatically register with your [ECS](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonECS/latest/developerguide/Welcome.html)
 cluster.

The next step is to create an [Application Load Balancer](https://aws.amazon.com/elasticloadbalancing/applicationloadbalancer/)
. The load balancer will accept HTTPS requests from the outside world and forward them to the application servers as HTTP requests. It will forward two types of requests: regular HTTP requests and [WebSocket](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/WebSocket)
 requests. (The [live help](https://docassemble.org/docs/livehelp.html)
 features of **docassemble** use the [WebSocket](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/WebSocket)
 protocol for real time communication between operators and users.) The [Application Load Balancer](https://aws.amazon.com/elasticloadbalancing/applicationloadbalancer/)
 needs to treat [WebSocket](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/WebSocket)
 requests differently. While regular [HTTP](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hypertext_Transfer_Protocol)
 requests can be forwarded randomly to any application server, [WebSocket](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/WebSocket)
 requests for a given session need to be forwarded to the same server every time.

To set up the [Application Load Balancer](https://aws.amazon.com/elasticloadbalancing/applicationloadbalancer/)
, first you need to create a [Security Group](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AWSEC2/latest/UserGuide/using-network-security.html)
 that will allow communication from the outside world through HTTPS. Go to the “Security Groups” section of the [EC2 Console](https://console.aws.amazon.com/ec2/home)
 and create a new [Security Group](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AWSEC2/latest/UserGuide/using-network-security.html)
. Set the “Security group name” to `docassembleLbSg` and set the “Description” to “docassemble load balancer security group.” Attach it to your default [VPC](https://aws.amazon.com/vpc/)
. Add two “Inbound” rules to allow HTTP and HTTPS traffic from anywhere. For the first rule, set “Type” to HTTPS and “Source” to “Anywhere.” For the second rule, set “Type” to HTTP and “Source” to “Anywhere.”

Then go to the “Load Balancing” -> “Target Groups” section of the [EC2 Console](https://console.aws.amazon.com/ec2/home)
 and create a new “Target Group.” Set the “Target group name” to `web`, set the “Protocol” to HTTP, set the “Port” to 80, and set the “VPC” to your default [VPC](https://aws.amazon.com/vpc/)
. Under “Health check settings,” set the “Protocol” to HTTP and the “Path” to `/health_check`. The “health check” is the load balancer’s way of telling whether a web server is healthy. The path `/health_check` on a **docassemble** web server is a page that responds with a simple “OK.” (All the load balancer cares about is whether the page returns an [HTTP success code](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_HTTP_status_codes#2xx_Success)
 or not.) Under “Advanced health check settings,” set the “Healthy threshold” to 10, “Unhealthy threshold” to 2, “Timeout” to 10 seconds, “Interval” to 120 seconds, and keep other settings at their defaults. Then click “Create.”

Once that “Target Group” is created, create a second “Target Group” called `websocket` with the same settings. Then, once the `websocket` “Target Group” is created, do Actions -> Edit Attributes on it, and under “Stickiness,” select “Enable load balancer generated cookie stickiness.” Keep other settings at their defaults. The “stickiness” duration should be 1 day or more (this should be long compared to the typical session duration). Then click “Create.”

Finally, create a third “Target Group” called `http-redirect`. Set the “Protocol” to HTTP, set the “Port” to 8081, and set the “VPC” to your default [VPC](https://aws.amazon.com/vpc/)
. Under “Health check settings,” set the “Protocol” to HTTP and the “Path” to `/health_check`. Under “Advanced health check settings,” change “Success codes” from 200 to 301. This is because all requests to this target group should respond with an [HTTP redirect](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/HTTP_301)
 response, the code for which is 301. Then click “Create.”

The purpose of the `http-redirect` target group is very limited: it will forward any HTTP requests to a special port on your **docassemble** web server, which will return a special “redirect” response that causes the user’s browser to be redirected back to the HTTPS listener on your load balancer.

Then go to the “Load Balancers” section of the [EC2 Console](https://console.aws.amazon.com/ec2/home)
 and create a “Load Balancer.” Select “Application Load Balancer” as the type of load balancer.

On the “Configure Load Balancer” page, set the name to `docassembleLb`. Keep the “Scheme” as “internet-facing” and keep the “IP Address Type” set to “ipv4.” Under “Listeners,” keep the HTTP listener listening to port 80 and click “Add listener” to add a second listener. Set the “Load Balancer Protocol” to HTTPS and set the “Load Balancer Port” to port 443.

Under “Availability Zones,” make sure your default [VPC](https://aws.amazon.com/vpc/)
 is selected. Then select all of the “available subnets” by clicking the plus buttons next to each one. (If it gives you any trouble about adding subnets, just add as many subnets as it will let you add.)

On the “Configure Security Settings” page, it will ask about SSL certificates and security policies. If you have never been here before, accept all of the defaults. This should result in [Amazon](https://amazon.com/)
 creating an SSL certificate for you. If you have created certificates in the past click “Choose a certificate from ACM” and select the existing certificate you want to use, or click the link to “Request a new certificate from ACM.”

On the “Configure Security Groups” page, select the `docassembleLbSg` [Security Group](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AWSEC2/latest/UserGuide/using-network-security.html)
 you created earlier. Select it as the only [Security Group](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AWSEC2/latest/UserGuide/using-network-security.html)
 for the load balancer.

On the “Configure Routing” page, set “Target group” to “Existing target group” and select `http-redirect` as the “Name” of the Target Group. The “Protocol” should be HTTP and the Port should be 8081.

Skip past the “Register Targets” page. On the “Review” page, click “Create” to create the Load Balancer.

Note that load balancers, like [EC2](https://aws.amazon.com/ec2/)
 instances, will cost you money even you are not using them. The cost is $5 per month or more. So if you are not using your load balancer, delete it. You do not need to delete the other resources you created (e.g., target groups, security groups) because they do not cost money to maintain.

Once the `docassembleLb` load balancer is created, you need to make a few manual changes to it.

In the “Load Balancers” section, select the `docassembleLb` load balancer, and open the “Listeners” tab. Select the “HTTP : 80” listener and click Edit. Under “Default action(s),” make sure that step 1 is “Forward to http-redirect.”

Once those changes (if any) are saved, select the “HTTPS : 443” listener and click Edit.

Under “Default action(s),” it will incorrectly say that requests should be forwarded to the `http-redirect` target group. This is the proper setting for HTTP (port 80), but not for HTTPS (port 443), so you need to change it. Click the edit button (pencil icon) and change it so that it forwards to the `web` target group. Then press “Update” to save your changes.

Then go back to the list of listeners, and under “HTTPS : 443,” click “View/edit rules.” Click the “+” button at the top of the screen to add a new rule. Make it the first rule in the list of rules. Construct the new rule so that it says, in effect, “if the path is `/ws/*`, forward the request to the `websocket` target group.” Then click “Save.” Your rules should look like this (keeping in mind that [AWS](https://aws.amazon.com/)
 might have changed its user interface since the time this documentation was written).

![HTTPS listener rules](https://docassemble.org/img/aws-lb-443-rules.png)

Now the `docassembleLb` load balancer will listen to port 443 and act on requests according to two “Rules.” The first rule says that if the path of the HTTPS request starts with `/ws/`, which is **docassemble**’s path for [WebSocket](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/WebSocket)
 communication, then the request will be forwarded using the `websocket` “Target Group,” which has the “stickiness” feature enabled. The second rule says that all other traffic will use the `web` “Target Group,” for which “stickiness” is not enabled. Your `docassembleLb` load balancer will also listen to port 80 and forward all those requests to port 8081 on your web servers, which will respond by redirecting the user to port 443 of your load balancer.

Finally, go to the “Description” tab for the `docassembleLb` load balancer and make note of the “DNS name.” It will be something like `docassemblelb-174526082.us-west-1.elb.amazonaws.com`.

Edit the [DNS](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Domain_Name_System)
 configuration of your domain and create a [CNAME](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/CNAME_record)
 that maps the [DNS](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Domain_Name_System)
 name that your users will use (e.g., `docassemble.example.com`) to the [DNS](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Domain_Name_System)
 name of the load balancer (e.g., `docassemblelb-174526082.us-west-1.elb.amazonaws.com`). This will allow your users to connect to the load balancer.

Then go to the [ECS Console](https://console.aws.amazon.com/ecs/home)
.

Create a [Task Definition](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonECS/latest/developerguide/task_defintions.html)
 called `backend` using the [JSON](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/JSON)
 configuration below. Edit the [`TIMEZONE`](https://docassemble.org/docs/docker.html#TIMEZONE)
, [`DAHOSTNAME`](https://docassemble.org/docs/docker.html#DAHOSTNAME)
, and [`S3BUCKET`](https://docassemble.org/docs/scalability.html#S3BUCKET)
 environment variables.

    {
      "family": "backend",
      "containerDefinitions": [\
        {\
          "name": "backend",\
          "image": "jhpyle/docassemble",\
          "cpu": 1,\
          "memory": 1800,\
          "portMappings": [\
            {\
              "containerPort": 8082,\
              "hostPort": 8082\
            },\
            {\
              "containerPort": 25,\
              "hostPort": 25\
            },\
            {\
              "containerPort": 5432,\
              "hostPort": 5432\
            },\
            {\
              "containerPort": 514,\
              "hostPort": 514\
            },\
            {\
              "containerPort": 6379,\
              "hostPort": 6379\
            },\
            {\
              "containerPort": 4369,\
              "hostPort": 4369\
            },\
            {\
              "containerPort": 5671,\
              "hostPort": 5671\
            },\
            {\
              "containerPort": 5672,\
              "hostPort": 5672\
            },\
            {\
              "containerPort": 25672,\
              "hostPort": 25672\
            },\
            {\
              "containerPort": 9001,\
              "hostPort": 9001\
            }\
          ],\
          "essential": true,\
          "environment": [\
            {\
              "name": "CONTAINERROLE",\
              "value": "sql:redis:rabbitmq:log:cron:mail"\
            },\
            {\
              "name": "DAHOSTNAME",\
              "value": "docassemble.example.com"\
            },\
            {\
              "name": "EC2",\
              "value": "true"\
            },\
            {\
              "name": "BEHINDHTTPSLOADBALANCER",\
              "value": "true"\
            },\
            {\
              "name": "TIMEZONE",\
              "value": "America/New_York"\
            },\
            {\
              "name": "S3BUCKET",\
              "value": "docassemble-example-com"\
            }\
          ],\
          "mountPoints": []\
        }\
      ],
      "volumes": []
    }

The [`CONTAINERROLE`](https://docassemble.org/docs/scalability.html#CONTAINERROLE)
 variable indicates that this server will serve the functions of SQL, [Redis](https://redis.io/)
, [RabbitMQ](https://www.rabbitmq.com/)
, and log message aggregation. The [`DAHOSTNAME`](https://docassemble.org/docs/docker.html#DAHOSTNAME)
 variable indicates the hostname at which the user will access the **docassemble** application. The `EC2` variable instructs **docassemble** that it will be running on [EC2](https://aws.amazon.com/ec2/)
, which means that the server needs to use a special method of obtaining its own [fully-qualified domain name](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fully_qualified_domain_name)
.

Then, check the [ECS Console](https://console.aws.amazon.com/ecs/home)
 and look at the `default` [cluster](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonECS/latest/developerguide/ECS_clusters.html)
. There should be three [container instances](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonECS/latest/developerguide/ECS_instances.html)
 available. (If not, something is wrong with the [Auto Scaling Group](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AutoScaling/latest/DeveloperGuide/AutoScalingGroup.html)
 you created, or perhaps the instances are still starting up.)

Then, create a Service called `backend` that uses the task definition you just created. Set the number of tasks to 1. Do not choose an [Elastic Load Balancer](https://aws.amazon.com/elasticloadbalancing/)
. This will cause the **docassemble** [Docker](https://docassemble.org/docs/docker.html)
 image to be installed on one of the [container instances](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonECS/latest/developerguide/ECS_instances.html)
. It will take some time for the image to download and start. In the meantime, the [ECS Console](https://console.aws.amazon.com/ecs/home)
 should show one “pending task.”

Next, create a [Task Definition](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonECS/latest/developerguide/task_defintions.html)
 called `app` using the [JSON](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/JSON)
 configuration below. Edit the [`S3BUCKET`](https://docassemble.org/docs/scalability.html#S3BUCKET)
 environment variable.

    {
      "family": "app",
      "containerDefinitions": [\
        {\
          "name": "app",\
          "image": "jhpyle/docassemble",\
          "cpu": 1,\
          "memory": 1800,\
          "portMappings": [\
            {\
              "containerPort": 80,\
              "hostPort": 80\
            },\
            {\
              "containerPort": 8081,\
              "hostPort": 8081\
            },\
            {\
              "containerPort": 9001,\
              "hostPort": 9001\
            }\
          ],\
          "essential": true,\
          "environment": [\
            {\
              "name": "CONTAINERROLE",\
              "value": "web:celery"\
            },\
            {\
              "name": "S3BUCKET",\
              "value": "docassemble-example-com"\
            }\
          ],\
          "mountPoints": []\
        }\
      ],
      "volumes": []
    }

Create a Service called `app` that uses the task definition `app`. Set the “Number of tasks” to 2. Under “Elastic load balancing,” click the “Configure ELB” button. Set “ELB type” to “Elastic Load Balancer.” The “IAM role for service” should be `ecsServiceRole`, an [IAM](https://aws.amazon.com/iam/)
 role that [ECS](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonECS/latest/developerguide/Welcome.html)
 creates for you automatically. Set “ELB Name” to `docassembleLb`, the name of the [Application Load Balancer](https://aws.amazon.com/elasticloadbalancing/applicationloadbalancer/)
 you created earlier. Under “Container to load balance,” select “app:80:80” and click “Add to ELB.” Set the “Target group name” to `web`, the “Target Group” you created earlier. Set the “Health check grace period” to 1200 seconds. Then click “Save.”

Note that the `app` task definition is much briefer than the `backend` task definition. This is because the `backend` service will save a configuration file in the [S3](https://aws.amazon.com/s3/)
 bucket. All that the `app` service needs to do is retrieve that configuration file. Note also that it is not necessary to include any secret keys in the [JSON](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/JSON)
 configuration. This is because the [Launch Configuration](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/autoscaling/latest/userguide/LaunchConfiguration.html)
 of your virtual machines includes the “IAM Role” of `docassembleInstanceRole`; the virtual machines themselves are authorized to access the [S3](https://aws.amazon.com/s3/)
 bucket.

Just one more thing needs to be done to make the **docassemble** server fully functional: you need to associate the `websocket` and `http-redirect` “Target Groups” with the same [EC2](https://aws.amazon.com/ec2/)
 instances that are associated with the `web` “Target Group.” (Unfortunately, this is not something that the [ECS](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonECS/latest/developerguide/Welcome.html)
 system can do automatically yet.) To fix this, go to the [EC2 Console](https://console.aws.amazon.com/ec2/home)
, go to the “Target Groups” section, select the `web` Target Group, go to the “Targets” tab, and note the Instance IDs of the “Registered Instances.” Now de-select `web`, select `websocket`, and click the “Edit” button within the “Targets” tab. On the “Register and deregister instances” page that appears, select the instances you just noted and click the “Add to registered” button. Then do the same with `http-redirect`.

### Shutting down[¶](https://docassemble.org/docs/scalability.html#ecs%20shutdown)

To shut down a multi-server **docassemble** configuration:

#. Go to the [ECS Console](https://console.aws.amazon.com/ecs/home)
. Go into the `default` cluster, and update the “app” [ECS](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonECS/latest/developerguide/Welcome.html)
 service. Set “Number of tasks” to zero. This will gracefully stop the [Docker](https://docassemble.org/docs/docker.html)
 containers that serve in the role of application servers. #. Wait until the tasks have stopped. #. Update the “backend” [ECS](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonECS/latest/developerguide/Welcome.html)
 service. Set “Number of tasks” to zero. This will stop the [Docker](https://docassemble.org/docs/docker.html)
 container that provides centralized services. #. Wait until the task has stopped. #. The **docassemble** application is now fully shut down. However, you are still spending money because there are [EC2](https://aws.amazon.com/ec2/)
 instances running. To shut down these instances, go to the [EC2 Console](https://console.aws.amazon.com/ec2/home)
 and edit the [Auto Scaling Group](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AutoScaling/latest/DeveloperGuide/AutoScalingGroup.html)
 you created (`docassembleAsg`). Set the desired number of instances to 0. This will cause the three instances you created to shut down. #. Now you are no longer paying money to keep instances running, but you are still paying to keep the load balancer alive (approximately $5 per month). To avoid this cost, go to the “Load Balancers” section of the [EC2 Console](https://console.aws.amazon.com/ec2/home)
 and delete the `docassembleLb` load balancer. #. If you do not care about retaining the database or configuration of the **docassemble** application you just shut down, go to the [S3 Console](https://console.aws.amazon.com/s3/home)
 and delete the bucket you created. Note that the cost of maintaining data in [S3](https://aws.amazon.com/s3/)
 is minimal as long as the volume of data is low. The data that **docassemble** stores in [S3](https://aws.amazon.com/s3/)
 will only be significant if you have a lot of uploaded files or a lot of static files in your [Playground](https://docassemble.org/docs/playground.html)
.

After you have shut down these resources, many other [AWS](https://aws.amazon.com/)
 resources that you created will still exist, but you do not need to delete them to avoid incurring costs. “Target groups” and “Launch Configurations,” for example, are just configuration data; [AWS](https://aws.amazon.com/)
 does not need to reserve computing power or IP addresses to maintain them in your account.

### Controlling AWS from the command line[¶](https://docassemble.org/docs/scalability.html#awscli)

In the **docassemble** [GitHub repository](https://github.com/jhpyle/docassemble)
, there is a command-line utility called [`da-cli`](https://github.com/jhpyle/docassemble/blob/master/da-cli)
 (which is short for “**docassemble** command line interface”) that you can use to manage your multi-server configuration on [AWS](https://aws.amazon.com/)
.

It depends on the [boto3](https://boto3.readthedocs.io/en/latest/)
 library, so you may need to run `sudo pip install boto3` in order for it to work. You need to initialize [boto3](https://boto3.readthedocs.io/en/latest/)
 by running `aws configure`.

Here are some ways that the command-line application can be used:

*   `./da-cli start_up 2` - bring up two `app` services and one `backend` service after bringing up three [EC2](https://aws.amazon.com/ec2/)
     instances with the `docassembleAsg` [Auto Scaling Group](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AutoScaling/latest/DeveloperGuide/AutoScalingGroup.html)
    . It also registers the appropriate instances for the `websocket` Target Group.
*   `./da-cli shut_down` - bring the count of the `app` and `backend` services down to zero, then bring the `docassembleAsg` [Auto Scaling Group](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AutoScaling/latest/DeveloperGuide/AutoScalingGroup.html)
     count down to zero.
*   `./da-cli shutdown_unused_ec2_instances` - finds [EC2](https://aws.amazon.com/ec2/)
     instances in the `docassembleAsg` [Auto Scaling Group](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AutoScaling/latest/DeveloperGuide/AutoScalingGroup.html)
     that are not being used to host a service, and terminates them.
*   `./da-cli update_ec2_instances 4` - increases the `docassembleAsg` [Auto Scaling Group](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AutoScaling/latest/DeveloperGuide/AutoScalingGroup.html)
     count to 4 and waits for the instances to become available.
*   `./da-cli connect_string app` - prints a command that you can use to SSH to each [EC2](https://aws.amazon.com/ec2/)
     instance running the service `app`.
*   `./da-cli fix_web_sockets` - registers target instances for the `websocket` Target Group.
*   `./da-cli update_desired_count app 4` - increases the desired count for the `app` service to 4.

If your [AWS](https://aws.amazon.com/)
 resources go by names different from those in the above setup instructions, you can easily edit the `da-cli` script, which is a simple [Python](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Python_%28programming_language%29)
 module. You might also want to extend the functionality of the script; anything that can be done in the [AWS](https://aws.amazon.com/)
 web interface can be done using the [boto3](https://boto3.readthedocs.io/en/latest/)
 library.

Single-server configuration on EC2 Container Service[¶](https://docassemble.org/docs/scalability.html#singleserver)

====================================================================================================================

If you want, you can use [EC2](https://aws.amazon.com/ec2/)
 to run **docassemble** in a single-server arrangement. (It is not particularly “scalable” to do so, but you might find it convenient.)

To do so, follow the instructions above for the multi-server configuration, but skip the creation of the [S3](https://aws.amazon.com/s3/)
 bucket, [IAM](https://aws.amazon.com/iam/)
 role, [Application Load Balancer](https://aws.amazon.com/elasticloadbalancing/applicationloadbalancer/)
, Target Groups, [Auto Scaling Group](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AutoScaling/latest/DeveloperGuide/AutoScalingGroup.html)
, [Task Definition](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonECS/latest/developerguide/task_defintions.html)
s, and the [ECS](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonECS/latest/developerguide/Welcome.html)
 services.

Instead, just [start an EC2 instance using an ECS-optimized AMI](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonECS/latest/developerguide/launch_container_instance.html)
 with a [Security Group](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AWSEC2/latest/UserGuide/using-network-security.html)
 that allows incoming connections over [HTTP](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hypertext_Transfer_Protocol)
 (port 80). Then go to the [ECS Console](https://console.aws.amazon.com/ecs/home)
 and set up a “service” called `docassemble` (or whatever name you want to give it) that runs a single “task” with a [Task Definition](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonECS/latest/developerguide/task_defintions.html)
 like the following.

    {
      "family": "docassemble-all",
      "containerDefinitions": [\
        {\
          "name": "docassemble-all",\
          "image": "jhpyle/docassemble",\
          "memory": 900,\
          "cpu": 1,\
          "portMappings": [\
            {\
              "containerPort": 80,\
              "hostPort": 80\
            }\
          ],\
          "essential": true,\
          "environment": [\
            {\
              "name": "EC2",\
              "value": "true"\
            }\
          ]\
        }\
      ]
    }

To start up the server, you would configure the [ECS](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonECS/latest/developerguide/Welcome.html)
 service to start one task. To shut down the server, you would reduce the number of tasks to 0.

A more complicated example of a configuration is the following, which assumes that you have a domain name that is mapped to your [EC2](https://aws.amazon.com/ec2/)
 instance, that you want to use [HTTPS](https://docassemble.org/docs/docker.html#https)
, and you have set up the [S3](https://aws.amazon.com/s3/)
 bucket and [IAM](https://aws.amazon.com/iam/)
 role so that your server can use [S3 persistent storage](https://docassemble.org/docs/docker.html#persistent%20s3)
.

    {
      "family": "docassemble-all",
      "containerDefinitions": [\
        {\
          "name": "docassemble-all",\
          "image": "jhpyle/docassemble",\
          "memory": 900,\
          "cpu": 1,\
          "portMappings": [\
            {\
              "containerPort": 80,\
              "hostPort": 80\
            },\
            {\
              "containerPort": 443,\
              "hostPort": 443\
            }\
          ],\
          "essential": true,\
          "environment": [\
            {\
              "name": "DAHOSTNAME",\
              "value": "docassemble.example.com"\
            },\
            {\
              "name": "EC2",\
              "value": "true"\
            },\
            {\
              "name": "USEHTTPS",\
              "value": "true"\
            },\
            {\
              "name": "USELETSENCRYPT",\
              "value": "true"\
            },\
            {\
              "name": "LETSENCRYPTEMAIL",\
              "value": "admin@example.com"\
            },\
            {\
              "name": "S3BUCKET",\
              "value": "docassemble-example-com"\
            }\
          ]\
        }\
      ]
    }

In this example, you would need to edit [`DAHOSTNAME`](https://docassemble.org/docs/docker.html#DAHOSTNAME)
, [`LETSENCRYPTEMAIL`](https://docassemble.org/docs/docker.html#LETSENCRYPTEMAIL)
, and [`S3BUCKET`](https://docassemble.org/docs/scalability.html#S3BUCKET)
. Note that [`CONTAINERROLE`](https://docassemble.org/docs/scalability.html#CONTAINERROLE)
 is not specified; it will default to `all`.

For more information about configuring your server, see the section on [Docker](https://docassemble.org/docs/docker.html)
.

How it works
============

Each **docassemble** application server reads its [configuration](https://docassemble.org/docs/config.html)
 from `/usr/share/docassemble/config/config.yml`. The default configuration for connecting to the central SQL database is:

    db:
      prefix: postgresql+psycopg2://
      name: docassemble
      user: docassemble
      password: abc123
      host: localhost

This will cause **docassemble** to connect to [PostgreSQL](https://www.postgresql.org/)
 on the local machine as the user “docassemble” and open the database “docassemble.” This configuration can be modified to connect to a remote server. If you use a remote SQL server that is not a [Docker](https://docassemble.org/docs/docker.html)
 container running the [`CONTAINERROLE`](https://docassemble.org/docs/scalability.html#CONTAINERROLE)
 `sql`, then you need to make sure that you create the user and database first, and give permission to the user to create tables in the database.

By default, the [Redis](https://redis.io/)
 server is assumed to be found at `redis://localhost` and the [RabbitMQ](https://www.rabbitmq.com/)
 server at `pyamqp://guest@your.hostname.local//`, where `your.hostname.local` is the value of [`socket.gethostname()`](https://docs.python.org/3/library/socket.html#socket.gethostname)
. However, you can modify the locations of the [Redis](https://redis.io/)
 server and [RabbitMQ](https://www.rabbitmq.com/)
 server in the [configuration](https://docassemble.org/docs/config.html)
 using the [`redis`](https://docassemble.org/docs/config.html#redis)
 and [`rabbitmq`](https://docassemble.org/docs/config.html#rabbitmq)
 directives:

    redis: redis://redis.example.local
    rabbitmq: pyamqp://guest@rabbit.example.local//'

Log file aggregation[¶](https://docassemble.org/docs/scalability.html#log)

---------------------------------------------------------------------------

In a multi-server configuration, log files can be centralized and aggregated by using [Syslog-ng](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Syslog-ng)
 on the application servers to forward local log files to port 514 on a central server, which in turn runs [Syslog-ng](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Syslog-ng)
 to listen to port 514 and write what it hears to files in `/usr/share/docassemble/log`. The hostname of this central server is set using the [`log server`](https://docassemble.org/docs/config.html#log%20server)
 directive in the [configuration](https://docassemble.org/docs/config.html)
:

    log server: log.example.local

If this directive is set, **docassemble** will write its own log messages to port 514 on the central server rather than appending to `/usr/share/docassemble/log/docassemble.log`.

When [`log server`](https://docassemble.org/docs/config.html#log%20server)
 is set, the “Logs” page of the web interface will call http://log.example.local:8082/ to get a list of available log files, and will retrieve the content of files by accessing URLs like http://log.example.local:8082/docassemble.log.

The following files make this possible:

*   [`Docker/config/nginx-log.dist`](https://github.com/jhpyle/docassemble/blob/master/config/nginx-log.dist)
     - template for an \[NGINX\] site configuration file that listens to port 8082.
*   [`Docker/docassemble-syslog-ng.conf`](https://github.com/jhpyle/docassemble/blob/master/Docker/docassemble-syslog-ng.conf)
     - on application servers, this is copied into `/etc/syslog-ng/conf.d/docassemble`. It depends on an environment variable `LOGSERVER` which should be set to the hostname of the central log server (e.g., `log.example.local`). It causes log messages to be forwarded to port 514 on the central log server. Note that [Syslog-ng](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Syslog-ng)
     needs to run in an environment where the `LOGSERVER` environment variable is defined, or the file needs to be edited to include the hostname explicitly.
*   [`Docker/syslog-ng.conf`](https://github.com/jhpyle/docassemble/blob/master/Docker/syslog-ng.conf)
     - on the central log server, this is copied to `/etc/syslog-ng/syslog-ng.conf`. It causes [Syslog-ng](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Syslog-ng)
     to listen to port 514 and copy messages to files in `/usr/share/docassemble/log/`.

Auto-discovery of services[¶](https://docassemble.org/docs/scalability.html#autodiscovery)

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

If you use [S3](https://aws.amazon.com/s3/)
/[Azure blob storage](https://azure.microsoft.com/en-us/services/storage/blobs/)
, you can can use **docassemble** in a multi-server configuration without manually specifying the hostnames of central services in the [configuration](https://docassemble.org/docs/config.html)
 file.

If any of the following [configuration](https://docassemble.org/docs/config.html)
 directives are `null` or undefined, and [S3](https://aws.amazon.com/s3/)
/[Azure blob storage](https://azure.microsoft.com/en-us/services/storage/blobs/)
 is enabled, then a **docassemble** application server will try to “autodiscover” the hostname of the service.

*   [`host`](https://docassemble.org/docs/config.html#db%20host)
     in the [`db`](https://docassemble.org/docs/config.html#db)
     section
*   [`redis`](https://docassemble.org/docs/config.html#redis)
    
*   [`rabbitmq`](https://docassemble.org/docs/config.html#rabbitmq)
    
*   [`log server`](https://docassemble.org/docs/config.html#log%20server)
    

**docassemble** will look for keys in the [S3 bucket](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonS3/latest/dev/UsingBucket.html)
 or [Azure blob storage](https://azure.microsoft.com/en-us/services/storage/blobs/)
 container called:

*   `hostname-sql`
*   `hostname-redis`
*   `hostname-rabbitmq`
*   `hostname-log`

If a key is defined, **docassemble** will assume that the value is a hostname, and will set the corresponding [configuration](https://docassemble.org/docs/config.html)
 variable appropriately (e.g., by adding a `redis://` prefix to the [Redis](https://redis.io/)
 hostname).

The [Docker](https://docassemble.org/docs/docker.html)
 initialization script runs the [`docassemble.webapp.cloud_register`](https://github.com/jhpyle/docassemble/blob/master/docassemble_webapp/docassemble/webapp/cloud_register.py)
 module, which writes the hostname to the appropriate [S3](https://aws.amazon.com/s3/)
 keys/[Azure blob storage](https://azure.microsoft.com/en-us/services/storage/blobs/)
 objects depending on the value of the environment variable `CONTAINERROLE`.

File sharing[¶](https://docassemble.org/docs/scalability.html#fileshare)

-------------------------------------------------------------------------

Configuring a cluster of **docassemble** servers requires centralizing the location of uploaded files, by using an [Amazon S3](https://aws.amazon.com/s3/)
 bucket (the [`s3` configuration setting](https://docassemble.org/docs/config.html#s3)
), using an [Azure blob storage](https://azure.microsoft.com/en-us/services/storage/blobs/)
 container (the [`azure` configuration setting](https://docassemble.org/docs/config.html#azure)
), or by making the uploaded file directory a network drive mount (the [`uploads`](https://docassemble.org/docs/config.html#uploads)
 configuration setting).

For more information about using [S3](https://aws.amazon.com/s3/)
 and [Azure blob storage](https://azure.microsoft.com/en-us/services/storage/blobs/)
 for file sharing, see the [file sharing](https://docassemble.org/docs/docker.html#file%20sharing)
 and [data storage](https://docassemble.org/docs/docker.html#data%20storage)
 sections of the [Docker](https://docassemble.org/docs/docker.html)
 page.

The default location of uploaded user files is defined by the `uploads` [configuration](https://docassemble.org/docs/config.html)
 setting:

    uploads: /usr/share/docassemble/files

However, **docassemble** will use [Amazon S3](https://aws.amazon.com/s3/)
 instead of this folder if [S3](https://aws.amazon.com/s3/)
 is enabled as follows:

    s3:
      enable: True
