/[Azure blob storage](https://docassemble.org/docs/docker.html#persistent%20azure)
, you can edit these configuration files in the cloud and then stop and start your container for the new configuration to take effect.

*   `DAWEBSERVER`: This can be set either to `nginx` (the default) or `apache`. See the [`web server`](https://docassemble.org/docs/config.html#web%20server)
     configuration directive.
*   `DBHOST`: The hostname of the [PostgreSQL](https://www.postgresql.org/)
     server. Keep undefined or set to `null` in order to use the [PostgreSQL](https://www.postgresql.org/)
     server on the same host. This environment variable, along with others that begin with `DB`, populates values in [`db` section](https://docassemble.org/docs/config.html#db)
     of the [configuration](https://docassemble.org/docs/config.html)
     file. If you are using a managed SQL database service, set `DBHOST` to the hostname of the database service. If you are using [PostgreSQL](https://www.postgresql.org/)
     and the database referenced by `DBNAME` does not exist on the SQL server, the [Docker](https://www.docker.com/)
     startup process will attempt to use the `DBUSER` and `DBPASSWORD` credentials to create the database. Otherwise, you need to make sure the database by the name of `DBNAME` exists before **docassemble** starts.
*   `DBNAME`: The name of the database. The default is `docassemble`.
*   `DBUSER`: The username for connecting to the [PostgreSQL](https://www.postgresql.org/)
     server. The default is `docassemble`.
*   `DBPASSWORD`: The password for connecting to the SQL server. The default is `abc123`. Valid characters include alphanumeric characters, `_`, `~`, `/`, `-`, `^`, `*`, `?`, and `!`.
*   `DBPREFIX`: This sets the prefix for the database specifier. The default is `postgresql+psycopg2://`. This corresponds with the `prefix` of the [`db`](https://docassemble.org/docs/config.html#db)
     configuration directive.
*   `DBPORT`: This sets the port that **docassemble** will use to access the SQL server. If you are using the default port for your database backend, you do not need to set this.
*   `DBTABLEPREFIX`: This allows multiple separate **docassemble** implementations to share the same SQL database. The value is a prefix to be added to each table in the database.
*   `DBBACKUP`: Set this to `false` if you are using **docassemble** in a multi-server configuration, you are using an external [PostgreSQL](https://www.postgresql.org/)
     `DBHOST` (i.e. a managed SQL server like AWS RDS) and you do not want the database to be backed up to the rolling backup by the daily cron job. This is important if the off-site SQL database is large compared to your available disk space. The default value is `true`.
*   `DBSSLMODE`: This is relevant if you have a [PostgreSQL](https://www.postgresql.org/)
     database and you have an SSL certificate for it. This sets the `sslmode` parameter. For more information, see the documentation for the [`db` section](https://docassemble.org/docs/config.html#db)
     of the [Configuration](https://docassemble.org/docs/config.html)
    .
*   `DBSSLCERT`: This is relevant if you have a [PostgreSQL](https://www.postgresql.org/)
     database and you have an SSL certificate for it. This is the name of a certificate file. For more information, see the documentation for the [`db` section](https://docassemble.org/docs/config.html#db)
     of the [Configuration](https://docassemble.org/docs/config.html)
    .
*   `DBSSLKEY`: This is relevant if you have a [PostgreSQL](https://www.postgresql.org/)
     database and you have an SSL certificate for it. This is the name of a certificate key file. For more information, see the documentation for the [`db` section](https://docassemble.org/docs/config.html#db)
     of the [Configuration](https://docassemble.org/docs/config.html)
    .
*   `DBSSLROOTCERT`: This is relevant if you have a [PostgreSQL](https://www.postgresql.org/)
     database and you have an SSL certificate for it. This is the name of a root certificate file. For more information, see the documentation for the [`db` section](https://docassemble.org/docs/config.html#db)
     of the [Configuration](https://docassemble.org/docs/config.html)
    .
*   `DASQLPING`: If your docassemble server runs in an environment in which persistent SQL connections will periodically be severed, you can set `DASQLPING: true` in order to avoid errors. There is an overhead cost to using this, so only enable this if you get SQL errors when trying to connect after a period of inactivity. The default is `false`. See the [`sql ping`](https://docassemble.org/docs/config.html#sql%20ping)
     configuration directive.
*   `EC2`: Set this to `true` if you are running [Docker](https://www.docker.com/)
     on [EC2](https://aws.amazon.com/ec2/)
    . This tells **docassemble** that it can use an [EC2](https://aws.amazon.com/ec2/)
    \-specific method of determining the hostname of the server on which it is running. See the [`ec2`](https://docassemble.org/docs/config.html#ec2)
     configuration directive.
*   `COLLECTSTATISTICS`: Set this to `true` if you want the server to use [Redis](https://redis.io/)
     to track the number of interview sessions initiated. See the [`collect statistics`](https://docassemble.org/docs/config.html#collect%20statistics)
     configuration directive.
*   `KUBERNETES`: Set this to `true` if you are running inside [Kubernetes](https://kubernetes.io/)
    . This tells **docassemble** that it can use the IP address of the Pod in place of the hostname. See the [`kubernetes`](https://docassemble.org/docs/config.html#kubernetes)
     configuration directive.
*   `USEHTTPS`: Set this to `true` if you would like **docassemble** to communicate with the browser using encryption. Read the [HTTPS](https://docassemble.org/docs/docker.html#https)
     section for more information. Defaults to `false`. See the [`use https`](https://docassemble.org/docs/config.html#use%20https)
     configuration directive. Do not set this to `true` if you are using a proxy server that forwards non-encrypted [HTTP](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hypertext_Transfer_Protocol)
     to your server; in that case, see the [`BEHINDHTTPSLOADBALANCER`](https://docassemble.org/docs/docker.html#BEHINDHTTPSLOADBALANCER)
     variable below.
*   `DAHOSTNAME`: Set this to the hostname by which web browsers can find **docassemble**. This is necessary for [HTTPS](https://docassemble.org/docs/docker.html#https)
     to function. See the [`external hostname`](https://docassemble.org/docs/config.html#external%20hostname)
     configuration directive.
*   `USELETSENCRYPT`: Set this to `true` if you are [using Let’s Encrypt](https://docassemble.org/docs/docker.html#letsencrypt)
    . The default is `false`. See the [`use lets encrypt`](https://docassemble.org/docs/config.html#use%20lets%20encrypt)
     configuration directive.
*   `LETSENCRYPTEMAIL`: Set this to the e-mail address you use with [Let’s Encrypt](https://letsencrypt.org/)
    . See the [`lets encrypt email`](https://docassemble.org/docs/config.html#lets%20encrypt%20email)
     configuration directive.
*   `LOGSERVER`: This is used in the [multi-server arrangement](https://docassemble.org/docs/docker.html#multi%20server%20arrangement)
     where there is a separate server for collecting log messages. The default is `none`, which causes the server to run [Syslog-ng](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Syslog-ng)
    . See the [`log server`](https://docassemble.org/docs/config.html#log%20server)
     configuration directive.
*   `REDIS`: If you are running **docassemble** in a [multi-server arrangement](https://docassemble.org/docs/docker.html#multi%20server%20arrangement)
    , set this to `redis://thehostname` where `thehostname` is the host name at which the [Redis](https://redis.io/)
     server can be accessed. See the [`redis`](https://docassemble.org/docs/config.html#redis)
     configuration directive.
*   `RABBITMQ`: If you are running **docassemble** in a [multi-server arrangement](https://docassemble.org/docs/docker.html#multi%20server%20arrangement)
    , set this to the URL at which the [RabbitMQ](https://www.rabbitmq.com/)
     server can be accessed, in the form `pyamqp://guest@rabbitmqserver.local//` or `pyamqp://user:xxsecretpasswdxx@rabbitmqserver.local//`. Note that [RabbitMQ](https://www.rabbitmq.com/)
     is very particular about hostnames. If the [RabbitMQ](https://www.rabbitmq.com/)
     server is running on a machine on which the command `hostname -s` evaluates to `rabbitmqserver.local`, then your application servers will need to use `rabbitmqserver.local` as the hostname in the `RABBITMQ` URL, even if other names resolve to the same IP address. Note that if you run **docassemble** using the instructions in the [scalability](https://docassemble.org/docs/scalability.html)
     section, you may not need to worry about setting `RABBITMQ`. See the [`rabbitmq`](https://docassemble.org/docs/config.html#rabbitmq)
     configuration directive.
*   `DACELERYWORKERS`: By default, the number of Celery workers is based on the number of CPUs on the machine and its total memory. If you want to set a different value, set `DACELERYWORKERS` to integer greater than or equal to 1. See the [`celery processes`](https://docassemble.org/docs/config.html#celery%20processes)
     configuration directive.
*   `DACELERYWORKERS`: By default, the number of Celery workers scales with the number of CPUs on the machine (one process per CPU reported by `nproc`) and is limited by the total memory (one process for every 2GB). If you want the number of Celery workers to scale based on the number of CPUs but the memory-based limit is to restrictive, you can set `DAMAXCELERYWORKERS` to integer greater than or equal to 1. See the [`max celery processes`](https://docassemble.org/docs/config.html#max%20celery%20processes)
     configuration directive.
*   `POSTURLROOT`: If users access **docassemble** at https://docassemble.example.com/da, set `POSTURLROOT` to `/da/`. The trailing slash is important. If users access **docassemble** at https://docassemble.example.com, you can ignore this. The default value is `/`. See the [`root`](https://docassemble.org/docs/config.html#root)
     configuration directive.
*   `BEHINDHTTPSLOADBALANCER`: Set this to `true` if you are using a load balancer or proxy server that accepts connections in HTTPS and forwards them to your server or servers as HTTP. This lets **docassemble** know that when it forms URLs, it should use the `https` scheme even though requests appear to be coming in as HTTP requests, and when it sends cookies, it should set the `secure` flag on the cookies. You also need to make sure that your proxy server is setting the `X-Forwarded-*` HTTP headers when it passes HTTP requests to your server or servers. See the [`behind https load balancer`](https://docassemble.org/docs/config.html#behind%20https%20load%20balancer)
     configuration directive for more information.
*   `DAALLOWUPDATES`: Set this to `false` if you want to disable the updating of software through the user interface. See the [`allow updates`](https://docassemble.org/docs/config.html#allow%20updates)
     configuration directive.
*   `DAUPDATEONSTART`: Set this to `false` if you do not want the container to update its software using `pip` when it starts up. Set `DAUPDATEONSTART` to `initial` if you want the container to update its software during the first `docker run`, but not on every `docker start`. See the [`update on start`](https://docassemble.org/docs/config.html#update%20on%20start)
     configuration directive.
*   `DAROOTOWNED`: Set this to `true` if you are setting `DAALLOWUPDATES=false` and `DAENABLEPLAYGROUND=false` you also want to take the extra step of making the directories containing code owned by `root` so that the web browser user cannot access them. See the [`root owned`](https://docassemble.org/docs/config.html#root%20owned)
     configuration directive.
*   `DAALLOWCONFIGURATIONEDITING`: Set this to `false` to prevent the editing of the Configuration. See the [`allow configuration editing`](https://docassemble.org/docs/config.html#allow%20configuration%20editing)
     configuration directive.
*   `DAENABLEPLAYGROUND`: Set this to `false` to disable the Playground on the server. See the [`enable playground`](https://docassemble.org/docs/config.html#enable%20playground)
     directive.
*   `DAALLOWLOGVIEWING`: Set this to `false` to prevent administrators and developers from viewing the system logs by going to Logs on the menu. By default, this menu is available to administrators and developers.
*   `DADEBUG`: Set this to `false` if you want the server to be in production mode rather than developer mode. This will also disable access to example and demonstration interviews in the `docassemble.base` and `docassemble.demo` packages. See the [`debug`](https://docassemble.org/docs/config.html#debug)
     and [`allow demo`](https://docassemble.org/docs/config.html#allow%20demo)
     configuration directives.
*   `TIMEZONE`: You can use this to set the time zone of the server. The value of the variable is stored in `/etc/timezone` and `dpkg-reconfigure -f noninteractive tzdata` is run in order to set the system time zone. The default is `America/New_York`. The time zone must exactly match the name of a time zone in the [tz database](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_tz_database_time_zones)
    . See the [`timezone`](https://docassemble.org/docs/config.html#timezone)
     configuration directive.
*   `LOCALE`: You can use this to enable a locale on the server. The value needs to exactly match one of the valid [locale values](https://docassemble.org/img/locales.txt)
     that [Ubuntu](https://ubuntu.com/)
    /[Debian](https://www.debian.org/)
     recognizes (listed in the `/etc/locale.gen` file on an [Ubuntu](https://ubuntu.com/)
    /[Debian](https://www.debian.org/)
     system). When the server starts, the value of `LOCALE` is appended to `/etc/locale.gen` and `locale-gen` and `update-locale` are run. The default is `en_US.UTF-8 UTF-8`. See the [`os locale`](https://docassemble.org/docs/config.html#os%20locale)
     configuration directive.
*   `OTHERLOCALES`: You can use this to set up other locales on the system besides the default locale. Set this to a comma separated list of locales. The values need to match entries in `/etc/locale.gen` on [Ubuntu](https://ubuntu.com/)
    . See the [`other os locales`](https://docassemble.org/docs/config.html#other%20os%20locales)
     configuration directive.
*   `PACKAGES`: If your interviews use code that depends on certain [Ubuntu](https://ubuntu.com/)
     packages being installed, you can provide a comma-separated list of [Ubuntu](https://ubuntu.com/)
     packages in the `PACKAGES` environment variable. The packages will be installed when the container is started. See the [`ubuntu packages`](https://docassemble.org/docs/config.html#ubuntu%20packages)
     configuration directive.
*   `PYTHONPACKAGES`: If you want to install certain [Python](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Python_%28programming_language%29)
     packages during the container start process, you can provide a comma-separated list of packages in the `PYTHONPACKAGES` environment variable. See the [`python packages`](https://docassemble.org/docs/config.html#python%20packages)
     configuration directive.
*   `DASECRETKEY`: The secret key for protecting against cross-site forgery. See the [`secretkey`](https://docassemble.org/docs/config.html#secretkey)
     configuration directive. If `DASECRETKEY` is not set, a random secret key will be generated.
*   `DABACKUPDAYS`: The number of days backups should be kept. The default is 14. See the [`backup days`](https://docassemble.org/docs/config.html#backup%20days)
     configuration directive.
*   `DAEXPOSEWEBSOCKETS`: You may need to set this to `true` if you are operating a [Docker](https://www.docker.com/)
     container [behind a reverse proxy](https://docassemble.org/docs/docker.html#forwarding)
     and you want to use the [WebSocket](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/WebSocket)
    \-based [live help](https://docassemble.org/docs/livehelp.html)
     features. See the [`expose websockets`](https://docassemble.org/docs/config.html#expose%20websockets)
     configuration directive.
*   `DAWEBSOCKETSIP`: You can set this if you need to manually specify the address on which the `websockets` service runs. See the [`websockets ip`](https://docassemble.org/docs/config.html#websockets%20ip)
     configuration directive.
*   `DAWEBSOCKETSPORT`: You can set this if you need to manually specify the port on which the `websockets` service runs. See the [`websockets port`](https://docassemble.org/docs/config.html#websockets%20port)
     configuration directive.
*   `PORT`: By default, if you are not using [HTTPS](https://docassemble.org/docs/docker.html#https)
    , the **docassemble** web application runs on port 80. When running Docker, you can map any port on the host to port 80 in the container. However, if you are using a system like Heroku which expects the Docker container to use the `PORT` environment variable, you can set `PORT` in your `env.list` file. See the [`http port`](https://docassemble.org/docs/config.html#http%20port)
     configuration directive.
*   `USEMINIO`: Set this to `true` if you are setting [`S3ENDPOINTURL`](https://docassemble.org/docs/docker.html#S3ENDPOINTURL)
     to point to [MinIO](https://min.io/)
     and you would like the bucket to be created when the container starts. See the [`use minio`](https://docassemble.org/docs/config.html#use%20minio)
     configuration directive.
*   `USECLOUDURLS`: Set this to `false` if you are using cloud storage but you do not want URLs for files to point directly to the cloud storage provider. See the [`use cloud urls`](https://docassemble.org/docs/config.html#use%20cloud%20urls)
     configuration directive.
*   `DASSLPROTOCOLS`: This indicates the SSL protocols that [NGINX](https://www.nginx.com/)
     should accept. The default is `TLSv1.2 TLSv1.3`. You might want to set it to `TLSv1 TLSv1.1 TLSv1.2 TLSv1.3` if you need to support older browsers. The value is passed directly to the [NGINX](https://www.nginx.com/)
     directive [`ssl_protocols`](https://nginx.org/en/docs/http/configuring_https_servers.html)
    . See the [`nginx ssl protocols`](https://docassemble.org/docs/config.html#nginx%20ssl%20protocols)
     configuration directive.
*   `DASSLCIPHERS`: This indicates the SSL ciphers that [NGINX](https://www.nginx.com/)
     should accept. The default is `HIGH:!aNULL:!MD5`. You might want to change it if you need to support older browsers or remove support for ciphers that are considered weak. The value is passed directly to the [NGINX](https://www.nginx.com/)
     directive [`ssl_ciphers`](https://nginx.org/en/docs/http/configuring_https_servers.html)
    . See the [`nginx ssl ciphers`](https://docassemble.org/docs/config.html#nginx%20ssl%20ciphers)
     configuration directive.
*   `PIPINDEXURL`: This controls the package index that `pip` uses. See the [`pip index url`](https://docassemble.org/docs/config.html#pip%20index%20url)
     configuration directive.
*   `PIPEXTRAINDEXURLS`: This controls the extra package index sites that `pip` uses. See the [`pip extra index urls`](https://docassemble.org/docs/config.html#pip%20extra%20index%20urls)
     configuration directive.
*   `ENABLEUNOCONV`: This controls whether [unoconv](https://linux.die.net/man/1/unoconv)
     is used for DOCX to PDF conversion. It can be set to `true` or `false`. See the [`enable unoconv`](https://docassemble.org/docs/config.html#enable%20unoconv)
     configuration directive.
*   `GOTENBERGURL`: This can be set to the URL of your [Gotenberg](https://gotenberg.dev/)
     server, if you have one. See the [`gotenberg url`](https://docassemble.org/docs/config.html#gotenberg%20url)
     configuration directive.
*   `DAGOOGLEFONTS`: This can be set to `true` if you want the [Google TrueType fonts](https://github.com/google/fonts)
     to be installed on your server.
*   `DAEXTRAFONTS`: This can be set to `true` if you want additional fonts to be installed on your server.
*   `USENGINXTOSERVEFILES`: If NGINX is not being used in the container (i.e. traffic is connected directly to `uwsgi`) then this should be `false`. Otherwise it should be `true`. The default is `true`. See the [`use nginx to serve files`](https://docassemble.org/docs/config.html#use%20nginx%20to%20serve%20files)
     configuration directive.
*   `SUPERVISORLOGLEVEL`: This can be set to `debug` if you would like log messages to print to the output of `supervisord`, which is the output of the container itself. This allows you to view log messages using `docker logs`. The default value is `info`, which means that the output of `supervisord` is limited to messages about the starting and stopping of services and processes.
*   `ENVIRONMENT_TAKES_PRECEDENCE`: It was noted above that once the [configuration](https://docassemble.org/docs/config.html)
     file is located in the [persistent volume](https://docassemble.org/docs/docker.html#persistent)
    , [S3](https://docassemble.org/docs/docker.html#persistent%20s3)
    , or [Azure blob storage](https://docassemble.org/docs/docker.html#persistent%20azure)
    , the values in that [configuration](https://docassemble.org/docs/config.html)
     file will take precedence over any values specified in [Docker](https://www.docker.com/)
     environment variables. This is the default behavior; the [Docker](https://www.docker.com/)
     environment variables are useful for 1) telling the server where to find an existing [configuration](https://docassemble.org/docs/config.html)
     file; and 2) if no [configuration](https://docassemble.org/docs/config.html)
     file exists already, pre-populating the initial [configuration](https://docassemble.org/docs/config.html)
     file. However, if you set `ENVIRONMENT_TAKES_PRECEDENCE` to `true`, then **docassemble** will override values in the [configuration](https://docassemble.org/docs/config.html)
     file with the values of [Docker](https://www.docker.com/)
     environment variables if they conflict. Note that the [YAML](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/YAML)
     of the [configuration](https://docassemble.org/docs/config.html)
     file will not be altered; you will still see the same [YAML](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/YAML)
     when you go to edit the [Configuration](https://docassemble.org/docs/config.html)
    . However, internally, **docassemble** will override those values with the values of the [Docker](https://www.docker.com/)
     environment variables. Since it can be confusing to have dueling sources of configuration values, it is encouraged that you update the [YAML](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/YAML)
     of your [Configuration](https://docassemble.org/docs/config.html)
     to align with the values in your [Docker](https://www.docker.com/)
     environment. The `ENVIRONMENT_TAKES_PRECEDENCE` option is primarily used in the [Kubernetes](https://kubernetes.io/)
    /[Helm](https://helm.sh/)
     environment, where there are some [Docker](https://www.docker.com/)
     environment variables that cannot be known in advance.

Changing the configuration[¶](https://docassemble.org/docs/docker.html#configuration)

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

If you already have an existing **docassemble** installation and you want to `run` a new [Docker](https://www.docker.com/)
 container using it, but you want to change the [configuration](https://docassemble.org/docs/config.html)
 of the container, there are some things you will need to keep in mind.

The existing [configuration](https://docassemble.org/docs/config.html)
 file takes precedence over the environment variables that you set using [Docker](https://www.docker.com/)
.

If you want to change the [configuration](https://docassemble.org/docs/config.html)
, and the server is running, you can edit the [configuration](https://docassemble.org/docs/config.html)
 using the web interface.

If the server is not running, and you are using [persistent volumes](https://docassemble.org/docs/docker.html#persistent)
, you can use [`docker volume inspect`](https://docs.docker.com/engine/reference/commandline/volume_inspect/)
 to find the location of the persistent volume.

When **docassemble** starts up on a [Docker](https://www.docker.com/)
 container, it:

*   Creates a [configuration](https://docassemble.org/docs/config.html)
     file from a template, using environment variables for initial values, if a [configuration](https://docassemble.org/docs/config.html)
     file does not already exist.
*   Initializes a [PostgreSQL](https://www.postgresql.org/)
     database, if one is not already initialized.
*   Configures the [NGINX](https://www.nginx.com/)
     configuration, if one is not already configured.
*   Runs [Let’s Encrypt](https://letsencrypt.org/)
     if the configuration indicates that [Let’s Encrypt](https://letsencrypt.org/)
     should be used, and [Let’s Encrypt](https://letsencrypt.org/)
     has not yet been configured.

When **docassemble** stops, it saves the [configuration](https://docassemble.org/docs/config.html)
 file, a backup of the [PostgreSQL](https://www.postgresql.org/)
 database, and backups of the [Let’s Encrypt](https://letsencrypt.org/)
 configuration. If you are using [persistent volumes](https://docassemble.org/docs/docker.html#persistent)
, the information will be stored there. If you are using [S3](https://docassemble.org/docs/docker.html#persistent%20s3)
 or [Azure blob storage](https://docassemble.org/docs/docker.html#persistent%20azure)
, the information will be stored in the cloud.

When **docassemble** starts again, it will retrieve the [configuration](https://docassemble.org/docs/config.html)
 file, the backup of the [PostgreSQL](https://www.postgresql.org/)
 database, and backups of the [Let’s Encrypt](https://letsencrypt.org/)
 configuration from storage and use them for the container.

Suppose you have an existing installation that uses HTTPS and [Let’s Encrypt](https://letsencrypt.org/)
, but you want to change the [`DAHOSTNAME`](https://docassemble.org/docs/docker.html#DAHOSTNAME)
. You will need to delete the saved configuration before running a new container. First, shut down the machine with [`docker stop -t 600`](https://docs.docker.com/engine/reference/commandline/stop/)
. Then, if you are using [S3](https://docassemble.org/docs/docker.html#persistent%20s3)
, you can go to the [S3 Console](https://console.aws.amazon.com/s3/home)
 and delete the “letsencrypt.tar.gz” file. If you are using [Azure blob storage](https://docassemble.org/docs/docker.html#persistent%20azure)
, you can go to the [Azure Portal](https://portal.azure.com/)
 and delete the “letsencrypt.tar.gz” file.

Also, if a configuration file exists on [S3](https://docassemble.org/docs/docker.html#persistents3)
/[Azure blob storage](https://docassemble.org/docs/docker.html#persistent%20azure)
 (`config.yml`) or in a [persistent volume](https://docassemble.org/docs/docker.html#persistent)
, then the values in that configuration will take precedence over the corresponding environment variables that are passed to [Docker](https://www.docker.com/)
. Once a configuration file exists, you should make changes to the configuration file rather than passing environment variables to [Docker](https://www.docker.com/)
. However, if your configuration is on [S3](https://docassemble.org/docs/docker.html#persistent%20s3)
/[Azure blob storage](https://docassemble.org/docs/docker.html#persistent%20azure)
, you will at least need to pass sufficient access keys (e.g., [`S3BUCKET`](https://docassemble.org/docs/docker.html#S3BUCKET)
, [`AZURECONTAINER`](https://docassemble.org/docs/docker.html#AZURECONTAINER)
, etc.) to access that storage; otherwise your container will not know where to find the configuration.

Also, there are some environment variables that do not exist in the configuration file because they are specific to the individual server being started. These include the [`CONTAINERROLE`](https://docassemble.org/docs/docker.html#CONTAINERROLE)
 and [`SERVERHOSTNAME`](https://docassemble.org/docs/docker.html#SERVERHOSTNAME)
 environment variables.

Data storage[¶](https://docassemble.org/docs/docker.html#data%20storage)

=========================================================================

[Docker](https://www.docker.com/)
 containers are volatile. They are designed to be run, turned off, and destroyed. When using [Docker](https://www.docker.com/)
, the best way to upgrade **docassemble** to a new version is to destroy and rebuild your containers.

But what about your data? If you run **docassemble**, you are accumulating valuable data in SQL, in files, and in [Redis](https://redis.io/)
. If your data are stored on the [Docker](https://www.docker.com/)
 container, they will be destroyed by [`docker rm`](https://docs.docker.com/engine/reference/commandline/rm/)
.

There are two ways around this problem. The first, and most preferable solution, is to use an object storage service. The standard-setting object storage service is [Amazon Web Services](https://aws.amazon.com/)
’s [S3](https://aws.amazon.com/s3/)
. If you use [AWS](https://aws.amazon.com/)
, you can create an [S3 bucket](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonS3/latest/dev/UsingBucket.html)
 for your data, and then when you launch your **docassemble** container, set the [`S3BUCKET`](https://docassemble.org/docs/docker.html#S3BUCKET)
, [`S3ACCESSKEY`](https://docassemble.org/docs/docker.html#S3ACCESSKEY)
, [`S3SECRETACCESSKEY`](https://docassemble.org/docs/docker.html#S3SECRETACCESSKEY)
, and [`S3REGION`](https://docassemble.org/docs/docker.html#S3REGION)
 environment variables.

If you don’t want to use [Amazon Web Services](https://aws.amazon.com/)
, you can use an [S3](https://aws.amazon.com/s3/)
\-compatible object storage service by setting [`S3ENDPOINTURL`](https://docassemble.org/docs/docker.html#S3ENDPOINTURL)
 to the URL of the service, along with the [`S3BUCKET`](https://docassemble.org/docs/docker.html#S3BUCKET)
, [`S3ACCESSKEY`](https://docassemble.org/docs/docker.html#S3ACCESSKEY)
, and [`S3SECRETACCESSKEY`](https://docassemble.org/docs/docker.html#S3SECRETACCESSKEY)
 [environment variables](https://docassemble.org/docs/docker.html#configuration%20options)
. There are [S3](https://aws.amazon.com/s3/)
\-compatible object storage services available for [Google Cloud](https://cloud.google.com/storage/docs/interoperability)
, [Wasabi](https://wasabi.com/s3-compatible-cloud-storage/)
, [Linode](https://www.linode.com/docs/platform/object-storage/how-to-use-object-storage/)
, [Vultr](https://www.vultr.com/docs/vultr-object-storage)
, [Digital Ocean](https://developers.digitalocean.com/documentation/spaces/#aws-s3-compatibility)
, [IBM Cloud](https://cloud.ibm.com/apidocs/cos/cos-compatibility)
, [Oracle Cloud](https://docs.cloud.oracle.com/en-us/iaas/Content/Object/Tasks/s3compatibleapi.htm)
, [Scaleway](https://www.scaleway.com/en/object-storage/)
, [Exoscale](https://www.exoscale.com/object-storage/)
, and others. If you are operating an on-premises server, you can deploy [MinIO](https://min.io/)
 ([MinIO](https://min.io/)
 is configured by default if you deploy **docassemble** [with Kubernetes](https://github.com/jhpyle/charts)
) or [Rook](https://rook.io/docs/rook/v0.8/object.html)
.

In addition to [S3](https://aws.amazon.com/s3/)
 and [S3](https://aws.amazon.com/s3/)
\-compatible object storage, **docassemble** supports [Azure blob storage](https://azure.microsoft.com/en-us/services/storage/blobs/)
. You can create a [blob storage container](https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/storage/storage-dotnet-how-to-use-blobs#create-a-container)
 inside [Microsoft Azure](https://azure.microsoft.com/)
 and then when you launch your container, you set the [`AZUREACCOUNTNAME`](https://docassemble.org/docs/docker.html#AZUREACCOUNTNAME)
, [`AZUREACCOUNTKEY`](https://docassemble.org/docs/docker.html#AZUREACCOUNTKEY)
, and [`AZURECONTAINER`](https://docassemble.org/docs/docker.html#AZURECONTAINER)
 [environment variables](https://docassemble.org/docs/docker.html#configuration%20options)
.

When [`docker stop -t 600`](https://docs.docker.com/engine/reference/commandline/stop/)
 is run, **docassemble** will backup the SQL database, the [Redis](https://redis.io/)
 database, the [configuration](https://docassemble.org/docs/config.html)
, and your uploaded files to the [S3 bucket](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonS3/latest/dev/UsingBucket.html)
 or [blob storage container](https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/storage/storage-dotnet-how-to-use-blobs#create-a-container)
. Then, when you issue a [`docker run`](https://docs.docker.com/engine/reference/commandline/run/)
 command with [environment variables](https://docassemble.org/docs/docker.html#configuration%20options)
 pointing **docassemble** to your [S3 bucket](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonS3/latest/dev/UsingBucket.html)
/[Azure blob storage](https://azure.microsoft.com/en-us/services/storage/blobs/)
 resource, **docassemble** will make restore from the backup. You can [`docker rm`](https://docs.docker.com/engine/reference/commandline/rm/)
 your container and your data will persist in the cloud.

The second method of persistent storage is to use [persistent volumes](https://docassemble.org/docs/docker.html#persistent)
, which is a feature of [Docker](https://www.docker.com/)
. This will store the data in directories on the [Docker](https://www.docker.com/)
 host, so that when you destroy the container, these directories will be untouched, and when you start up a new container, it will use the saved directories. This feature is only available if you are running **docassemble** in a single-server configuration.

These two options are explained in the following subsections.

If you are operating a development server in a single-server configuration, and you will be using the [Playground](https://docassemble.org/docs/playground.html)
, using [persistent volumes](https://docassemble.org/docs/docker.html#persistent)
 is recommended. When a development server uses [S3](https://aws.amazon.com/s3/)
, every time a [Playground](https://docassemble.org/docs/playground.html)
 file is accessed, it must be copied from S3 to the server. This negatively impacts performance.

Using S3 or S3-compatible[¶](https://docassemble.org/docs/docker.html#persistent%20s3)

---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

To use [S3](https://aws.amazon.com/s3/)
 (or a non-[AWS](https://aws.amazon.com/)
 [S3](https://aws.amazon.com/s3/)
\-compatible service) for persistent storage, you need to obtain credentials and create a bucket.

If you want to use [Amazon Web Services](https://aws.amazon.com/)
, you would first sign up for an [AWS](https://aws.amazon.com/)
 account, and go to the [S3 Console](https://console.aws.amazon.com/s3/home)
, click “Create Bucket,” and pick a name. If your site is at docassemble.example.com, a good name for the bucket is `docassemble-example-com`. (Client software will have trouble accessing your bucket if it contains `.` characters.) Under “Region,” pick the region nearest you. Then you need to obtain an access key and a secret access key for [S3](https://aws.amazon.com/s3/)
. To obtain these credentials, go to [IAM Console](https://console.aws.amazon.com/iam)
 and create a user with “programmatic access.” Under “Attach existing policies directly,” find the policy called `AmazonS3FullAccess` and attach it to the user.

When you run your **docassemble** [Docker](https://www.docker.com/)
 container, set the [configuration options](https://docassemble.org/docs/docker.html#configuration%20options)
 [`S3BUCKET`](https://docassemble.org/docs/docker.html#S3BUCKET)
, [`S3ACCESSKEY`](https://docassemble.org/docs/docker.html#S3ACCESSKEY)
, [`S3SECRETACCESSKEY`](https://docassemble.org/docs/docker.html#S3SECRETACCESSKEY)
, and [`S3REGION`](https://docassemble.org/docs/docker.html#S3REGION)
. For example, you might use an `env.list` file such as:

    DAHOSTNAME=interviews.example.com
    S3ENABLE=true
    S3BUCKET=interviews-example-com
    S3ACCESSKEY=YERWERGDFSGERGSDFGSW
    S3SECRETACCESSKEY=WERWR36dddeg3udjfRT1+rweRTHRTookiMVASDAS
    S3REGION=us-east-2
    TIMEZONE=America/New_York
    USEHTTPS=true
    EC2=true
    USELETSENCRYPT=true
    LETSENCRYPTEMAIL=dev@example.com

If you are using an [S3](https://aws.amazon.com/s3/)
\-compatible object storage service, you will need to set [`S3ENDPOINTURL`](https://docassemble.org/docs/docker.html#S3ENDPOINTURL)
 to the URL endpoint of your service, which you can find in the service’s documentation or in your account settings. You likely will not need to set [`S3REGION`](https://docassemble.org/docs/docker.html#S3REGION)
 unless the service supports the “region” concept.

For example, if you are using the [Digital Ocean](https://developers.digitalocean.com/documentation/spaces/#aws-s3-compatibility)
 Spaces service using the San Francisco datacenter, your `env.list` file might contain:

    S3ENABLE=true
    S3BUCKET=interviews-example-com
    S3ACCESSKEY=YERWERGDFSGERGSDFGSW
    S3SECRETACCESSKEY=WERWR36dddeg3udjfRT1+rweRTHRTookiMVASDAS
    S3ENDPOINTURL=https://sfo3.digitaloceanspaces.com

Keep in mind that the secret access keys that you include in your environment variables will become available to all developers who use your **docassemble** server, since they will be stored in the configuration file.

Note that if you run **docassemble** on [EC2](https://aws.amazon.com/ec2/)
, you can launch your [EC2](https://aws.amazon.com/ec2/)
 instances with an [IAM](https://aws.amazon.com/iam/)
 role that allows **docassemble** to access to an [S3](https://aws.amazon.com/s3/)
 bucket without the necessity of setting [`S3ACCESSKEY`](https://docassemble.org/docs/docker.html#S3ACCESSKEY)
 and [`S3SECRETACCESSKEY`](https://docassemble.org/docs/docker.html#S3SECRETACCESSKEY)
. In this case, the only environment variable you need to pass is [`S3BUCKET`](https://docassemble.org/docs/docker.html#S3BUCKET)
.

If you are using [AWS](https://aws.amazon.com/)
 and you want to limit access to a particular bucket, you do not have to use the `AmazonS3FullAccess` policy when obtaining [S3](https://aws.amazon.com/s3/)
 credentials. Instead, you can create your own policy with the following definition:

    {
        "Version": "2012-10-17",
        "Statement": [\
            {\
                "Effect": "Allow",\
                "Action": [\
                    "s3:ListBucket"\
                ],\
                "Resource": [\
                    "arn:aws:s3:::docassemble-example-com"\
                ]\
            },\
            {\
                "Effect": "Allow",\
                "Action": [\
                    "s3:PutObject",\
                    "s3:GetObject",\
                    "s3:DeleteObject"\
                ],\
                "Resource": [\
                    "arn:aws:s3:::docassemble-example-com/*"\
                ]\
            }\
        ]
    }

Replace `docassemble-example-com` in the above text with the name of your [S3](https://aws.amazon.com/s3/)
 bucket.

When setting up your [S3](https://aws.amazon.com/s3/)
 bucket, you should consider setting up a backup mechanism using the [S3](https://aws.amazon.com/s3/)
 provider’s site. When uploaded files are stored in [S3](https://aws.amazon.com/s3/)
, **docassemble** does not include those files in its rotating backups.

Using Microsoft Azure[¶](https://docassemble.org/docs/docker.html#persistent%20azure)

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Using [Microsoft Azure](https://azure.microsoft.com/)
 is very similar to using [S3](https://aws.amazon.com/s3/)
. From the [Azure Portal](https://portal.azure.com/)
 dashboard, search for “Storage accounts” in the “Resources.” Click “Add” to create a new storage account. Under “Account kind,” choose “BlobStorage.” Under “Access tier,” you can choose either “Cool” or “Hot,” but you may have to pay more for “Hot.”

Once the storage account is created, go into your “Blobs” service in the storage account and click “+ Container” to add a new container. Set the “Access type” to “Private.” The name of the container corresponds with the [`AZURECONTAINER`](https://docassemble.org/docs/docker.html#AZURECONTAINER)
 environment variable. Back at the storage account, click “Access keys.” The “Storage account name” corresponds with the environment variable [`AZUREACCOUNTNAME`](https://docassemble.org/docs/docker.html#AZUREACCOUNTNAME)
. The “key1” corresponds with the [`AZUREACCOUNTKEY`](https://docassemble.org/docs/docker.html#AZUREACCOUNTKEY)
 environment variable. (You can also use “key2.”). For example, you might use an `env.list` file such as:

    DAHOSTNAME=interviews.example.com
    AZUREENABLE=true
    AZUREACCOUNTNAME=exampledotcom
    AZUREACCOUNTKEY=98f89asdfjwew/YosdfojweafASDFErgergDFGsergagaweWRTIQgqERGs243rergE4534tERgEFBDRGferEEB==
    AZURECONTAINER=interviews-example-com
    TIMEZONE=America/New_York
    USEHTTPS=true
    USELETSENCRYPT=true
    LETSENCRYPTEMAIL=dev@example.com

If you enable both [S3](https://docassemble.org/docs/docker.html#persistent%20s3)
 and [Azure blob storage](https://docassemble.org/docs/docker.html#persistent%20azure)
, only [S3](https://aws.amazon.com/s3/)
 will be used.

When setting up Azure blob storage, you should consider setting up a backup mechanism in Azure. When uploaded files are stored in Azure blob storage, **docassemble** does not include those files in its rotating backups.

Using persistent volumes[¶](https://docassemble.org/docs/docker.html#persistent)

---------------------------------------------------------------------------------

To run **docassemble** in a [single-server arrangement](https://docassemble.org/docs/docker.html#single%20server%20arrangement)
 in such a way that the [configuration](https://docassemble.org/docs/config.html)
, the [Playground](https://docassemble.org/docs/playground.html)
 files, the uploaded files, and other data persist after the [Docker](https://www.docker.com/)
 container is removed or updated, run the image as follows:

    docker run --env-file=env.list \
    -v dabackup:/usr/share/docassemble/backup \
    -d -p 80:80 -p 443:443 --restart always --stop-timeout 600 \
    jhpyle/docassemble

where `--env-file=env.list` is an optional parameter that refers to a file `env.list` containing environment variables for the configuration. A [template for the `env.list` file](https://github.com/jhpyle/docassemble/blob/master/Docker/env.list)
 is included in distribution.

An advantage of using persistent volumes is that you can completely replace the **docassemble** container and rebuild it from scratch, and when you `run` the `jhpyle/docassemble` image again, docassemble will keep running where it left off.

If you are using [HTTPS](https://docassemble.org/docs/docker.html#https)
 with your own certificates (as opposed to using [Let’s Encrypt](https://letsencrypt.org/)
), or you need to provide other SSL certificates to **docassemble** (for example, for PostgreSQL and/or Redis encryption) you can use a persistent volume to provide the certificates to the [Docker](https://www.docker.com/)
 container; add `-v dacerts:/usr/share/docassemble/certs` to your [`docker run`](https://docs.docker.com/engine/reference/commandline/run/)
 command. For more information on creating a persistent volume for SSL certificates, see [below](https://docassemble.org/docs/docker.html#own%20certificates)
.

To see what volumes exist on your [Docker](https://www.docker.com/)
 system, you can run:

    docker volume ls

A volume will be created when you run `docker run` with the `-v` option. For example, the `docker run` command above specified `-v dabackup:/usr/share/docassemble/backup`. If a volume called `dabackup` does not already exist, it will be created, and the `jhpyle/docassemble` container will initialize its contents. The `dabackup` volume will be associated with the directory `/usr/share/docassemble/backup` inside the container. The `docker volume ls` command can be used to list the files inside of a volume. `docker cp` can be used to copy files from the host to the `/usr/share/docassemble/backup` folder.

You might want to initialize a volume before starting your **docassemble** server, for example in order to provide certificates to **docassemble**. For example, to create a volume called `dacerts`, you can `docker run` the minimal `busybox` container with the volume mounted to `/data`:

    docker run -v dacerts:/data --name deleteme busybox true

Then you can use `docker cp` to copy files to it:

    docker cp redis.crt deleteme:/data/
    docker cp redis.key deleteme:/data/

Then you can delete the container, as it is no longer needed:

Now delete the [BusyBox](https://hub.docker.com/_/busybox)
 container. (The volume `dacerts` will not be deleted.)

    docker rm deleteme

Now that the `dacerts` volume has already been created on the host, if you do:

    docker run --env-file=env.list \
    -v dabackup:/usr/share/docassemble/backup \
    -v dacerts:/usr/share/docassemble/certs \
    -d -p 80:80 -p 443:443 --restart always --stop-timeout 600 \
    jhpyle/docassemble

then the directory `/usr/share/docassemble/certs` will already be prepopulated when **docassemble** starts.

If you want to see the files in the `dacerts` volume, do:

    docker volume ls dacerts

If you want to delete the `dacerts` volume, do:

    docker volume rm dacerts

To delete all of the volumes, do:

    docker volume rm $(docker volume ls -qf dangling=true)

[Docker volumes](https://docs.docker.com/storage/volumes/)
 are powerful but complicated. If you want to use them, you can read about them in the Docker documentation and other places on the internet.

It is recommended that you do not create [Docker volumes](https://docs.docker.com/storage/volumes/)
 for directories other than `/usr/share/docassemble/certs` and `/usr/share/docassemble/backup`. If you try to mount other directories as volumes, you might experience hard-to-debug problems. Depending on the architecture, the directories might cause the container to malfunction because certain file system features that the software depends on are not available. If you create a volume for a directory, your directory will supplant the directory that is present in the Docker image, so unless you populate the directory with the same files that the image provides, your **docassemble** server may be non-functional.

Ultimately, the better [data storage](https://docassemble.org/docs/docker.html#data%20storage)
 solution is to use cloud storage ([S3](https://docassemble.org/docs/docker.html#persistent%20s3)
, [Azure blob storage](https://docassemble.org/docs/docker.html#persistent%20azure)
) because:

1.  [S3](https://docassemble.org/docs/docker.html#persistent%20s3)
     and [Azure blob storage](https://docassemble.org/docs/docker.html#persistent%20azure)
     make scaling easier. They are the “cloud” way of storing persistent data, at least until cloud-based network file systems become more robust.
2.  It is easier to upgrade your virtual machines to the latest software and operating system if you can just destroy them and recreate them, rather than running update scripts. If your persistent data is stored in the cloud, you can destroy and recreate virtual machines at will, without ever having to worry about copying your data on and off the machines.

However, you can get around the second problem by using [`docker volume create`](https://docs.docker.com/engine/reference/commandline/volume_create/)
 to put your [Docker volume](https://docs.docker.com/storage/volumes/)
 on a separate drive. That way, you could remove the virtual machine that runs the application, along with its primary drive, without affecting the drive with the **docassemble** data.

If you lose your admin password[¶](https://docassemble.org/docs/docker.html#password%20loss)

---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

If you cannot log into your **docassemble** server because you forgot the password of your account with `admin` privileges, it is possible to go in through the back end to change the password. This is complicated by the fact that passwords are encrypted.

In order to change the password of your user with `admin` privileges, you will need to know the password of another account on your server, even if it is an unprivileged account. If you don’t know the password of any other account, you can register as a new user.

The following instructions assume that you have not configured **docassemble** to use an external SQL database, and you are using the default configuration, which is that there is a SQL database inside the Docker container named `docassemble`.

Use `docker exec` to get a command line inside your container. (If you don’t know how to do this, see the [troubleshooting](https://docassemble.org/docs/docker.html#troubleshooting)
 section.)

Then do:

    # su postgres
    $ psql docassemble

Now you should have a `#` prompt inside of `psql`, so that you can issue SQL commands.

First, find out the user ID of the user whose password you know, as well as the user ID of the user with administrative privileges whose password you want to change.

    # select id, email from "user"

Then, get the encrypted password of that user (assuming the ID is `15`):

    # select password from user_auth where user_id=15;

Now that you know the encrypted version of the known password, you can change the password of the admin user to that encrypted password (assuming the ID of the user with `admin` privileges is `1`).

    # update user_auth set password='$2b$12$B5WscXrNatMg/0mMGU63VuY8NPN6IFY7MmhFocKGGU86OXFryLuDi' where id=1;

Now, you should be able to log in as the user with `admin` privileges using the known password.

Note that the passwords are encrypted using the [`secretkey`](https://docassemble.org/docs/config.html#secretkey)
 as a “salt,” so the encrypted version of a password will vary from server to server.

Recovery from backup files[¶](https://docassemble.org/docs/docker.html#recovery)

---------------------------------------------------------------------------------

When you are using [data storage](https://docassemble.org/docs/docker.html#data%20storage)
, you can do `docker stop -t 600` on a container, followed by `docker rm`, and then re-run your original `docker run` command, and when the system starts again, it will be in the same place it was before, with the same uploaded files, the same SQL database.

During the `docker stop` process, application data are saved into files and directories in the [data storage](https://docassemble.org/docs/docker.html#data%20storage)
 area. During `docker run` (and `docker start` as well), application data are restored from the [data storage](https://docassemble.org/docs/docker.html#data%20storage)
 area before the server attempts to start. Included in the application data is a the list of Python packages installed on your system; when the server starts, `pip` will be used to install the same list of packages.

This backup-on-shutdown/restore-on-startup feature is very powerful because it means you can shut down, delete your Docker container, pull a new Docker image, and then re-run `docker run`, and all of your application data and Python packages will be restored. Between the old Docker image and the new Docker image, the versions of the operating system, PostgreSQL, and Python might have changed, but the restore process will adjust for this.

However, if your server has an unsafe shutdown, the files in the [data storage](https://docassemble.org/docs/docker.html#data%20storage)
 area might be corrupted. They might also be missing or very old (dating from the last time there was a safe shutdown). If this happens, not all is lost, because you can restore from one of the daily backup snapshots.

If you are using [S3](https://docassemble.org/docs/docker.html#persistent%20s3)
 or [Azure blob storage](https://docassemble.org/docs/docker.html#persistent%20azure)
, the files and directories that are saved during the shutdown process are:

*   `postgres` - a folder containing a “dump” of each database hosted by the [PostgreSQL](https://www.postgresql.org/)
     server. Usually the operative file is called `docassemble`, for the database called `docassemble`. If you point your server to an external database using the [`db` section](https://docassemble.org/docs/config.html#db)
     of your [Configuration](https://docassemble.org/docs/config.html)
    , this is not applicable. The backup file will exist, but it will be an empty database.
*   `redis.rdb` - a file containing a backup of the [Redis](https://redis.io/)
     database. If you point your server to an external [Redis](https://redis.io/)
     database using a [`redis`](https://docassemble.org/docs/config.html#redis)
     directive in your [Configuration](https://docassemble.org/docs/config.html)
    , this is not applicable. The `redis.rdb` file will exist, but it will be an empty database.
*   `log` - a folder containing **docassemble** log files.
*   `nginxlogs` - a folder containing the logs for [NGINX](https://www.nginx.com/)
    . If you are using Apache, the relevant folder is `apachelogs`. This is not applicable unless the `CONTAINERROLE` is `all`.

The `files` folder, the `config.yml` file, and the `letsencrypt.tar.gz` (if Let’s Encrypt is used) are important for restoring the system on startup, but they are always up-to-date; they are not copied from the server during the shutdown process. So even if you have an unsafe shutdown, you will have up-to-date versions of `files`, `config.yml`, and `letsencrypt.tar.gz`.

If you are not using [S3](https://docassemble.org/docs/docker.html#persistent%20s3)
 or [Azure blob storage](https://docassemble.org/docs/docker.html#persistent%20azure)
, then during a safe shutdown process, application data is saved into the following files and folders:

*   `/usr/share/docassemble/backup/postgres` - a folder containing a “dump” of each database hosted by the [PostgreSQL](https://www.postgresql.org/)
     server. Usually the operative file is called `docassemble`, for the database called `docassemble`. If you point your server to an external database using the [`db` section](https://docassemble.org/docs/config.html#db)
     of your [Configuration](https://docassemble.org/docs/config.html)
    , this is not applicable. The backup file will exist, but it will be an empty database.
*   `/usr/share/docassemble/backup/redis.rdb` - a file containing a backup of the [Redis](https://redis.io/)
     database. If you point your server to an external [Redis](https://redis.io/)
     database using a [`redis`](https://docassemble.org/docs/config.html#redis)
     directive in your [Configuration](https://docassemble.org/docs/config.html)
    , this is not applicable. The `redis.rdb` file will exist, but it will be an empty database.
*   `/usr/share/docassemble/backup/files` - a directory containing all of the stored files in your system (document uploads, assembled documents, ZIP files for installed packages, etc.). If [`backup file storage`](https://docassemble.org/docs/config.html#backup%20file%20storage)
     in the [Configuration](https://docassemble.org/docs/config.html)
     is set to `false`, then this will not exist.
*   `/usr/share/docassemble/backup/log` - a folder containing **docassemble** log files.
*   `/usr/share/docassemble/backup/nginxlogs` - a folder containing the logs for [NGINX](https://www.nginx.com/)
    . If you are using Apache, the relevant folder is `/usr/share/docassemble/backup/apachelogs`. This is not applicable unless the `CONTAINERROLE` is `all`.
*   `/usr/share/docassemble/backup/config/config.yml` - a file containing the Configuration of your system.

The file `/usr/share/docassemble/backup/letsencrypt.tar.gz` is important for restoring the system (if Let’s Encrypt is used), but it is always up-to-date; it is not copied from the server during the shutdown process.

Whenever a **docassemble** container starts up, the [PostgreSQL](https://www.postgresql.org/)
 database in `postgres/docassemble` is used to restore **docassemble**’s SQL database. The `redis.rdb` file is used to restore the Redis database. These files are created during the shutdown process. It is important that the shutdown process happens gracefully, because otherwise these files will not be complete.

As protection against the risk of an unsafe shutdown (as well as the risk of the accidental deletion of data), **docassemble** maintains a daily rotating backup. The daily backup is created whenever the daily cron job runs (which is typically around 6:00 in the morning).

If you are using [S3](https://docassemble.org/docs/docker.html#persistent%20s3)
 or [Azure blob storage](https://docassemble.org/docs/docker.html#persistent%20azure)
, these backups are in the `backup` folder in the cloud storage. There is a subfolder in the `backup` folder for each container that has used the cloud storage area. The subfolder names come from the internal hostnames of containers. In a [multi-server arrangement](https://docassemble.org/docs/docker.html#multi%20server%20arrangement)
, you will see several subfolders. You may also see several subfolders if you have called `docker run` multiple times. Within a subfolder for a container, there are subfolders for each day for which there is a backup. The folders are in the format `MM-DD` where `MM` is the month and `DD` is the day of the month. If you want to restore your system to a snapshot of where it was when a daily backup was made, you will need to shut down your server(s) with `docker stop -t 600` if it is still running. Then you will need to copy files from the daily backup location to the places where they will be used when the system starts up again. In particular, you will copy the following out of the daily backup folder:

*   `config/config.yml` to `config.yml` in the root of the cloud storage.
*   `files` to `files` in the root of the cloud storage.
*   `postgres` to `postgres` in the root of the cloud storage.
*   `redis.rdb` to `redis.rdb` in the root of the cloud storage.
*   `log` to `log` in the root of the cloud storage.

Copying `log` is optional. The contents of log files are not critical to the functionality of the systems.

Note that when using [S3](https://docassemble.org/docs/docker.html#persistent%20s3)
 or [Azure blob storage](https://docassemble.org/docs/docker.html#persistent%20azure)
, file storage (uploaded files, assembled documents) are already in the cloud, so they are not backed up to the `backup` folder. If you want a backup mechanism for these files, you can enable it using [S3](https://aws.amazon.com/s3/)
 or [Azure blob storage](https://azure.microsoft.com/en-us/services/storage/blobs/)
 site.

If you are not using [S3](https://docassemble.org/docs/docker.html#persistent%20s3)
 or [Azure blob storage](https://docassemble.org/docs/docker.html#persistent%20azure)
, the disaster recovery backup files are in folders named `/usr/share/docassemble/backup/MM-DD` where `MM` is the month and `DD` is the day the backup was made. If you want to restore your system to a snapshot of where it was when a daily backup was made, you will first need to shut down your server with `docker stop -t 600` if it is still running. Then you will need to copy files from the daily backup location to the places where they will be used when the system starts up again. In particular, you will copy the following out of the daily backup folder:

*   the `config/config.yml` file to `/usr/share/docassemble/backup/config.yml`
*   the `files` folder to `/usr/share/docassemble/backup/files`
*   the `postgres` folder to `/usr/share/docassemble/backup/postgres`
*   the `redis.rdb` file to `/usr/share/docassemble/backup/redis.rdb`
*   the `log` folder to `/usr/share/docassemble/backup/log`

Copying `log` is optional. The contents of log files are not critical to the functionality of the systems.

After copying these files into place, you can start your server(s) with `docker run` (using the same parameters you originally used) or `docker start`.

Multi-server arrangement[¶](https://docassemble.org/docs/docker.html#multi%20server%20arrangement)

===================================================================================================

Services on different machines
------------------------------

The **docassemble** application consists of several services, some of which are singular and some of which can be plural.

The singular services include:

*   SQL
*   [Redis](https://redis.io/)
    
*   [RabbitMQ](https://www.rabbitmq.com/)
     for coordinating [background processes](https://docassemble.org/docs/background.html#background)
    
*   The **docassemble** log message aggregator
*   A [cron](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cron)
     service that runs [scheduled tasks](https://docassemble.org/docs/background.html#scheduled)
     and housekeeping functions

The (potentially) plural services include:

*   Web servers
*   [Celery](https://docs.celeryq.dev/en/stable/)
     nodes

The **docassemble** [Docker](https://www.docker.com/)
 container will run any subset of these six services, depending on the value of the environment variable [`CONTAINERROLE`](https://docassemble.org/docs/docker.html#CONTAINERROLE)
, which is passed to the container at startup. In a single-server arrangement ([`CONTAINERROLE`](https://docassemble.org/docs/docker.html#CONTAINERROLE)
 = `all`, or left undefined), the container runs all of the services (except the log message aggregator, which is not necessary in the case of a single-server arrangement).

You can run **docassemble** in a [multi-server arrangement](https://docassemble.org/docs/docker.html#multi%20server%20arrangement)
 using [Docker](https://www.docker.com/)
 by running the **docassemble** image on different hosts using different [configuration options](https://docassemble.org/docs/docker.html#configuration%20options)
.

In a multi-server arrangement, you can have one machine run SQL, another machine run [Redis](https://redis.io/)
 and [RabbitMQ](https://www.rabbitmq.com/)
, and any number of machines run web servers and [Celery](https://docs.celeryq.dev/en/stable/)
 nodes. You can decide how to allocate services to different machines. For example, you might want to run central tasks on a powerful server, while running many web servers on less powerful machines.

Since the SQL, [Redis](https://redis.io/)
, and [RabbitMQ](https://www.rabbitmq.com/)
 services are standard services, they do not have to be run from **docassemble** [Docker](https://www.docker.com/)
 containers. For example, if you are already running a SQL server, a [Redis](https://redis.io/)
 server, and a [RabbitMQ](https://www.rabbitmq.com/)
 server, you could just point **docassemble** to those resources.

To change the SQL server that **docassemble** uses, edit the [`DBHOST`](https://docassemble.org/docs/docker.html#DBHOST)
, [`DBNAME`](https://docassemble.org/docs/docker.html#DBNAME)
, [`DBUSER`](https://docassemble.org/docs/docker.html#DBUSER)
, [`DBPASSWORD`](https://docassemble.org/docs/docker.html#DBPASSWORD)
, [`DBPREFIX`](https://docassemble.org/docs/docker.html#DBPREFIX)
, [`DBPORT`](https://docassemble.org/docs/docker.html#DBPORT)
, and [`DBTABLEPREFIX`](https://docassemble.org/docs/docker.html#DBTABLEPREFIX)
 [configuration options](https://docassemble.org/docs/docker.html#configuration%20options)
.

To change the [Redis](https://redis.io/)
 server that **docassemble** uses, edit the [`REDIS`](https://docassemble.org/docs/config.html#redis)
 [configuration option](https://docassemble.org/docs/docker.html#configuration%20options)
.

To change the [RabbitMQ](https://www.rabbitmq.com/)
 server that **docassemble** uses, edit the [`RABBITMQ`](https://docassemble.org/docs/config.html#rabbitmq)
 [configuration option](https://docassemble.org/docs/docker.html#configuration%20options)
.

If you are only using a single Docker container to run the **docassemble** web application and [Celery](https://docs.celeryq.dev/en/stable/)
, then even if you are using an external SQL server, external [Redis](https://redis.io/)
 server, and external [RabbitMQ](https://www.rabbitmq.com/)
 server, you can keep the `CONTAINERROLE` as `all` (or undefined).

Port opening[¶](https://docassemble.org/docs/docker.html#ports)

----------------------------------------------------------------

Note that for every service that a [Docker](https://www.docker.com/)
 container provides, appropriate ports need to be forwarded from the [Docker](https://www.docker.com/)
 host machine to the container.

*   Regardless of the [`CONTAINERROLE`](https://docassemble.org/docs/docker.html#CONTAINERROLE)
    , port 9001 needs to be forwarded so that the container can be controlled via [supervisor](http://supervisord.org/)
    .
*   If [`CONTAINERROLE`](https://docassemble.org/docs/docker.html#CONTAINERROLE)
     includes `sql`: forward port 5432 ([PostgreSQL](https://www.postgresql.org/)
    )
*   If [`CONTAINERROLE`](https://docassemble.org/docs/docker.html#CONTAINERROLE)
     includes `web`: forward ports 80 (HTTP) and 443 (HTTPS)
*   If [`CONTAINERROLE`](https://docassemble.org/docs/docker.html#CONTAINERROLE)
     includes `log`: forward ports 514 ([Syslog-ng](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Syslog-ng)
    ) and 8080 (custom web server)
*   If [`CONTAINERROLE`](https://docassemble.org/docs/docker.html#CONTAINERROLE)
     includes `redis`: forward port 6379 ([Redis](https://redis.io/)
    )
*   If [`CONTAINERROLE`](https://docassemble.org/docs/docker.html#CONTAINERROLE)
     includes `rabbitmq`: forward ports 4369, 5671, 5672, and 25672 ([RabbitMQ](https://www.rabbitmq.com/)
    ).
*   If [`CONTAINERROLE`](https://docassemble.org/docs/docker.html#CONTAINERROLE)
     includes `mail`: forward port 25 ([e-mails](https://docassemble.org/docs/background.html#email)
    ).

For example:

    docker run \
    --env CONTAINERROLE=sql:redis \
    ...
    -d -p 5432:5432 -p 6379:6379 -p 9001:9001 \
    --restart always --stop-timeout 600 jhpyle/docassemble

    docker run \
    --env CONTAINERROLE=web:celery \
    ...
    -d -p 80:80 -p 443:443 -p 9001:9001 \
    --restart always --stop-timeout 600 jhpyle/docassemble

Note that [Docker](https://www.docker.com/)
 will fail if any of these ports is already in use. For example, many Linux distributions run a mail transport agent on port 25 by default; you will have to stop that service in order to start [Docker](https://www.docker.com/)
 with `-p 25:25`. For example, on [Amazon Linux](https://aws.amazon.com/amazon-linux-ami/)
 you may need to run:

    sudo /etc/init.d/sendmail stop

File sharing[¶](https://docassemble.org/docs/docker.html#file%20sharing)

-------------------------------------------------------------------------

If you run multiple **docassemble** [Docker](https://www.docker.com/)
 containers on different machines, the containers will need to have a way to share files with one another.

One way to share files among containers is to make `/usr/share/docassemble/` a [persistent volume](https://docassemble.org/docs/docker.html#persistent)
 on a network file system. This directory contains the configuration, SSL certificates, [Python virtual environment](https://docs.python-guide.org/dev/virtualenvs/)
, and uploaded files. However, network file systems present problems.

A preferable way to share files is with [Amazon S3](https://aws.amazon.com/s3/)
 or [Azure blob storage](https://azure.microsoft.com/en-us/services/storage/blobs/)
, which **docassemble** supports. See the [using S3](https://docassemble.org/docs/docker.html#persistent%20s3)
 and [using Azure blob storage](https://docassemble.org/docs/docker.html#persistent%20azure)
 sections for instructions on setting this up.

Configuration file[¶](https://docassemble.org/docs/docker.html#config%20file)

------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Note that when you use the cloud ([S3](https://docassemble.org/docs/docker.html#persistent%20s3)
 or [Azure blob storage](https://docassemble.org/docs/docker.html#persistent%20azure)
) for [data storage](https://docassemble.org/docs/docker.html#data%20storage)
, **docassemble** will copy the `config.yml` file out of the cloud on startup, and save `config.yml` to the cloud whenever the configuration is modified.

This means that as long as there is a `config.yml` file in the cloud with the configuration you want, you can start **docassemble** containers without specifying a lot of [configuration options](https://docassemble.org/docs/docker.html#configuration%20options)
; you simply have to refer to your cloud storage bucket/container, and **docassemble** will take it from there. For example, to run a central server, you can do:

    docker run \
    --env CONTAINERROLE=sql:redis:rabbitmq:log:cron:mail \
    --env S3BUCKET=docassemble-example-com \
    --env S3ACCESSKEY=FWIEJFIJIDGISEJFWOEF \
    --env S3SECRETACCESSKEY=RGERG34eeeg3agwetTR0+wewWAWEFererNRERERG \
    -d -p 80:8080 -p 25:25 -p 465:465 -p 5432:5432 -p 514:514 \
    -p 6379:6379 -p 4369:4369 -p 5671:5671 \
    -p 5672:5672 -p 25672:25672 -p 9001:9001 \
    --restart always --stop-timeout 600 jhpyle/docassemble

To run an application server, you can do:

    docker run \
    --env CONTAINERROLE=web:celery \
    --env S3BUCKET=docassemble-example-com \
    --env S3ACCESSKEY=FWIEJFIJIDGISEJFWOEF \
    --env S3SECRETACCESSKEY=RGERG34eeeg3agwetTR0+wewWAWEFererNRERERG \
    -d -p 80:80 -p 443:443 -p 9001:9001 \
    --restart always --stop-timeout 600 jhpyle/docassemble

Encrypting communications[¶](https://docassemble.org/docs/docker.html#Encryption)

==================================================================================

Using HTTPS[¶](https://docassemble.org/docs/docker.html#https)

---------------------------------------------------------------

If you are running **docassemble** on [EC2](https://aws.amazon.com/ec2/)
, the easiest way to enable HTTPS support is to set up an [Application Load Balancer](https://aws.amazon.com/elasticloadbalancing/applicationloadbalancer/)
 that accepts connections in HTTPS format and forwards them to the web servers in HTTP format. In this configuration [Amazon](https://amazon.com/)
 takes care of creating and hosting the necessary SSL certificates.

If you are not using a [load balancer](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Load_balancing_(computing))
, you can use HTTPS either by setting up [Let’s Encrypt](https://letsencrypt.org/)
 or by providing your own certificates.

### With Let’s Encrypt[¶](https://docassemble.org/docs/docker.html#letsencrypt)

If you are running **docassemble** in a [single-server arrangement](https://docassemble.org/docs/docker.html#single%20server%20arrangement)
, or in a [multi-server arrangement](https://docassemble.org/docs/docker.html#multi%20server%20arrangement)
 with only one web server, you can use [Let’s Encrypt](https://letsencrypt.org/)
 to enable [HTTPS](https://docassemble.org/docs/docker.html#https)
. If you have more than one web server, you can enable encryption [without Let’s Encrypt](https://docassemble.org/docs/docker.html#own%20certificates)
 by installing your own certificates.

To use [Let’s Encrypt](https://letsencrypt.org/)
, set the following environment variables in your task definition or `env.list` file:

*   `USELETSENCRYPT`: set this to `true`.
*   `LETSENCRYPTEMAIL`: [Let’s Encrypt](https://letsencrypt.org/)
     requires an e-mail address, which it will use to get in touch with you about renewing the SSL certificates.
*   `DAHOSTNAME`: set this to the hostname that users will use to get to the web application. [Let’s Encrypt](https://letsencrypt.org/)
     needs this in order to verify that you have access to the host.
*   `USEHTTPS`: set this to `true`.

For example, your `env.list` may look like:

    CONTAINERROLE=all
    USEHTTPS=true
    DAHOSTNAME=docassemble.example.com
    USELETSENCRYPT=true
    LETSENCRYPTEMAIL=admin@example.com
    TIMEZONE=America/New_York

The first time the server is started, the `letsencrypt` utility will be run, which will change the [NGINX](https://www.nginx.com/)
 configuration in order to use the appropriate SSL certificates. When the server is later restarted, the `letsencrypt renew` command will be run, which will refresh the certificates if they are within 30 days of expiring.

In addition, a [script](https://github.com/jhpyle/docassemble/blob/master/Docker/cron/docassemble-cron-weekly.sh)
 will run on a daily basis to attempt to renew the certificates.

If you are using a [multi-server arrangement](https://docassemble.org/docs/docker.html#multi%20server%20arrangement)
 with a single web server, you need to run the `cron` role on the same server that runs the `web` role. If you use the [e-mail receiving](https://docassemble.org/docs/background.html#email)
 feature with [TLS encryption](https://docassemble.org/docs/docker.html#tls)
, the `mail` role also has to share the server with the `web` and `cron` roles.

### Using your own certificates[¶](https://docassemble.org/docs/docker.html#own%20certificates)

If you do not want to use Let’s Encrypt to support HTTPS, or you have other SSL certificates that your server needs to use, you can pass your certificates to **docassemble**.

Using your own SSL certificates with [Docker](https://www.docker.com/)
 requires that your SSL certificates reside within each container. There are several ways to get your certificates into the container:

*   Use [S3](https://docassemble.org/docs/docker.html#persistent%20s3)
     or [Azure blob storage](https://docassemble.org/docs/docker.html#persistent%20azure)
     and upload the certificates to `certs/` in your bucket/container.
*   [Build your own private image](https://docassemble.org/docs/docker.html#build)
     in which your SSL certificates are placed in `Docker/ssl`. During the build process, these files will be copied into `/usr/share/docassemble/certs`.
*   Use [persistent volumes](https://docassemble.org/docs/docker.html#persistent)
     and copy the SSL certificate files into the volume for `/usr/share/docassemble/certs` before starting the container.

The default [NGINX](https://www.nginx.com/)
 configuration file expects SSL certificates to be located in the following files:

    ssl_certificate /etc/ssl/docassemble/nginx.crt;
    ssl_certificate_key /etc/ssl/docassemble/nginx.key;

The meaning of these files is as follows:

*   `nginx.crt`: this file is generated by your certificate authority when you submit a certificate signing request.
*   `nginx.key`: this file is generated at the time you create your certificate signing request.

Other certificate files that **docassemble** uses include:

*   `exim.crt` - certificate for the Exim mail daemon
*   `exim.key` - certificate key for Exim mail daemon
*   `redis.crt` - certificate for an external Redis server
*   `redis.key` - certificate key for an external Redis server
*   `redis_ca.crt` - certificate authority certificate for an external Redis server

In addition, if your [`db`](https://docassemble.org/docs/config.html#db)
 configuration refers to an `ssl cert`, `ssl key`, or `ssl root cert`, these need to be the names of files that are present in certificate storage.

In order to make sure that these certificates are replicated on every web server, the [supervisor](http://supervisord.org/)
 will run the `docassemble.webapp.install_certs` module before starting the web server.

If you are using [S3](https://docassemble.org/docs/docker.html#persistent%20s3)
 or [Azure blob storage](https://docassemble.org/docs/docker.html#persistent%20azure)
, this module will copy the files from the `certs/` prefix in your bucket/container to `/etc/ssl/docassemble`. You can use the [S3 Console](https://console.aws.amazon.com/s3/home)
 or the [Azure Portal](https://portal.azure.com/)
 to create a folder called `certs` and upload your certificate files into that folder.

If you are not using [S3](https://docassemble.org/docs/docker.html#persistent%20s3)
 or [Azure blob storage](https://docassemble.org/docs/docker.html#persistent%20azure)
, the `docassemble.webapp.install_certs` module will copy the files from `/usr/share/docassemble/certs` to `/etc/ssl/docassemble`.

There are two ways that you can put your own certificate files into the `/usr/share/docassemble/certs` directory. The first way is to [create your own Docker image](https://docassemble.org/docs/docker.html#build)
 of **docassemble** and put your certificates into the `Docker/ssl` directory. The contents of this directory are copied into `/usr/share/docassemble/certs` during the build process.

The second way is to use [persistent volumes](https://docassemble.org/docs/docker.html#persistent)
. If you have a persistent volume for the directory `/usr/share/docassemble/certs`, you can copy the SSL certificate files into that directory before starting the container.

If you are starting a new server using a [persistent volume](https://docassemble.org/docs/docker.html#persistent)
, you can set up your own certificates as follows.

Create an `env.list` file like the following:

    DAHOSTNAME=myserver.example.com
    TIMEZONE=America/New_York
    USEHTTPS=true

(Of course, you may also need additional environment variables, such as `EC2`, depending on your setup.)

Create a docker volume called `dacerts` using a temporary container based on the minimal [BusyBox](https://hub.docker.com/_/busybox)
 image.

    docker run -v dacerts:/data --name deleteme busybox true

Now copy your SSL certificate files to the volume.

    docker cp nginx.crt deleteme:/data/
    docker cp nginx.key deleteme:/data/

You may want to copy other certificates as well, for example for PostgreSQL or Redis.

Now delete the [BusyBox](https://hub.docker.com/_/busybox)
 container. (Your volume will not be deleted.)

    docker rm deleteme

Now start your **docassemble** container using the `dacerts` volume mounted to `/usr/share/docassemble/certs`:

    docker run \
      --restart always \
      --stop-timeout 600 \
      --env-file=env.list \
      -v dabackup:/usr/share/docassemble/backup \
      -v dacerts:/usr/share/docassemble/certs \
      -d -p 80:80 -p 443:443 jhpyle/docassemble

When it comes time to update your NGINX certificate files, save the new certificates as `nginx.crt` and `nginx.key`, and then do:

    docker cp nginx.crt a3970318cb38:/usr/share/docassemble/certs/
    docker cp nginx.key a3970318cb38:/usr/share/docassemble/certs/

replacing `a3970318cb38` with whatever the ID or name of your container is.

Then restart your container:

    docker stop -t 600 a3970318cb38
    docker start a3970318cb38

This last step is important; the location `/usr/share/docassemble/certs/` is not a working directory, but a staging area. If the server is running, changing the files in that directory will not change the certificates that **docassemble** uses. You need to stop and start the container for `docassemble.webapp.install_certs` to copy the files to the correct working directories and for the services to restart using the new certificates.

If you want to use different filesystem or cloud locations, the `docassemble.webapp.install_certs` module can be configured to use different locations. See the [configuration](https://docassemble.org/docs/config.html)
